Tab for the Guest
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions)
These notes help us draft focused questions and an event description.
General questions
How would you like us to introduce you?
What are you currently focused on—projects or themes you’d enjoy discussing?
Which topics are you most keen to cover?
Are there any topics you’d rather skip or avoid discussing publicly?
Social media links:
LinkedIn: TODO
Twitter: TODO
Github: TODO
Website: TODO
More detailed questions
Below are two optional templates you can use when submitting the details for your episode to us.
They’re only guides; feel free to fill in as much or as little as you like, add extra notes, or blend sections from both tables.
If you do include numbers or business outcomes, great; if not, that’s fine too. We’ll craft the final description from whatever you provide.
How to use these templates
Choose one template or mix rows from both.
Write in plain text. No formatting needed unless you’d like to spend time on this.
More details are welcome. Share anecdotes, links, or context you think will help.
Hopefully, this process will also help you to prepare for the podcast.
Template 1: Practical Focus (results, tips, numbers)
What vivid fact, problem, or provocative question will hook listeners?
What quick stat or story first made you feel this topic matters?
If someone had 10 seconds, which pain point would make them keep listening?
What key insight or skill will listeners gain?
Who needs this advice most right now?
Who wastes time or money if they ignore this?
Is there a beginner group that keeps asking you about this?
What 3-5 milestones or ideas should we cover, in order?
What’s your role, and what single metric or result links you to this topic?
How does your current job shape your viewpoint here?
If listeners have 15 minutes, what tiny first step should they take?
What small action, resource, or reflection would you recommend next?
Template 2 — Personal Story Focus (narrative-forward)
Give a one-sentence snapshot of the moment that set this journey in motion.
Where does this story begin for you?
Who or what first pulled you toward the topic?
Which events, successes, or stumbles taught you the most?
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?
Who might recognize their own journey in yours?
Whose path resembles your early struggles?
What 3-5 main stops, projects, or challenges mark the path—listed in order?
Was there a detour that almost changed your direction?
What’s your current role, and what single experience ties you to this theme?
What fact links present-you to this topic?
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
We’ll handle the editing and formatting from there. Thanks for contributing to the community!

topic: Redefining AI Infrastructure: Open-Source, Chips, and the Future Beyond Kubernetes
title: Redefining AI Infrastructure: Open-Source, Chips, and the Future Beyond Kubernetes
subtitle: The future of AI infrastructure - Andrey Cheptsov
bio: Andrey Cheptsov is the founder and CEO of dstack, an open-source alternative to Kubernetes and Slurm, built to simplify the orchestration of AI infrastructure. Before dstack, Andrey worked at JetBrains for over a decade helping different teams make the best developer tools. Links:
questions: How did you begin working on AI infrastructure?
What specific challenges do AI engineers face with current infrastructure solutions?
How can infrastructure limit or hinder the deployment of AI models?
What trends are you most excited about?
What’s next for dstack?
What is dstack?
What does dstack offer?
How does dstack compare to other tools, such as kubernetes or slurm?
Can you explain some of the concepts that a user will come in contact with when using dstack: what is, for example, a fleet?
What dev environments are available?
How is dstack more resource (costs, GPU time) efficient than other solutions (is it)?
Is dstack a paid service?
What are the most significant challenges you see in the evolution of AI infrastructure in the near future?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

unsectioned: Prepared questions from me about the topic (30-40 minutes)
Questions from the audience (the remaining time)
Title: Redefining AI Infrastructure: Open-Source, Chips, and the Future Beyond Kubernetes
Subtitle: The future of AI infrastructure - Andrey Cheptsov
bio: Andrey Cheptsov is the founder and CEO of dstack, an open-source alternative to Kubernetes and Slurm, built to simplify the orchestration of AI infrastructure. Before dstack, Andrey worked at JetBrains for over a decade helping different teams make the best developer tools.
links: LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/andrey-cheptsov/
Twitter: https://twitter.com/andrey_cheptsov/
Github: https://github.com/dstackai/dstack/
Website: https://dstack.ai/
This week we’ll talk about AI Infrastructure and everything around that.
We have a special guest today - Andrey Cheptsov.

Andrey Cheptsov is the founder and CEO of dstack, an open-source alternative to Kubernetes and Slurm, built to simplify the orchestration of AI infrastructure. Before dstack, Andrey worked at JetBrains for over a decade helping different teams make the best developer tools.
Welcome, Andrey!
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!
Before we go into our main topic of trends in AI infrastructure, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
How did you begin working on AI infrastructure? What was the transition like from IDEs to infrastructure orchestration?
Add your questions here: TODO
1. OpenAI (and alike) vs OSS AI
2. Large-scale training challenges
3. Large models vs small models
4. NVIDIA, AMD, and other AI chips
5. Inference optimization challenges
6. Why an alternative to Kubernetes and Slurm
7. Why containers
On challenges of infrastructure on AI engineering.
What specific challenges do AI engineers face with current infrastructure solutions?
How can infrastructure limit or hinder the deployment of AI models?
What trends are you most excited about?
What’s next for dstack?
On dstack
What is dstack?
What does dstack offer? What are advantages of dstack for the development and deployment of AI models over current infrastructure?
How does dstack compare to other tools, such as kubernetes or slurm?
Let’s say I want to deploy a LLM model with dstack on aws- what do I do?
Let’s say I want to deploy a LLM model (for example LLama) with dstack on a university cluster or local cluster - what do I do? Do I need to talk to the IT department/OPs team at all?
You call dstack “code as infrastructure” - but infrastructure is also very hardware dependent. How does dstack work under the hood?
Dstack supports various hardware like NVIDIA, AMD, and TPU. Does the increasing diversity in hardware solutions pose a challenge for services like dstack?
Can you explain some of the concepts that a user will come in contact with when using dstack: what is, for example, a fleet? And what a “task” and a “service”, related to dstack?
What dev environments are available? Can I run dstack out of vscode?
How is dstack more resource (costs, GPU time) efficient than other solutions (is it)?
Is dstack a paid service?
What are the most significant challenges you see in the evolution of AI infrastructure in the near future?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):

topic: Human-Centered AI for Disordered Speech Recognition
title: Human-Centered AI for Disordered Speech Recognition
subtitle: Automatic speech recognition - Katarzyna Foremniak
bio: Katarzyna is a computational linguist with over 10 years of experience in NLP and speech recognition. She has developed language models for automotive brands like Audi and Porsche and specializes in phonetics, morpho-syntax, and sentiment analysis. Kasia also teaches at the University of Warsaw and is passionate about human-centered AI and multilingual NLP. Links:
questions: What’s “specializes in phonetics, morpho-syntax, and sentiment analysis”?
What is a speech disorder?
Are there any speech disorders that are universal for all languages or are they always language specific?
How do speech disorders interfere in ASR (automatic speech recognition)?
What can we do to help ASR systems to deal with such data?
How to collect such data?
Can you explain the difference between Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) for typical speech and ASR for disordered speech?
What unique challenges arise when adapting ASR for individuals with speech disorders?
What role does contrastive linguistics play in your research on ASR for disordered speech?
How does comparing Polish and Italian languages help improve ASR systems?
How do factors like dialects and regional accents influence ASR performance?
Are these factors more or less challenging than speech disorders in speech recognition?
What are the current limitations of ASR systems when dealing with disordered speech?
How close are we to overcoming these limitations?
What potential trends are you excited about?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

This week we’ll talk about X.
We have a special guest today - Katarzyna Foremniak.

Kasia is a computational linguist with over 10 years of experience in NLP and speech recognition. She has developed language models for automotive brands like Audi and Porsche and specializes in phonetics, morpho-syntax, and sentiment analysis. Kasia also teaches at the University of Warsaw and is passionate about human-centered AI and multilingual NLP.
Welcome, Kasia!
Before we go into our main topic of Human-Centered AI for Disordered Speech Recognition , let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
What’s “specializes in phonetics, morpho-syntax, and sentiment analysis”?
What is a speech disorder?
Are there any speech disorders that are universal for all languages or are they always language specific?
How do speech disorders interfere in ASR (automatic speech recognition)?
What can we do to help ASR systems to deal with such data?
How to collect such data? How to clean and organise them, what problems can we encounter?
Can you explain the difference between Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) for typical speech and ASR for disordered speech?
What unique challenges arise when adapting ASR for individuals with speech disorders?
What role does contrastive linguistics play in your research on ASR for disordered speech?
How does comparing Polish and Italian languages help improve ASR systems?
How do factors like dialects and regional accents influence ASR performance?
Are these factors more or less challenging than speech disorders in speech recognition?
What are the current limitations of ASR systems when dealing with disordered speech?
How close are we to overcoming these limitations?
What potential trends are you excited about? What problems do you anticipate to be solved in 10 years?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):
Biogram proposal (please elaborate on it if needed, feel free to cut, correct and adjust to your website’s needs :-)
Katarzyna Foremniak
Katarzyna Foremniak is a computational linguist with a Ph.D. in Contrastive Linguistics and over ten years of academic and industry experience across three main areas:
· NLP and speech recognition sector for automotive industry, educational institutions and (choosing human-centred AI approach to prioritise human needs and values)
· academic (corpus linguistics; speech analysis; morphosyntactic analysis)
· translation and localization industry (Italian, Polish, English), specialised in arts and cultural sector.

Katarzyna is also the author of the Italian grammar course for Polish speakers, Włoski w tłumaczeniach (Preston Publishing, 2015–2019), which was adapted for the German market as Satz für Satz Italienisch (PONS, 2019).
Currently she works as a researcher and academic teacher at the Faculty of Modern Languages at the University of Warsaw and collaborates with several data companies on NLP projects.
Possible topics for our podcast:
1. Human-centred AI and diversity-focused data handling
- speech recognition for individuals with speech disorders and imperfections

I’m currently working on a project about automatic speech recognition of disordered speech. I’m analysing data for Polish and Italian, preparing a contrastive analysis. The project started in the automotive industry context, as a part of my job for Audi and Porsche, but has developed to a more universal one :-)
We can start with talking about human-centred AI approaches and then pass to a specific example discussing the ASR for disordered speech.
Possible questions:
- What is a speech disorder?
- Are there any speech disorders that are universal for all languages or are they always language specific?
- How do speech disorders interfere in ASR (automatic speech recognition)?
- What can we do to help ASR systems to deal with such data?
- How to collect such data? How to clean and organise them, what problems can we encounter?

source_path: podcast_examples/Podcast/Archive/2024-11-20 - Tamara Atanasoska - Linguistics and Fairness.docx
topic: A sociotechnical AI perspective
title: A sociotechnical AI perspective
subtitle: Building fair AI systems -Tamara Atanasoska
bio: Tamara works on ML explainability, interpretability and fairness as Open Source Software Engineer at :probabl.. She is a maintainer of fairlearn, contributor to scikit-learn and skops. Tamara has both computer science/ software engineering and a computational linguistics(NLP) background. Links:
questions: What inspired you to study both language and AI?
Why is understanding language so important when designing AI systems?
How do computational linguistics and engineering differ in their approach to language technology?
What inspired you to get involved with the Fairlearn project?
What are the main objectives of this project?
What is fairlean's approach to assessing fairness?
Can you share a specific project or case where Fairlearn was used effectively?
How has your background influenced your approach to fairness in AI?
What else do you do at probabl?
What gains and challenges will the field of AI fairness bring in the next years?
Do you have any advice for someone who considers going back to uni for a degree?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

links: LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/tamaraatanasoska/
Twitter: /
Github: https://github.com/TamaraAtanasoska
Website: /
Substack: https://holophrase.substack.com/
This week we’ll talk about A sociotechnical AI perspective. (or “a sociotechnical perspective on AI”)
We have a special guest today - Tamara Atanasoska.

Tamara works on ML explainability, interpretability and fairness as Open Source Software Engineer at :probabl.. She is a maintainer of fairlearn, contributor to scikit-learn and skops. Tamara has both computer science/ software engineering and a computational linguistics (NLP) background.
Welcome, Tamara!
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!

Before we go into our main topic of A sociotechnical AI perspective/a sociotechnical perspective on AI , let’s start with your background . Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
What inspired you to study both language and AI?
Why is understanding language so important when designing AI systems?
How do computational linguistics and engineering differ in their approach to language technology?
What would be a sociotechnical approach to modeling language
What inspired you to get involved with the Fairlearn project?
What are the main objectives of this project?
What is fairlean's approach to assessing fairness?
If someone is really interested now, how can they contribute to Fairlearn? What kind of contributions can developers or researchers make to Fairlearn
Can you share a specific project or case where Fairlearn was used effectively?
How has your background influenced your approach to fairness in AI? This can be any background ;)
What else do you do at probabl?
What gains and challenges will the field of AI fairness bring in the next years?
Changing subject a bit: You have also gone back to Uni for a masters at the age of 31. How did you decide to do that? Can you tell us about that experience?
Do you have any advice for someone who considers going back to uni for a degree?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):
I just took down my website in order to build a new one, so this is a bit unfortunate :D. I will try to gather what is still public below:

- I have a rather new Substack publication (https://holophrase.substack.com/) where I post every week(or two) a new post about the intersection of language, technology and society. Over the next week the plan is to publish one interview with a language learning app builder, and conversations about existing in a language(the language experience) and language ideologies. I had a lightning talk (https://holophrase.substack.com/p/a-short-deliberation-on-generative)this year about this at PyDataParis.
- A rather older podcast on grounded language (before the multimodal boom). (https://youtu.be/rD21l4GJDhg?si=n5MljKqjF8Fa4u9Z)
- A video of a PyLadies talk (https://www.youtube.com/live/jvQJHIXPTOc?si=YIvHrY_0HInNDIDN) about some of my NLP research, also contains my late-in-life Uni return story.
- My GitHub profile (https://github.com/TamaraAtanasoska). The repositories and projects I currently work on or maintain are featured.

- Linkedin posts: one (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tamaraatanasoska_fairlearn-activity-7254443767728951297-2fCf?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop), two (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tamaraatanasoska_scikit-learndocwhatsnewv16rst-at-f38aa4923330b593c1e5319ab8aa043844582fc5-activity-7231297512207269890-LsyR?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop), three (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tamaraatanasoska_can-europe-compete-on-ai-for-some-the-answer-activity-7202370813927272449-tU1W?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop), four (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tamaraatanasoska_i-know-that-one-day-we-will-regret-anthropomorphising-activity-7140665785466654722-ecHY?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop), five (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tamaraatanasoska_herbert-h-clark-why-and-therefore-how-activity-7082676414470086656-cPIe?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop).
I find the narrowing of topics hard . I will try.

- I am always really happy to talk about language, the cognitive science of language and sociolinguistics and how it relates to the way we build language-based products, or language is a wider phenomena than just an explicit, immediate communication tool(or coded as such).

- Computational linguistics vs an engineering only view of language modeling(Bender's talk for clarification, starts 46min in (https://underline.io/events/466/sessions/18203/lecture/104931-test-of-time-awards-lifetime-achievement-awards-presidential-address)).

- I also am the maintainer of fairlearn (https://fairlearn.org/), a community-driven project to increase the fairness of AI systems, I'd love to promote the project and invite contributions and usage . Please ask me about that. I am not a researcher in the area and most of my contributions there are engineering first(you can go through my latest PRs and it will be obvious :D), I guess just keep that in mind.
- probabl (https://probabl.ai/), the company where I work.
- Performance-interpretability tradeoffs for models.
- Going back to Uni as a 31yo to study language and AI, and why I eventually decided not to pursue my academic dreams.

- Any aspect of OSS(I've been involved since around 2009-2010, starting with a hackerspace membership and contribution programs, a hint to those beginnings here (https://opensource.googleblog.com/2012/05/macedonia-google-summer-of-code-meetups.html) and here (https://foundation.gnome.org/2011/04/26/after-a-successful-first-round-gnome-project-announces-new-outreach-program-for-women-interns/) and here (https://igorstama.github.io/presentations/share2/dzslides/template.html)). I've been involved and advocated for OSS in every job I've had, even at the very closed source Ableton.

Let me know if this would do! I just did a braindump here, I hope it was useful. I can always retrieve tests about research projects directly that are now missing from the website if that helps further.

I guess it is worth noting that this is just a brainstorm of what we could talk about, you can definitely exclude certain topics as they all expand a lot in many directions. I am a language nerd so I am always happy to discuss anything language related, but I don't do much of that at work these days, so it is fine if you decide to ask more of the rest :). Whatever works best.

topic: Continuous Learning in Data Science
title: Continuous Learning in Data Science
subtitle: Career advice, learning, and featuring women in ML and AI - Isabella Bicalho
bio: Isabella is a Machine Learning Engineer and Data Scientist with three years of hands-on AI development experience. She draws upon her early computational research expertise to develop ML solutions. While contributing to open-source projects, she runs a newsletter dedicated to showcasing women's accomplishments in data science. Links:
questions: What advice would you give to people who have just finished their studies/degree and would like to start working in the field of ML/AI?
How do you stay up to date with new developments in AI and ML?
Do you have any advice for people who want to close gaps in their education around ML and AI?
What challenges do women in data face, in your opinion?
Do you have an example of a success story or something that stuck with you from running the substack?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

1 Career path and early career advice
bio: Isabella is a Machine Learning Engineer and Data Scientist with three years of hands-on AI development experience. She draws upon her early computational research expertise to develop ML solutions. Twitter: TODO

This week we’ll talk about X.
We have a special guest today - Isabella Bicalho.

Isabella is a Machine Learning Engineer and Data Scientist with three years of hands-on AI development experience. She draws upon her early computational research expertise to develop ML solutions. While contributing to open-source projects, she runs a newsletter dedicated to showcasing women's accomplishments in data science.
Welcome, Isabella!
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Add your questions here: TODO
On the career path and early career advice
Your background is in mathematical and computational biology, and then you did some interesting internships, including at INRIA. What did you work on there?
Your current role on Linkedin is listed as a Machine learning engineer, self employed. What are you working on? And does it still include biology or have you fully transitioned to ML?
You have also done a variety of freelance activities around ML in the past. What were your projects, and how did you secure them?
You have worked on a variety of projects at this point - which one did you like most? Can you explain what the problem was, and what your solution was?
What advice would you give to people who have just finished their studies/degree and would like to start working in the field of ML/AI?
Continuous learning
You have done the Oxford Machine learning summer school. What did you learn there, how did you like it and would you recommend it? Was it expensive?
How do you stay up to date with new developments in AI and ML?
Do you have any advice for people who want to close gaps in their education around ML and AI?
Women in data
You are running a substack featuring women in data. Can you tell us what the motivation behind this idea is?
What challenges do women in data face, in your opinion? Are they different from challenges men face?
Do you have an example of a success story or something that stuck with you from running the substack?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):
women in data, collaborations in open source, early career advice (I am still 3 years of experience at this point in time), continuous learning.

topic: Career choices, transitions and promotions in and out of tech
title: Career choices, transitions and promotions in and out of tech
subtitle: A passion driven approach - Agita Jaunzeme
bio: Agita has designed a career spanning DevOps/DataOps engineering, management, community building, education, and facilitation. She has worked on projects across corporate, startup, open source, and non-governmental sectors. Following her passion, she founded an NGO focusing on the inclusion of expats and locals in Porto. Embodying the values of innovation, automation, and continuous learning, Agita provides practical insights on promotions, career pivots, and aligning work with passion and purpose. Links:
questions: What are these traits?
How can psychology and personality traits be leveraged to aid a career choice or transition?
What tools do you use to find out what someone’s personality is and how to align their career with it?
What motivates your clients commonly to take a course on career alignment?
What if a career alignment course results in the conclusion that a client should quit their job?
Did it help you?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

unsectioned: Prepared questions from me about the topic (30-40 minutes)
Questions from the audience (the remaining time)
Title: Career choices, transitions and promotions in and out of tech
Subtitle: A passion driven approach - Agita Jaunzeme
bio: Agita has designed a career spanning DevOps/DataOps engineering, management, community building, education, and facilitation. She has worked on projects across corporate, startup, open source, and non-governmental sectors. Following her passion, she founded an NGO focusing on the inclusion of expats and locals in Porto. Embodying the values of innovation, automation, and continuous learning, Agita provides practical insights on promotions, career pivots, and aligning work with passion and purpose.
links: LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/agita/
Twitter: TODO
Github: TODO
Website: TODO
This week we’ll talk about career choices, transitions and promotions in and out of tech.
We have a special guest today - Agita Jaunzeme.

Agita has done a lot of things in her career, including DevOps, DataOps, data engineering, management, community building, education, and facilitation. She has worked in corporate environments, startups, open source, and in non-governmental sectors. She founded an NGO focusing on the inclusion of expats and locals in Porto.
Welcome, Agita!
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Agita’s non-traditional tech career

Your career has taken some rather non-linear turns - for example, you shared that you got a promotion after automating yourself out of my project (creating a pipeline of the work you were tasked to do and the rewards of it). Can you share more about that?
You also worked on an DataOps Open Source project, which was your dream after attending your first FOSDEM conference. Did that dream hold up to reality>
And you were community manager for various projects. What did that entail?
You then quit IT after 6 years. What was the reason behind this decision, if you don’t mind to share?
Overall it seems you seem to take a rather intuitive approach to your career and finding your path. How can one leverage the intuition/passion/energy flow in the context of career development?
Data/DevOps and psychology
You describe yourself as “intuitive, feeling driven and people person”. How did you end up studying computer science?
You also have published articles such as “What personality traits are best suited for Data Engineering?”
What are these traits?
How can psychology and personality traits be leveraged to aid a career choice or transition?
Career alignment
What tools do you use to find out what someone’s personality is and how to align their career with it?
What motivates your clients commonly to take a course on career alignment? What challenges do they face and how can they overcome them?
What if a career alignment course results in the conclusion that a client should quit their job? Has that happened?

Some conclusions from a career alignment course can be quite scary (I guess) for example the realization that one should quit their job. Do people follow up on those plans that they make during the course, especially when they are challenging?
Just random stuff from Agita’s incredible linkedin
The last post on your linkedin is about you throwing a “brain storming party” for yourself. What did you do and what was the outcome?
Did it help you?
You also did a pledge to help 100 people find their purpose. Can you tell us more about it?
Another post was about “agile principles in life”. Can you share the similarities that you listed in this post?
Add your questions here: TODO
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):
Here's the list of my previous work:
- Blog posts: LinkedIn Newsletter (https://www.linkedin.com/newsletters/6945418595787026432/)
- Talks: Conf42 (https://youtu.be/vfnTNV1KeF0?si=ZMSmQL4B3t1KBXRr) New Stars of Data (https://youtu.be/5aeyjjSO2ac?si=KqzbreqRWq2S5ZOs)

- Community Meetings: Versatile Data it Community Meetings (https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL7TEE8NoRdVTTi1kxuG5iRN1Yt5EXAhi2&si=gR3B2J6LlRCY43XK) (this tool is no longer supported by VMware and is like a legacy for my Community mgmt work)
- Maybe best to share some of my actual current work which I haven't really published anywhere, I'll see what I can do
I can talk about the topic of career choices/transitions/promotions within and out of tech.
Just to share some of my career experience:
1. In DevOps - getting promo after automating myself out of my project (creating a pipeline of the work I was tasked to do and the rewards of it).
2. Working on DataOps open source project (which was my dream since the first FOSDEM conference I attended and how it was compared to reality).

3. Now, since I no longer work in tech, and in general transitioned to community work + facilitation and mentoring, I can share my experience and knowledge on how to follow the intuition/passion/energy flow in the context of career development.

Just random stuff from Agita’s incredible linkedin
The last post on your linkedin is about you throwing a “brain storming party” for yourself. What did you do and what was the outcome?
Did it help you?
You also did a pledge to help 100 people find their purpose. Can you tell us more about it?
Another post was about “agile principles in life”. Can you share the similarities that you listed in this post?
Add your questions here: TODO
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):
Here's the list of my previous work:
- Blog posts: LinkedIn Newsletter (https://www.linkedin.com/newsletters/6945418595787026432/)
- Talks: Conf42 (https://youtu.be/vfnTNV1KeF0?si=ZMSmQL4B3t1KBXRr) New Stars of Data (https://youtu.be/5aeyjjSO2ac?si=KqzbreqRWq2S5ZOs)
- Community Meetings: Versatile Data it Community Meetings (https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL7TEE8NoRdVTTi1kxuG5iRN1Yt5EXAhi2&si=gR3B2J6LlRCY43XK) (this tool is no longer supported by VMware and is like a legacy for my Community mgmt work)
- Maybe best to share some of my actual current work which I haven't really published anywhere, I'll see what I can do
I can talk about the topic of career choices/transitions/promotions within and out of tech.
Just to share some of my career experience:
1. In DevOps - getting promo after automating myself out of my project (creating a pipeline of the work I was tasked to do and the rewards of it).
2. Working on DataOps open source project (which was my dream since the first FOSDEM conference I attended and how it was compared to reality).
3. Now, since I no longer work in tech, and in general transitioned to community work + facilitation and mentoring, I can share my experience and knowledge on how to follow the intuition/passion/energy flow in the context of career development.

This week we’ll talk about From LHC (Large Hardron Collider) to LLMs: a typical non-linear career in ML.
We have a special guest today - Anastasia.

Anastasia is a particle physicist who turned data scientist, with experience of running experiments at the Large Hadron Collider and later at Blue Yonder, where she scaled AI solutions for global supply chains. She also led NLP and search efforts at Kaufland e-commerce. Anastasia is also an ML/AI mentor, and she’s passionate about developing the next generation of data science talent in Germany.
Welcome, Anastasia !
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Add your questions here: TODO
A bit more on Anastasia’s background
You have a fantastic Linkedin profile intro!
Maybe we can talk about that a bit.
The first part is:

“My background is particle physics, where I was completely spoiled by access to large amounts of data and the freedom to try out every hot ML algorithm on it. The experiments I participated in were so-called large scale experiments (e.g Large Hadron Collider) and had from 500+ up to 2.5k other people working on them. So in addition to physics, I was exposed to the best software development practices that helped us to avoid a complete mess and destroy the Universe.”
What was your experience with software development at such a scale (aka so many contributors)? And can you share some of the best practices you learned?
Moving on, you write about your experience at a company:

“Afterwards, I spent some time in Blue Yonder, a software vendor company that creates B2B SaaS solutions for the Supply Chain industry. Here I learned a big deal about what it takes to go from "it works on my laptop" to "it smoothly runs for a global retail across 30 countries 24/7". As part of the customer-facing team I had the opportunity to learn about the culture and data-driven applications in many leading companies in retail and manufacturing (Heineken, IKEA, Coca-Cola, AVON, Galeria-Kaufhof, etc)”
You talk about how you learned about what it takes to go from "it works on my laptop" to "it smoothly runs for a global retail across 30 countries 24/7".
Can you share with us what you developed? What was the process of scaling your product to run 24/7 across 30 counties?
You say your next stop was in information retrieval in e-commerce. What was your work there?
Can you share how you made your transitions from field to field? Were they driven by job offers or did you suddenly decide that you wanted to work on BSB Saas Solutions for Supply Chains etc?
What made you transition from academia to the private sector? What challenges did you face? Do you have any advice for anyone pondering this transition?
On AI/ML mentoring
Now you are working more on AI/Ml mentoring. Can you share what you do there?
What are typical clients? What are the challenges or problems that clients bring to you?
How did decide that this was something that interested you? Are there any challenges you face?
What is good mentoring in your opinion?
On some of Anastasia’s blog posts:

You wrote a blog post about the fact that the data scientist role does not exit anymore (in Germany) (https://www.karavdina.com/post/data-scientist-role-doesn-t-exist-in-germany-any-more). Can you elaborate on this opinion?
The Nobel prize in physics was just awarded to a physicist (John Hopfield) and a cognitive/computational psychologist (Geoffrey Hinton) doing ML/AI research. What do you think about that?
Are you more happy that a physicist how does ML/AI got it?
Or more mad that computational psychologist who never did physics also got it?
You have also written a blog post on the best way to Data Science from zero level: online courses, Bootcamps or Mentor? - can you elaborate on that?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):

This week we’ll talk about X.
We have a special guest today, Alexander Guschin.

Alexander Guschin is a Machine Learning Engineer with 10+ years of experience, a Kaggle Grandmaster ranked 5th globally, and a teacher to 100K+ students. He leads DS and SE teams and contributes to open-source ML tools.
Welcome, Alexander!
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!
Before we go into talking about teaching and competitive ML, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Add your questions here: TODO
On kaggle
Kaggle was a career launcher for you. Can you tell us how you got into Kaggle?
What was the first competition you participated in?
How did you/do you prepare for a competition?
How do you decide how to feature engineer your data?
How do you decide what model to train/run?
What category are you gand master in, on kaggle?
Do you still compete on kaggle?
Do you think it is still possible to kickstart a DS/ML career off kaggle today (like it was in 2016)?
Do you have tips for anyone who wants to get started with kaggle?
You also created a course on Coursera on how to win ML competitions that was rather popular. Can you explain some of the strategies behind winning ML competitions (and on kaggle)?
On Alexander’s post kaggle career
Can you summarize your career after kaggle and how kaggle helped you?

You basically designed your own career path and selectively picked challenges that would teach you new skills. Can you give tips on how you did that? What advice do you have for people who might not have been taugh DS/ML skills in their degrees and want to pursue a career in these fields?
You also worked at iterative.ai, the company behind DVC. Can you tell us what you did there? And why you left?
How does DVC work under the hood?
On teaching DS and ML
You are now working for a university in Russia with a very practical and industry oriented focus. Can you tell us what you do there and the projects you work on there?
You even left the UK for that opportunity, on a global talent visa, is that right (quite an opportunity). Have you regretted it?
How does good industry oriented teaching look like, in your opinion?
What classes or courses do you teach?
Do you also teach outside of your university job?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):
Possible Topics:
About the topics. I think we can cover three parts of my career in the interview:

- First - talk about Kaggle. I'm Kaggle Competitions Grandmaster (5th place in the world in 2017 https://www.kaggle.com/aguschin). This was my ticket to DS and ML field. At that time, in Russia there were no practical ML-oriented courses or programs in university, which is still a big problem for some countries. Kaggle was a place I was able to learn from nothing and I believe it's still a good place for many people to start their career when there are no better alternatives at hand.

- Second - talk about my industrial DS/ML career that started after Kaggle. Since my career started back then many things about starting a career aren't very relevant now (may be interesting from a "historical" viewpoint, but it's up to you to decide if that is worth it), but what's interesting is how it advanced. I started as a Data Analyst (at Avito), then realized I don't have neither sufficient Python/Programming skills nor deep ML/statistical knowledge and it's hurting the projects I'm working on. I tried to fix missing ML/statistical knowledge in the next place of work (Yandex) where I was DS. Yandex was great but it's a big company so my scope of work was pretty narrow (fit ML model and make it useful to business, but don't care about production things). I saw that as a problem and decided to develop myself in that direction. To learn more about ML production and become an ML Engineer, I moved to a small startup. In that startup, we tried to build a niche closed-source product around autoML for Industrial enterprises. There I realized another thing: you can be a brilliant ML Engineer, know Python and how to deploy everything, but eventually it's really cool to work on products that help people do their stuff. So I started to work at iterative.ai (http://iterative.ai/) (you may know them for DVC dvc.org (http://dvc.org/)) to build an MLflow models alternative with alternative pros and cons. That was pretty exciting until the startup ran out of money, making me realize some hard things about working at startups. That was a pretty long explanation of my career path, but eventually I tried different hats and became what some call Fullstack ML Engineer (in contrast with some folks who are excellent in a more narrow scope, which is also pretty awesome). This has some pros and cons, but is an interesting profile to have as I find it.

- Third - talk about teaching ML and my current job. I started teaching 10 years ago, as soon as I started winning ML competitions. Back in 2017 we created a class about how to win ML competitions for Coursera, and around 100k people watched it. Then I was teaching offline in Moscow (both competitive ML and Production ML), then I recorded an online class about MLOps. But it was always a hobby for me - although I liked it a lot. Until last year (2024) I got an interesting offer from a new university in Moscow (https://centraluniversity.ru (https://centraluniversity.ru/)) whose goal is to give a good industrial education on a scale. For some years I wanted to try to do some good things in education, but it was scary to abandon an industrial ML career (who knows how hard it will be to get back to industry after few years of full-time in education) + I didn't heard about any really fast-growing initiative in education that was worth a switch in my case (I mean there should be big goals & fast pace & enough money involved & a good spot for me specifically, since there are really a lot of good ML folks out there who can make a good things in education, although I think not a lot will agree to pause their industrial career for that). When I got that offer, I had just received a UK Global Talent visa and was living in the UK for a couple of months already and going through some interviews with tech startups. It was a hard call, but I decided to try this opportunity with that university in Moscow and I hadn't ever regretted it. I do a lot of interesting stuff - launch classes on Production ML and ML System Design, develop LLM-based graders that can grade students' text assignments (such as an ML System Design Doc) and train Russian team International Olympiad in AI (last year we took the "absolute" 1st place and gold medals in this olympiad - https://ioai-official.org/results/. Our team name was "Letovo" and not "Russia" due to the reasons I'm sure you are aware of).

source_path: podcast_examples/Podcast/Archive/2025-01-22- Adrian Brudaru- Topic.docx
topic: Trends in Data Engineering
title: Trends in Data Engineering
subtitle: The past, present and future of data engineering - Adrian Brudaru
bio: Adrian Brudaru studied economics in Romania but soon got bored with how creative the industry was, and chose to go instead for the more factual side. I ended up in Berlin at the age of 25 and started a role as a business analyst. At the age of 30, I had enough of startups and decided to join a corporation, but quickly found out that it did not provide the challenge I wanted. As going back to startups was not a desirable option either, I decided to postpone my decision by taking freelance work. I have since never looked back, and now, 5 years later, I am co-founding a company in the data space so I can try new things. This company is also looking to release a bunch of open source tooling (the kind we use ourselves) to help democratise data engineering.
questions: How have you been?
What’s happening in the industry?
Which things do you think we will see more?
Which orchestrator to use in 2025?
How is AI impacting data engineering roles?
Where is the data integration space moving?
What’s the place of dlt in the ecosystem?
What is the future of dlt going to look like?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
How has data engineering been done in the past, and what is changing now?
What does “democratise data engineering” mean?
What is a “schema” with respect to data engineering?
Why did you move from freelancing to founding your own company?
What are the problems you had to solve when trying to build a data structuring pipeline?
Can you describe schema evolution, and different ways to impose schema, and how dlt does schema evolution?
What are the stakeholders involved in a data engineering pipeline, and how can talking to all of them all the time be managed better?
Is there a way to automatise these interactions?
How can dlt help to transform data?
What does the deployment of a pipeline, for example on Gihub, involve?

bio: Adrian Brudaru studied economics in Romania but soon got bored with how creative the industry was, and chose to go instead for the more factual side. I ended up in Berlin at the age of 25 and started a role as a business analyst. At the age of 30, I had enough of startups and decided to join a corporation, but quickly found out that it did not provide the challenge I wanted.

As going back to startups was not a desirable option either, I decided to postpone my decision by taking freelance work. I have since never looked back, and now, 5 years later, I am co-founding a company in the data space so I can try new things. This company is also looking to release a bunch of open source tooling (the kind we use ourselves) to help democratise data engineering.

We have a repeat guest today, Nemanja, who our listeners might remember form our ML in finance course?
How have you been?
Since you were last on the podcast, you have run a machine learning in finance zoomcamp. How did that go?
But today we want to talk about MLOps in Corporations vs, in Startups. Let’s say I am an MLOps engineer and consider applying to either a big company or a startup. What should I expect?
What are the differences in MLOps strategies in big corporations and startups?
Are there any benefits or drawbacks of working in MLOps for a startup or a big corporation?
What if the Startup grows bigger? Form your experience, does the MLOps scale well with, for example, 10x, 100x growth?
What can be done to build for growth early on?
How can a startup be managed through growth and change effectively?
Can big corporations also learn from MLOps strategies of startups?

Let’s say in a startup only one person is responsible for MLOps. Then, because the startup grows, there is a team. How can that be dealt with? How can one person bottlenecks be avoided (for example, only one person knows the entire code base?)
How can technical debt be avoided when the startup grows? Or that that whole MLOps cycle needs to be migrated/rewritten when the company grows?
You also mention “the HR dept in IT”. Can you elaborate on that?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

topic: Data Intensive AI
title: Data Intensive AI
subtitle: TODO - Bartosz Mikulski
bio: I’m an AI and data engineer. I specialize in moving AI projects from the good-enough-for-a-demo phase to production by building a testing infrastructure and fixing the issues detected by tests. On top of that, I teach programmers and non-programmers how to use AI. I contributed one chapter to the book 97 Things Every Data Engineer Should Know, and I was a speaker at several conferences, including Data Natives, Berlin Buzzwords, and Global AI Developer Days. Links:
questions: What are 97 things a data engineer should know?
What are reasons that AI fails to be adapted by employees in company settings?
How does (or can) content moderation for LLMs work (https://mikulskibartosz.name/ai-content-moderation)?
What do you think about Deepseek?
How can system accuracy of RAGs be enhanced (https://mikulskibartosz.name/advanced-rag-techniques-explained)?
Can you walk us through how semantic search and vector similarity work?
How can query expansion and hypothetical document embedding improve the AI’s answer?
How can one prevent LLM hallucinations from reaching the users in RAG systems (https://mikulskibartosz.name/preventing-llm-hallucinations)?
How can improvement in information retrieval quality be measured in RAGs (https://mikulskibartosz.name/measuring-information-retrieval-quality-in-rag-systems)?
How can we get a ground truth/a good benchmark?
What measures and libraries are there?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

bio: Eddy is a Staff Data Engineer at Kinaxis, building robust data platforms across Google Cloud, Azure, and AWS. With a decade of experience in data, he actively shares his expertise as a Mentor on ADPList and Teaching Assistant at Uplimit. Previously, he was a Senior Data Engineer at Home Depot, specializing in e-commerce and supply chain analytics. Currently pursuing a Master’s in Analytics at the Georgia Institute of Technology, Eddy is also passionate about open-source data projects and enjoys watching/exploring the analytics behind the Fantasy Premier League. Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))

How does a physical warehouse resemble a digital warehouse? How do they differ?
What made you interested in digital warehousing?
What are the biggest cost optimization challenges in data infrastructure, and how can engineers tackle them?

On: From physical to digital warehousing

You started out with engineering and process improvement in physical warehouses (Supply chain management). What is a typical engineering problem one would have to solve when managing a physical warehouse?

Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):Rough idea:

1. Share my realization that operations at a physical warehouse is very similar to a digital warehouse on cloud. The difference is that digital infrastructure is more malleable and there are more opportunities for engineers to cost-optimize and architect infrastructure that can scale with business needs.
2. Sharing what is FinOps, similarities with Process Optimization Methods and some recent trends.

Title: Build a Strong Career in Data
Subtitle: Navigating the AI Landscape – From Carnegie Mellon to Industry with Lavanya Gupta
1 Career Journey: From Interest to Impact
bio: TODO

Lavanya is a Carnegie Mellon University (CMU) alumni of the Language Technologies Institute (LTI). She works as a Sr. AI/ML Applied Associate at JPMorgan Chase in their specialized Machine Learning Center of Excellence (MLCOE) vertical. Her latest research on long-context evaluation of LLMs was published in EMNLP 2024.

In addition to having a strong industrial research background of 5+ years, she is also an enthusiastic technical speaker. She has delivered talks at events such as Women in Data Science (WiDS) 2021, PyData, Illuminate AI 2021, TensorFlow User Group (TFUG), and MindHack! Summit. She also serves as a reviewer at top-tier NLP conferences (NeurIPS 2024, ICLR 2025, NAACL 2025). Additionally, through her collaborations with various prestigious organizations, like Anita BOrg and Women in Coding and Data Science (WiCDS), she is committed to mentoring aspiring machine learning enthusiasts.

title: From Hackathons to Developer Advocacy: Scaling Impact Through Open Source and Storytelling
subtitle: TODO - Will Russell
bio: Will Russell is a Developer Advocate at Kestra, known for his videos on workflow orchestration. Previously, Will built open source education programs to help up and coming developers make their first contributions in open source. With a passion for developer education, Will creates technical video content and documentation that makes technologies more approachable for developers. Links:
questions: Can you tell us about the projects you built, like Willmojis (https://devpost.com/software/willmojis) and Horrible Audio (https://devpost.com/software/horrible-audio)?
How did you come up with the ideas, and what were the stories behind them?
Which one was your favorite, and which one was the most ridiculous?
What is Major League Hacking?
Why did you join, and what did you do there?
How did COVID influence you taking up this role?
What are typical challenges that students face when starting contributing to open source projects?
What are typical projects that fellows work on during their fellowship?
How is MLH funded, btw?
What made you shift from running programs to becoming a Developer Advocate?
What drew you to Kestra specifically?
How do you balance technical depth with approachability in educational content?
How has “video content creation and story telling” helped your career?
Why is good storytelling important?
What are some tips or things that you think everyone should know/learn when it comes to story telling?
What makes for a good technical video?
How do you approach explaining something that’s flexible and complex like Kestra?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

Add your questions here: TODO
On getting started with Hackathons (-> wacky projects (https://devpost.com/wrussell1999))
Can you tell us about the projects you built, like Willmojis (https://devpost.com/software/willmojis) and Horrible Audio (https://devpost.com/software/horrible-audio)?
How did you come up with the ideas, and what were the stories behind them?
Which one was your favorite, and which one was the most ridiculous?
Joining MLH (during COVID)
You then joined Major League Hacking, as one of the first members, right?
What is Major League Hacking?
Why did you join, and what did you do there?
How did COVID influence you taking up this role?
You helped MLH to scale up. What were the challenges? From a curriculum side, from a technical/infrastructure perspective?
The MLH Fellowship & Open Source for Students
Were you more involved with the hackathons or the fellowships? Or both?
What are typical challenges that students face when starting contributing to open source projects?
What are typical projects that fellows work on during their fellowship?
How is MLH funded, btw? Fellows get a stipend, is that correct?
Transition to Developer Advocacy
What made you shift from running programs to becoming a Developer Advocate?
What drew you to Kestra specifically? What problems is it solving that got you excited?
Developer Advocacy is a broad term—how do you define what you do?
How do you balance technical depth with approachability in educational content?
Teaching, story telling, communication
You’ve made videos for MLH, hackathons, now Kestra—how did you get into that side of things?
How has “video content creation and story telling” helped your career? Would you recommend learning those skills?
Why is good storytelling important?
What are some tips or things that you think everyone should know/learn when it comes to story telling?
What makes for a good technical video? Any lessons learned from past flops?
How do you approach explaining something that’s flexible and complex like Kestra?
Any tips for the zoomcamps? :)
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
Materials from the guest (for preparing questions):

A bit of background on me but the short version is that before Kestra, I used to build open source programs for students - basically a way for them to get experience contributing to large projects to help them start their careers. Because of this, I am a big fan of open source.
More detail:
Studied Computer Science at university and went to lots of hackathons building wacky projects (https://devpost.com/wrussell1999) in 24 hours.
When COVID-19 came around, was part of the driving force in turning hackathons into digital events.
Through that I got involved with building an open source development program for students as a lot of them lost internships and this was a good way to get experience building on big projects
Worked on that originally part time as a student but eventually transitioned full time and ran it for 4 years.
Worked with some interesting projects including Apache Airflow, Meta FAIR projects, Python pallets projects, Jupyter Lab projects and more.

After doing it and running various different programs in Web3, Production Engineering etc, I decided I wanted to move into a more technical role where I can use the knowledge and skills I gained working with so many open source projects.
Joined Kestra as a Developer Advocate - a product that has lots of flexibility and complexity and without some proper education will be impossible for people to learn.
Made lots of video testimonials for the MLH Fellowship as well as freelance videos for hackathons so naturally video production at Kestra made a lot of sense.
Useful resources for the interview:
Personal Website (https://wrussell.co.uk/)
Hackathon Projects (https://devpost.com/wrussell1999)
MLH Fellowship (https://fellowship.mlh.io/)
Graduation Video from 2021 (https://youtu.be/cz50Jg1xspk)
Getting Started in Open Source (https://youtu.be/fhqoPnSaPSg)
Talk at NEARCon about Open Source programs (https://youtu.be/nsnit6wKIUU)
Kestra YouTube (https://www.youtube.com/@kestra-io)

Title: From Simulation Algorithms to Production-Grade Data Systems
Subtitle: Leveraging Simulation Expertise for Rapid Prototyping, Automation, and Scalable Data Pipelines - Orell Garten

Join us for an in-depth conversation with Orell Garten, a former simulation engineer turned freelance data consultant. He’ll share how his background in high-precision electromagnetic simulations led to today’s rapid-prototyping, automation, and production-grade data pipelines.
Simulation Foundations: How simulations inform the way Orell builds reliable data pipelines.
Rapid MVPs: The one-month prototype rule and how to apply it in your work.
Production Checklist: The must-do steps (CI/CD, monitoring, schema versioning) and the common blind spots teams miss.
Workflow Automation: Orell’s quick rule for spotting repeatable tasks, measuring ROI, and a real example of saved engineering hours.
Orell’s disciplined, practical approach will give you clear, actionable steps to move from concept to production. Register now and bring your questions!

Orell Garten is a freelance IT consultant and software engineer based in Leipzig, Germany. He began his career building high-precision simulation pipelines at TU Dresden and Ruhr-Universität Bochum. After pausing his PhD when COVID hit, he joined a government-backed startup program to validate research prototypes in the market.
Today, Orell helps businesses worldwide by:
Custom software when off-the-shelf tools fall short
Data engineering: automated, reliable pipelines for analytics, dashboards, and ML
Workflow automation: eliminating error-prone tasks and cutting operational risk
Rapid prototyping: backend/data proofs-of-concept in four weeks
Scaling to production: from CI/CD and monitoring to full-scale, reliable systems

Bio: Orell graduated in 2018 with a degree in Electrical Engineering, where he focused on simulation algorithms. He then began a PhD, but when COVID arrived, he decided to leave academia and look for new challenges. After that, he joined a government-funded startup program to explore how to turn scientific research into real products. Although that project didn’t go as planned, it taught him what he loves most: applying rigorous simulation methods to solve practical problems.
Today, Orell is a freelance consultant in software and data engineering. He uses his deep experience in simulations to build custom data infrastructure and applications for industrial clients.
Welcome, Orell!
The questions for today’s interview were prepared by Johanna Bayer. Thanks Johanna for your help!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))

Studied electrical engineering until 2018, focus on simulation algorithms
Orell’s career began by building high-precision simulation algorithms. Those projects shaped how he thinks about data today.
You built electromagnetic-wave simulations under strict numerical limits. Now you design production-grade data pipelines for clients. How do those early limits influence your pipeline design?
Most teams work with simple tables like sales records. Simulation data adds dimensions such as time, space, and randomness. What is the key change they must make in their tools or thinking?
Started a PhD and quit when COVID came around
You began a PhD but paused it when COVID arrived, and later developed your ‘rapid MVP’ approach.
On the day you paused your PhD, what thoughts ran through your mind—and how did that experience push you toward rapid prototyping?
You now promise an MVP in one month. Did that rule start on your COVID decision day, or later? Can you tell us the story?
Many researchers worry about wasted effort. Which milestones or numbers helped you decide, ‘It’s time to switch to industry’?
Since leaving academia, do you ever miss research work, or does freelancing give you enough challenge?

Orell’s EXIST grant taught him how to validate real market needs before building products.

Which interview questions or frameworks gave you the clearest signal that a prototype solved a real customer problem?
Your first startup did not succeed. Looking back, what early technical or market warning signs told you to change direction?

As a solo consultant, you move proofs-of-concept into production.
When you take a PoC to production, what checklist items (CI/CD, monitoring, schema versioning, etc.) are non-negotiable, and which do clients often forget?
What is the most common mistake you see teams make when they try to scale a prototype?
On your LinkedIn you say you help clients ‘start small, target immediate value, then scale.’ Can you walk us through a project where you delivered a one-month MVP from a flood of sensor or log data?
Once your MVP proves its value, what three steps—automation, monitoring, scaling—do you always follow before calling it ‘production’?
Clients often forget one critical element when going live. Is it CI/CD, observability, or data versioning? What do they usually miss, and why does it matter?
Does Workflow automation
You replace repetitive tasks with automation and emphasise freeing teams from ‘boring work.’
You automate repetitive, error-prone work. What simple rule tells you a task is ripe for automation, and how do you measure its benefit afterward?
Can you share a concrete example where your automation saved dozens of engineering hours or cut real operational risk?
Consulting practice and podcast
Which three daily habits or tools do you swear by to keep your solo practice smooth and effective?
You call yourself a ‘simulation-savvy’ engineer, not a generalist. How do you explain that difference so prospects immediately see your edge?
As co-host of The Data Rant and an active blogger, how has public writing or podcasting sharpened your consulting work and helped you find the right clients?
For a data pro who wants to record a 20-minute podcast or write a blog post next week, what one practical tip can you share?
Service-spotlight
You offer five core services and work either independently or embedded with teams.

Your services include custom software, data pipelines, workflow automation, rapid prototypes, and scaling to production. Which do clients ask for first, and which do they actually need after you start?
When a company thinks they need a large data platform, you advise ‘start small.’ What exact deliverable do you promise in the first month?
After you finish a project, how do you ensure the client’s team can maintain and extend your work without heavy hand-holding?
Which single library or service saves you the most time today when building production systems?
Resources
For listeners new to simulation algorithms and data engineering, which books or online courses would you recommend?
For senior engineers, what cutting-edge papers, open-source tools, or communities do you follow right now?

Additional information from Lior (for preparing event description)

Hook: Despite 97% of organizations investing in data initiatives, only 24% of executives report having created a data-driven organization. As practitioners, we often feel this gap daily, building great pipelines and models that don't translate to business impact. What if ancient Zen philosophy could help us bridge that gap from the ground up?

Value: Listeners will learn how to transform their day-to-day data work into strategic business value using the Data Strategy Triangle framework, measure and communicate their impact using two specific KPIs that executives understand, and build organizational trust in their data products through mindful development practices, whether they're data engineers, scientists, or analysts.

Who Should Listen: Primary: Data engineers, data scientists, and analysts who want their technical work to drive real business decisions and career advancement. Secondary: Team leads and senior practitioners looking to influence organizational data strategy from their current roles, plus freelancers/consultants wanting to deliver more strategic value to clients.
Main Topic Breakdown:
The Data Ecosystem Vision board: Why is it important to start with the high-level overview to create a shared reality and understanding on the data impact drivers on the business
Wabi-Sabi for Data Products: Building trust through transparency rather than perfection—practical techniques for data engineers and scientists
The Strategy Triangle for Teams: How individual contributors can balance maintenance, innovation, and rollout in their daily work

The Data Team Stress Index: How to Prioritize, Optimize, and Avoid Burnout - In professional basketball, Phil Jackson's teams needed "40 W's Before 20 L's" to be championship contenders. Similarly, data teams need a balanced approach to achieve sustainable success.
Measuring team stress through the S = (X + Y)/(Z - U) formula
Managing transitions between maintenance, innovation, and roll-out
Making strategic decisions about tool sunsetting and replacement
Scaling data teams through different growth stages

Guest Context: Independent Data Strategy Consultant & Content Creator, and former hands-on practitioner who's helped teams transform from reactive firefighting to strategic impact creation. Developed practical frameworks like the Data Ecosystem Vision Board and Data Product Lifecycle Manager based on real implementation challenges. Writes the Cooking Data newsletter applying Zen principles to daily data work, with insights from practitioners in tech, finance, healthcare, and beyond. Listeners can run a 15-minute "Data Impact Audit" on their current projects using the shared framework, or visit cookingdata.substack.com for free templates, including the Data Product Lifecycle Manager tool, which helps track the ROI of data products from development to retirement.

event_description: Title: Mindful Data Strategy: From Pipelines to Business Impact
Subtitle: Using Zen Principles to Bridge Technical Excellence and Strategic Outcomes - Lior Barak

Despite 97% of organizations investing in data, only 24% of executives feel truly data-driven. Too often, data teams build impressive pipelines and dashboards that never connect back to business results.

In this podcast episode, Lior Barak, Data Zen Master and author of Data Is Like Hummus, shares how ancient Zen principles, paired with modern data strategy frameworks, can help teams close the gap between technical effort and business impact.
What we plan to cover:
The Data Strategy Triangle: How to balance maintenance, innovation, and rollout, and align your work with long-term organizational goals.
Executive KPIs that Matter: The two metrics every data team should track to tie technical output to revenue growth, cost reduction, or retention.
Wabi-Sabi for Data Products: How embracing imperfection and transparency builds trust faster than chasing unattainable “perfect” data.
The Data Team Stress Index: A tool to prioritize work, anticipate burnout, and make strategic decisions about tooling and staffing as your team grows.
About the speaker:

Lior Barak is an independent data strategy consultant and former hands-on practitioner. He’s helped organizations in tech, finance, and healthcare evolve from reactive firefighting to sustainable, high-impact data practices.
He’s also the creator of:
The Data Ecosystem Vision Board: a visual planning tool for aligning data efforts across teams
The Data Product Lifecycle Manager: a framework for tracking ROI across a data product’s entire lifespan
Lior also writes Cooking Data, a newsletter that pairs mindful data strategy with nourishing, practical advice. As he says: “Data is like hummus: it should be simple and nourishing.”
speech: This week, we're focusing on mindful data strategy and how data teams can shift from building pipelines to delivering real business outcomes. We'll look at how principles from Zen philosophy can help teams work more intentionally and effectively.

We have a special guest today, Lior Barak. Lior is a data strategy consultant, former practitioner, and author of Data Is Like a Plate of Hummus. He developed the Data Ecosystem Vision Board and the Data Product Lifecycle Manager - frameworks designed to help teams move from reactive tasks to more strategic, long-term thinking.
Welcome, Lior!
Before we begin, Lior, could you briefly describe your career journey and what led you to create these frameworks? (not too long please =))
Questions:
You describe yourself as a “Data Zen Master.” When did you realize that Zen principles could apply to data strategy?
Can you explain the Data Ecosystem Vision Board? What elements does it include, and how does it support better decision-making?
You use the term “Strategy Triangle” to describe maintenance, innovation, and rollout. What does this framework represent?
Should teams aim to balance those three areas? How can they recognize imbalance, and what’s a good first step to address it?
You’ve brought the Wabi-Sabi concept into data strategy — the idea of finding value in imperfection. How do you apply this in real data work?
Many stakeholders ask for perfect data. How do you explain that transparency and iteration often build more trust than perfectionism?
You've said there are two KPIs that matter most to executives. What are they, and why focus on just two?
What is the Data Team Stress Index? How is it used, and what value does it offer to growing teams?
You’ve mentioned that the industry often overcomplicates problems. Where do you see the most inefficiency, and what’s one way to simplify?
Given that data is never perfect, how do you decide when a model or dashboard is “good enough” to release?
You’ve said AI won’t replace data teams, but it will change how we work. What shifts should teams start making now?
For those listening today, what’s one small “data impact check” they can run in 15 minutes to assess their team’s focus?
To close, a few quick personal questions:
Your favorite hummus topping?
A Zen idea that influences how you lead?
One non-technical book you recommend for data leaders?

Subtitle: Using Zen Principles to Bridge Technical Excellence and Strategic Outcomes - Lior Barak
Despite 97% of organizations investing in data, only 24% of executives feel truly data-driven. Too often, data teams build impressive pipelines and dashboards that never connect back to business results.
In this podcast episode, Lior Barak, Data Zen Master and author of Data Is Like Hummus, shares how ancient Zen principles, paired with modern data strategy frameworks, can help teams close the gap between technical effort and business impact.
What we plan to cover:
The Data Strategy Triangle: How to balance maintenance, innovation, and rollout, and align your work with long-term organizational goals.
Executive KPIs that Matter: The two metrics every data team should track to tie technical output to revenue growth, cost reduction, or retention.
Wabi-Sabi for Data Products: How embracing imperfection and transparency builds trust faster than chasing unattainable “perfect” data.
The Data Team Stress Index: A tool to prioritize work, anticipate burnout, and make strategic decisions about tooling and staffing as your team grows.
About the speaker:
Lior Barak is an independent data strategy consultant and former hands-on practitioner. He’s helped organizations in tech, finance, and healthcare evolve from reactive firefighting to sustainable, high-impact data practices.
He’s also the creator of:
The Data Ecosystem Vision Board: a visual planning tool for aligning data efforts across teams
The Data Product Lifecycle Manager: a framework for tracking ROI across a data product’s entire lifespan
Lior also writes Cooking Data, a newsletter that pairs mindful data strategy with nourishing, practical advice. As he says: “Data is like hummus: it should be simple and nourishing.”
Links:
LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/liorbarak
Website: https://cookingdata.substack.com/
Product product lifecycle manager: https://app--data-product-lifecycle-manager-c81b10bb.base44.app
Speech
This week, we're focusing on mindful data strategy and how data teams can shift from building pipelines to delivering real business outcomes. We'll look at how principles from Zen philosophy can help teams work more intentionally and effectively.
We have a special guest today, Lior Barak. Lior is a data strategy consultant, former practitioner, and author of Data Is Like a Plate of Hummus. He developed the Data Ecosystem Vision Board and the Data Product Lifecycle Manager - frameworks designed to help teams move from reactive tasks to more strategic, long-term thinking.
Welcome, Lior!
Before we begin, Lior, could you briefly describe your career journey and what led you to create these frameworks? (not too long please =))
Questions:
You describe yourself as a “Data Zen Master.” When did you realize that Zen principles could apply to data strategy?
Can you explain the Data Ecosystem Vision Board? What elements does it include, and how does it support better decision-making?
You use the term “Strategy Triangle” to describe maintenance, innovation, and rollout. What does this framework represent?
Should teams aim to balance those three areas? How can they recognize imbalance, and what’s a good first step to address it?
You’ve brought the Wabi-Sabi concept into data strategy — the idea of finding value in imperfection. How do you apply this in real data work?
Many stakeholders ask for perfect data. How do you explain that transparency and iteration often build more trust than perfectionism?
You've said there are two KPIs that matter most to executives. What are they, and why focus on just two?
What is the Data Team Stress Index? How is it used, and what value does it offer to growing teams?
You’ve mentioned that the industry often overcomplicates problems. Where do you see the most inefficiency, and what’s one way to simplify?
Given that data is never perfect, how do you decide when a model or dashboard is “good enough” to release?
You’ve said AI won’t replace data teams, but it will change how we work. What shifts should teams start making now?
For those listening today, what’s one small “data impact check” they can run in 15 minutes to assess their team’s focus?
To close, a few quick personal questions:
Your favorite hummus topping?
A Zen idea that influences how you lead?
One non-technical book you recommend for data leaders?

Copy the sections that suit you, add any extra context, and send them back. We’ll handle the editing and formatting from there. Thanks for contributing to the community!
Final text based on Partor’s answers:

My path into AI began pragmatically: I needed income while studying medicine, and the side work I found ended up shaping my career. The real inflection point was ML Zoomcamp. By putting my work out in public during the course, I started getting interviews and offers without relying on freelancer platforms. That experience taught me two durable lessons: first, consistent connection with people compounds—many of my best opportunities trace back to conversations I started then; second, courses can be direct pipelines to jobs when you treat them as a place to build both skill and network.

A theme I see repeatedly—both in my story and in people I meet—is time-constrained learners trying to make room for ML. Many believe they need another degree to be considered; in practice, focused projects, visible output, and community engagement move the needle faster. My own milestones reflect that bias toward doing: I spent almost three years building a stock-market pipeline, which demanded serious engineering discipline and pushed me beyond toy examples. I shared what I learned on social media, which kept me accountable and surfaced collaborations. And I kept investing in learning; it’s been the highest-ROI decision of my career.

Today I work as an AI engineer. The work is less glamorous than it looks from the outside: most days are spent moving data, wiring APIs, and feeding LLMs—the plumbing that makes models useful. That continuity links my present to my start: solve real problems, show the work, iterate in public.

If there’s one takeaway, it’s this: treat learning as an intentional priority. Block time, ship small projects, and share them. If you have 15 minutes this week, pick a tiny task—run a single ML Zoomcamp exercise, post a short summary of what you learned, or wire one API call into a notebook. Small, finished steps compound.
