event_description: Title: From Medicine to Machine Learning: How Public Learning Turned into a Career
How do you learn machine learning while studying medicine and turn it into a full-time career?

In this episode, we’re joined by Pastor Soto, a Machine Learning Engineer who learned ML through focused online learning and consistent public work. While still in medical school, Pastor took on side projects that led him into machine learning, and the ML Zoomcamp course (https://datatalks.club/blog/machine-learning-zoomcamp.html) served as a turning point in his career.
By sharing his work online, Pastor opened the door to job interviews, including at Meta and DeepLearning.AI.
We plan to cover:
How Pastor learned ML while studying medicine
How ML Zoomcamp helped Pastor to get interviews at companies like Meta and DeepLearning AI
Lessons for time-constrained learners: small wins, public updates, and ROI
How to position a non-traditional background (like an MD) in AI interviews
About the speaker:

Pastor Soto is a Machine Learning Engineer and mentor with a practical bias toward shipping. While studying medicine at the university, he took on side work that pulled him into machine learning. The inflection point was ML Zoomcamp: by publishing exercises and projects publicly, he attracted interviews and job offers.

Pastor’s work centers on production ML: moving data, wiring APIs, and feeding LLMs so applications run. He mentors with DeepLearning.AI, leads sections for Stanford’s Code in Place, and writes about focused learning and small, finished steps that compound.

topic: From Algorithms to Agents: Lessons from Building Copilot
title: From Algorithms to Agents: Lessons from Building Copilot
subtitle: What Generative AI Has Changed and Which Fundamentals Still Matter - Vadim Smolyakov
bio: Vadim Smolyakov is a Machine Learning Engineer at Microsoft working on Copilot AI. He is a former PhD student at MIT with interests in Generative AI. Vadim is an author of “Machine Learning Algorithms in Depth” book which is focused on algorithmic machine learning. Current focus
questions: What’s a misconception about “intelligent assistants” you often encounter, even among ML professionals?
What does an “agent” mean to you in 2025 beyond just a single LLM call?
Where do current agent systems most reliably fail?
Which job tasks do agents replace, and which do they just accelerate?
Why is embodiment the next step rather than just improving context windows and tools?
What innate structures would you incorporate into a physical agent (such as motor primitives and active perception loops), and what must be learned solely from the environment?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

guest_tab: Vadim’s Content
bio: Vadim Smolyakov is a Machine Learning Engineer at Microsoft working on Copilot AI. He is a former PhD student at MIT with interests in Generative AI. Vadim is an author of “Machine Learning Algorithms in Depth” book which is focused on algorithmic machine learning.
Current focus
3-4 bullet points
Generative AI (agents, RL training)
Growth, self-development, sharing knowledge and what I learned with others
Impact of generative AI on society
Evolution and building intelligent machines

Vadim Smolyakov is a Machine Learning Engineer at Microsoft, working on Copilot AI, an author of Machine Learning Algorithms in Depth, and a former MIT PhD student. He focuses on generative AI (agents, RL), self-development, knowledge sharing, and the societal impact of intelligent systems.
links: 2-5 URLs
LinkedIn: Vadim Smolyakov | LinkedIn (https://www.linkedin.com/in/vsmolyakov/)
Twitter: Vadim Smolyakov (@vsmolyakov) / X (https://x.com/vsmolyakov)
Github: vsmolyakov (Vadim Smolyakov) · GitHub (https://github.com/vsmolyakov)
Website: Vadim Smolyakov (https://vsmolyakov.github.io/)
YouTube: Vadim Smolyakov - YouTube (https://www.youtube.com/@VadimSmolyakov)
Instagram: Instagram (https://www.instagram.com/lifeguidenow/)
Previous podcast: Machine Learning algorithms explained, with Vadim Smolyakov - HS#18 (https://www.hockeystick.show/p/simplifying-algorithms-vadim-smolyakov-777)
Topics you’re keen to cover
Short list
Generative AI (agents, fine-tuning LLMs, synthetic data generation)
How ML evolved over the years, e.g. the shift from algorithmic ML to generative AI
Physical AI (next step of evolution?), building intelligent machines
Vision / Purpose: helping all of life to survive (biological life and artificial life)
Copilots as assistants, not as a replacement for thinking, impact of AI agents on jobs
Philosophy of setting goals (inspiration for lifegiudenow channel) and sharing what worked for me with others
Leaving a legacy (something that lasts with time)
Anything you’d rather skip
Where I live
detailed_questions: Physical AI as the next step of evolution. AI embodiment in the physical world, what to build in (motor controls etc) and what to learn (from the environment), “a theory of a thousand brains book”: old brain (survival, genes) and new brain (neo cortex - reasoning, discovery), designing intelligent machines by letting go of old brain (genes, biology) and building in intelligence (discovery, exploration)

Vision for life. We are alive in the unvierse, we have agency, we move around in physical space, we need to preserve that, i.e. help all of life to survive (biological life and artificial / created by humans / intelligent machines life)

Evolution of Machine Learning (ML) over the past 10-20 years. I was always fascinated with algorithms, that’s what got me into ML. ML 10 years ago was way more mathematical, if you wanted to innovate and design a new algorithm for example, you needed to be really good in math and coding. I used to do a lot more from scratch implementations (hence my book focusing on algorithmic ML from scratch) but I find now it’s either foundation model training or fine-tuning and synthetic data generation for training. It’s much less from scratch.

Impact of copilot on society and jobs. I see copilot as an assistant, knowledge on demand, it helps me fix bugs and saves time doing research myself on-line. However, you have to be careful because it’s not a replacement for thinking. There’s a temptation to just ask chat-gpt a question you have in mind first and read the information (collective knowledge) without taking the time to brainstorm. There was a study saying that chat-gpt users use less of their brain power. That’s not good. Similarly, AI agents can automate tasks traditionally performed by humans. For example, I love tutoring and I’m trying to understand if tutoring will still exist in the chat-gpt era.

Leaving a legacy. Life is short and focusing your energy on helping improve people's lives and helping all of life to survive is a worthy pursuit. Leaving a legacy, something that lasts with time, is important to me. I’ve been thinking about two categories and I’m curious to hear about more: physical and digital artifacts. Physical (when you build something in the physical world) and digital (when you create or record something in the digital world)

Setting goals. Early on I got into self-help literature and it helped me gain confidence in highly competitive environments (in college) and it introduced me to goal setting. I really like that philosophy of goal setting, seeing yourself as an agent and a designer of your life. Defining a vision for what you want, defining the goals that will help you achieve that vision, asking effective yourself effective questions (i’m working on a new illustration book right now that describes that in more detail - like guide principles for life without tons of pages, brief, high signal, things that work). Stay tuned.

Resources for learning and building with LLMs. I recommend LLM Engineer’s Handbook and Building LLM from scratch books to learn the foundations. Also, OpenAI documentation is really helpful if you want to for example build your own agent from scratch (not using any of the frameworks) you can build a client in python that supports multi-turn conversations with tool calling and that would be a good exercise. Also, TRL library by HuggingFace is really helpful for both fine-tuning LLMs and learning by reading documentation. I recommend getting experience with LoRA parameter efficient fine-tuning as well as techniques for synthetic data generation (for fine-tuning). Evaluating quality of LLM generated output is another important area, e.g. getting experience with LLM-as-a-judge and writing judge prompts.

Algorithms for LLM alignment. Alignment means steering LLM response to match human preferences / requirements. Starting with SFT (supervised fine-tuning) where we need to provide sample outputs, to GRPO (group-relative policy optimization used to train DeepSeek-R1) where we sample multiple responses and fine-tune the model to mimic better responses, DPO (direct preference optimization) that takes in chosen and rejected pairs (good and bad examples), RLHF (reinforcement-learning from human feedback) collects ranking preferences from human annotators and uses PPO (proximal policy optimization) for fine-tuning the LLM. All of these algorithms are implemented in HuggingFace TRL library (https://huggingface.co/docs/trl/). Another great reference is:
RLHF Book by Nathan Lambert (https://rlhfbook.com/)
event_description: Opening Snapshot

I’ve always been fascinated with algorithms, at first computer science algorithms and later machine learning algorithms. The human brain is the most complex biological computer that differentiates us from other species and creating artificial brain / intelligence excites me.
Key Moments & Lessons

Do what you are passionate about. While working on my master’s in wireless architecture I discovered that the thing I enjoy doing the most are algorithms and I wanted to pursue that in greater depth, which led me to a PhD program in machine learning. Similarly in choosing which teams to work with in the industry, I prioritize interesting work and interesting people.
Another important theme is adaptation in work and personal life. AI is a fast changing field and you are missing out if you are not working on the latest technology.
Who Should Listen
Students deciding on their majors, graduate students deciding on the company to join, researchers deciding where to concentrate their efforts
Path Markers (3-5 bullets)

People I met in college and grad school inspired me to learn and contribute (e.g. self-learning / filling in the gaps in my free time in college, self-directed learning has been a major theme, sharing what I learn with other which led me to writing blogs and later my book)
Following my passion for ML led to me a research group that worked in the area I was interested in (I had to overcome many obstacles to find the right research advisor)
Desire to work on the latest in ML (e.g. Copilot AI) led me to my present team at Microsoft.
Context in One Line
I’m passionate about data science and machine learning and as a machine learning engineer I’m excited to contribute to this field.
Reflection Invitation
If you could build at the speed of thought, what would you create?
(if you had infinite potential, resources, time, if you could think anything into existence, what would it be?)
Title: From Algorithms to Agents: Lessons from Building Copilot
Subtitle: What Generative AI Has Changed and Which Fundamentals Still Matter - Vadim Smolyakov

Vadim’s path spans from algorithmic ML and MIT research to building Copilot at Microsoft, providing him with a front-row view of how practice has shifted from hand-built algorithms to agentic systems.
In this conversation, he reflects on what genuinely changed with generative AI and what fundamentals still matter.
We’ll discuss assistants as complements to human thinking, why embodiment (“physical AI”) may be the next step, and how goal-setting and sharing knowledge shape work that lasts.
We plan to cover:
What Copilot taught about scope, trade-offs, and evaluation
Agents as assistants: avoiding over-reliance while gaining real leverage
Fine-tuning, synthetic data, and how the craft of ML has evolved
Physical AI: what to build in vs. what to learn from the environment
Purpose, habits, and leaving durable digital/physical artifacts
rough_plan: Overall duration – up to 1 hour
Intro (5 minutes)
Prepared questions from me about the topic (30-40 minutes)
Questions from the audience (the remaining time)
speech: This week, we’ll discuss how practice has shifted from hand-built algorithms to agent-based systems.
We have a special guest today - Vadim Smolyakov.

Vadim is a Machine Learning Engineer at Microsoft working on Copilot AI. He is a former PhD student at MIT with interests in Generative AI. Vadim is the author of the book “Machine Learning Algorithms in Depth,” which is focused on algorithmic machine learning.
Welcome, Vadim!

Before we go into our main topic of how practice has shifted from hand-built algorithms to agentic systems, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Add your questions here:
You’ve worked across algorithmic ML, academia, and now Copilot AI. What perspective do you use today to decide what to build next?
Looking back over the past 10-15 years, which algorithmic skills still provide long-term benefits, and which are now low-ROI?
If you were updating Machine Learning Algorithms in Depth for 2025, which chapters would stay, which would be condensed, and what new chapter would you add first?
What’s a misconception about “intelligent assistants” you often encounter, even among ML professionals?
What does an “agent” mean to you in 2025 beyond just a single LLM call? What minimal technology stack qualifies: such as a planner, memory, tools, and feedback loop?
You see Copilot as “knowledge on demand,” not a replacement for thinking. Could you discuss this further?
Where do current agent systems most reliably fail? Tool selection, long-horizon credit assignment, grounding, or state management?
If you had to choose one reliability tactic for agents in production, which would provide the best results and why?
Let’s discuss agents' memory. Memory can be short-term (scratchpads), long-term (vector/RAG stores), or structured logs. How do you decide what to keep, what to forget, and when to reset the state?
Which job tasks do agents replace, and which do they just accelerate? Are there any surprising examples from Copilot-like work that you can share?
Why is embodiment the next step rather than just improving context windows and tools?
What innate structures would you incorporate into a physical agent (such as motor primitives and active perception loops), and what must be learned solely from the environment?
You promote explicit goal-setting. What’s your specific weekly cycle (review, plan, act, reflect), and can you share an example where it altered your trajectory?
For people navigating the rapidly changing AI landscape, how do you choose a niche that withstands waves: from algorithmic ML to generative models to agents?
You think in terms of “physical vs. digital artifacts.” What’s one example of each you’d like to leave behind, and what qualities make them resilient over decades?
One belief about intelligent machines that you hold, which most capable people in the field might challenge.
Where should interested listeners begin with your materials: book, GitHub, talks, or the channel you
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

What innate structures would you incorporate into a physical agent (such as motor primitives and active perception loops), and what must be learned solely from the environment?
You promote explicit goal-setting. What’s your specific weekly cycle (review, plan, act, reflect), and can you share an example where it altered your trajectory?
For people navigating the rapidly changing AI landscape, how do you choose a niche that withstands waves: from algorithmic ML to generative models to agents?
You think in terms of “physical vs. digital artifacts.” What’s one example of each you’d like to leave behind, and what qualities make them resilient over decades?
One belief about intelligent machines that you hold, which most capable people in the field might challenge.
Where should interested listeners begin with your materials: book, GitHub, talks, or the channel you
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

event_description: Title: Lessons from Two Decades of AI
Subtitle: TODO - Micheal Lanham

Micheal Lanham has been building intelligent systems since the early 2000s, starting with neural networks and evolutionary algorithms in games and moving through enterprise software, geoscience, AR/VR, and now AI agents. Over the years, he has written more than ten technical books and worked across industries as an architect, manager, and hands-on AI engineer.

In this conversation, Micheal shares hard-earned lessons from two decades at the intersection of data, software, and AI. We’ll explore what games can teach us about intelligence, why evolutionary methods are resurfacing, and how to think about AI agents beyond the hype.
We plan to cover:
What games can teach us about AI and data products
The promise (and limits) of evolutionary deep learning
AI agents in practice: beyond LLMs and prompts
How XR and intelligent systems are converging
What it takes to productionize AI across industries
What has really changed in AI over the last 20 years
bio: Micheal Lanham is a best-selling author, innovator, and AI engineer based in Calgary, Canada. His work spans games, graphics, GIS, enterprise software, and machine learning. He has published over ten technical books, including Evolutionary Deep Learning, Hands-On Reinforcement Learning for Games, and AI Agents in Action. Micheal has worked as a lead AI developer, architect, and manager across industries from oil and gas to fintech, and today focuses on building intelligent systems with deep reinforcement learning, evolutionary methods, and generative AI.

speech: Today on the podcast, we’re joined by Micheal Lanham.

Micheal’s career spans two decades, from building game AI with neural networks and evolutionary algorithms, to writing more than ten books on AR/VR, reinforcement learning, and AI agents. Today, he focuses on advanced deep learning, evolutionary methods, and building intelligent systems that go beyond LLMs.

In this conversation, we’ll talk about what makes AI agents different from simple chatbots, how games and simulations continue to shape AI research, and what has really changed in practice as the field moved from early neural nets to today’s generative AI.
Micheal, welcome to the podcast!
Micheal, you’ve worked in games, enterprise software, geoscience, and now AI agents. Can you walk us through your journey, and what connects all these fields?
Questions:
You started experimenting with neural networks and evolutionary algorithms in the early 2000s. How did that experience shape how you look at AI today?
You’ve built AI for games and also written books on game development. What can the AI industry learn from the way games use data and intelligence?
Do you think reinforcement learning and simulation-driven AI will become more important again in the era of LLMs?
In your book Evolutionary Deep Learning, you argue for combining genetic algorithms with neural networks. Where do you see the biggest promise of this approach?
Your latest book is AI Agents in Action. How do you define an “AI agent,” and how is it different from just using an LLM with prompts?
What kinds of real-world problems do you think agents are best suited for today — and which ones are still hype?
You’ve also written about AR, VR, and immersive environments. How do you see AI agents and XR coming together?
You’ve worked in very different industries, from oil and gas to fintech to software. What lessons about taking ML to production apply everywhere?
You’ve published more than 10 technical books. How does writing help you make sense of such a fast-moving field?
When teaching others, what fundamentals do you find people struggle with most — and which ones remain timeless despite all the hype?
You’ve seen the field shift from early neural nets to today’s GPT agents. What has really changed in practice, and what hasn’t?
Looking forward, where do you think data and AI teams should focus in the next 5 years?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

topic: Lessons from Applied AI: Tesla, Waymo, and Beyond
title: Lessons from Applied AI: Tesla, Waymo, and Beyond
bio: Aishwarya Jadhav is a Machine Learning Engineer at Waymo, where she leads projects on gesture understanding and pedestrian semantics. She previously worked at Tesla Autopilot AI on multimodal LLMs and video understanding, and at Morgan Stanley on large-scale recommendation systems. Aishwarya holds a Master’s degree in Computer Science from Carnegie Mellon University, where she focused on multimodal deep learning and text information extraction. She has also contributed to social impact projects, including malaria vector mapping in Africa, which helped reduce mosquito populations by 60% at minimal cost. Links:
questions: What connects these very different domains for you?
What skills carried across these domains, and what did you have to relearn from scratch?
What was the most challenging adjustment when moving from financial recommendation systems at Morgan Stanley to perception models at Tesla and Waymo?
When you evaluate models for pedestrian or gesture recognition, what metrics or validation methods matter most to decide if they are safe enough to deploy?
What’s an example of a trade-off you’ve had to make between model accuracy and real-time performance in multimodal or perception systems?

rough_plan: Overall duration – up to 1 hour
Intro (5 minutes)
Prepared questions from me about the topic (30-40 minutes)
Questions from the audience (the remaining time)
event_description: Title: Lessons from Applied AI: Tesla, Waymo, and Beyond

Aishwarya Jadhav has worked at the forefront of applied AI: from large-scale recommender systems in finance to malaria mapping in Africa, and from self-driving systems at Tesla and Waymo. With a background in multimodal deep learning and computer vision from Carnegie Mellon University, she now leads efforts in gesture understanding and pedestrian semantics at Waymo.

In this conversation, Aishwarya shares what it really takes to bring AI from research to production in safety-critical environments. We’ll discuss the trade-offs between accuracy and performance, how to evaluate perception models for deployment, and what lessons from AI for social good can teach big-tech engineering teams.
We plan to cover:
What it takes to move perception and multimodal models into production
How to evaluate and validate safety-critical AI systems
Where multimodal AI makes a real difference, and where it adds complexity
Lessons from malaria vector mapping for low-resource, high-impact applications
Skills and habits engineers need to thrive in applied AI roles
bio: Aishwarya Jadhav is a Machine Learning Engineer at Waymo, where she leads projects on gesture understanding and pedestrian semantics. She previously worked at Tesla Autopilot AI on multimodal LLMs and video understanding, and at Morgan Stanley on large-scale recommendation systems. Aishwarya holds a Master’s degree in Computer Science from Carnegie Mellon University, where she focused on multimodal deep learning and text information extraction. She has also contributed to social impact projects, including malaria vector mapping in Africa, which helped reduce mosquito populations by 60% at minimal cost.
links: LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/aishwaryajadhav8/
Twitter: TODO
Github: TODO
Website: TODO
speech: Today on the podcast, we’re joined by Aishwarya Jadhav — Machine Learning Engineer at Waymo, formerly part of Tesla’s Autopilot AI team, and a Carnegie Mellon University alum.

Aishwarya has worked across some of the toughest applied AI problems: financial recommender systems at Morgan Stanley, multimodal research at CMU, perception and video understanding at Tesla, and now gesture and pedestrian semantics at Waymo. She’s also contributed to AI for social good, including a malaria mapping project in Africa that achieved real-world impact at scale.
Aishwarya, welcome to the podcast!
You’ve worked on ML in finance, healthcare, and now autonomous driving. Can you tell us more about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
What connects these very different domains for you?
What skills carried across these domains, and what did you have to relearn from scratch?
Questions:
What was the most challenging adjustment when moving from financial recommendation systems at Morgan Stanley to perception models at Tesla and Waymo?

At Tesla you worked on video understanding and multimodal LLMs, and at Waymo you lead projects on gesture and pedestrian semantics. What practical problems do teams face when turning research models into production systems for self-driving?
When you evaluate models for pedestrian or gesture recognition, what metrics or validation methods matter most to decide if they are safe enough to deploy?

In your CMU research and later roles, you worked on combining vision, text, and trajectory data. Where have you seen multimodality clearly improve results, and where did it add complexity without much benefit?
What’s an example of a trade-off you’ve had to make between model accuracy and real-time performance in multimodal or perception systems?
Your malaria vector mapping project reduced mosquito populations significantly at very low cost. What design choices made the model both accurate and lightweight enough for low-resource settings?
Looking back, what lessons from that project have stayed with you when working on high-budget, high-compute projects in big tech?
Organizing AI related projects
Researchy, need multiple streams or groups working on different experiments
Cannot rely completely on one method, need multiple parallel
Making trade-offs still relates to having accurate information about what are the accuracy / error expectations
That governs a whole lot of decision choices you make: from the kind of data you include to what metrics you choose to compare and pick models
Analyzing deeply to uncover issues and building based off of those insights rather than blindly just trying everything
In your experience, what are the main bottlenecks preventing broader deployment of self-driving systems today — is it perception accuracy, edge cases, or something else?

Technological problems: edge-cases, world is constantly evolving and people or other road entities can be very dynamic. Although the SDC is much safer than a human driver (given its faster reaction times), there are still a few open research problems in terms of communicating with the world around it
Wide-spread adoption - there is still the people aspect, do not trust it and are not fully accepting of it → ties into gov and policies.
For engineers who want to enter applied AI fields like autonomy, what skills or habits do you think matter most beyond just knowing the algorithms?

topic: From Semiconductors to Machine Learning: A Career in Data and Teaching
title: From Semiconductors to Machine Learning: A Career in Data and Teaching
bio: Dashel Ruiz Perez is a data analyst, ML engineer, and educator based in Oregon, USA. He spent nearly a decade at Microchip Technology, where he worked across production, process, yield, and software engineering roles. Today, he teaches programming and data skills at major U.S. universities through ThriveDX and continues to deepen his expertise in machine learning and AI. Dashel holds degrees in computer science and data analytics from Western Governors University and is a graduate of ML Zoomcamp. Speech
questions: How did ML Zoomcamp help you move deeper into machine learning and engineering roles?
What role do you see for frameworks like LangChain in making ML accessible to software developers who aren’t data experts?
Are there any books, courses, or other resources you’d recommend to listeners who want to follow a similar path?

Intro (5 minutes)

event_description: Title: From Semiconductors to Machine Learning: A Career in Data and Teaching

Dashel Ruiz Perez has built his career across semiconductor engineering, software development, and machine learning. He worked with complex industrial datasets at Microchip Technology, transitioned into software and analytics, and most recently taught programming and data skills at major universities through ThriveDX.
In this conversation, Dashel shares how data shaped his work in manufacturing, what it takes to teach ML effectively, and how completing ML Zoomcamp influenced his career growth.
We plan to cover:
Using data in semiconductor manufacturing and engineering
Lessons from teaching programming and ML at scale
First-hand experience with ML Zoomcamp: projects, takeaways, and career impact
Moving from dashboards to ML-powered applications
Advice for professionals transitioning into data and AI later in their careers
bio: Dashel Ruiz Perez is a data analyst, ML engineer, and educator based in Oregon, USA. Dashel holds degrees in computer science and data analytics from Western Governors University and is a graduate of ML Zoomcamp.
speech: Today on the podcast, we’re joined by Dashel Ruiz Perez — data analyst, ML engineer, software engineer, and ML Zoomcamp graduate.

Dashel’s career path has spanned semiconductor manufacturing, software engineering, data analytics, and teaching. At Microchip Technology, he worked with complex engineering and production data before moving into software and machine learning. Today, he teaches programming and data at major U.S. universities as Lead Instructor at ThriveDX.
Dashel, welcome to the podcast!
Your path is unusual: from semiconductors to software, data, and ML. And you also worked as a guitar teacher! Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Questions:

At Microchip Technology, you moved from production into yield and software engineering. How was data used in semiconductor manufacturing, and what did you learn from working with such complex industrial datasets?
You’ve shifted careers multiple times—from manufacturing to software, and then to ML. What advice would you give to professionals who want to break into data and AI later in their career?
As Lead Instructor at ThriveDX, you’ve taught programming and data skills at major universities. What do learners struggle with most when they first get into data and ML?
You’ve earned multiple certifications and degrees while working and teaching. Looking back, what learning paths or skills gave you the biggest leverage?
You’re also a graduate of ML Zoomcamp. What motivated you to join, and what was your experience like?
During ML Zoomcamp, what project or lesson from the course had the biggest impact on you?
How did ML Zoomcamp help you move deeper into machine learning and engineering roles?
If someone is considering joining, what advice would you give them?
You’ve worked with tools like Tableau, React, and LangChain. Where do you see the biggest opportunities to move from dashboards to ML-powered applications?
What role do you see for frameworks like LangChain in making ML accessible to software developers who aren’t data experts?
Based on your experience, what’s one skill every data professional should focus on in the next 3 years?
Are there any books, courses, or other resources you’d recommend to listeners who want to follow a similar path?

3-4 bullet points

Abouzar Abbaspour is a machine learning and data engineer whose career spans startups, academia, e-commerce, theme parks, and automotive. He co-founded a telecom startup in Iran, worked on forecasting models and recommendation engines at Efteling, and later deployed large-scale ML models at bol.com. Today, at Tesla, he focuses on predictive maintenance, LLM agents, and scalable pipelines. Abouzar holds an EngD in Data Science from Eindhoven University of Technology.

event_description: Title: From Theme Parks to Tesla: Building Data Products That Work
Subtitle: A conversation with Abouzar Abbaspour on ML engineering, data pipelines, and real-world impact

What does it take to turn raw data into products that millions of people love to use? Abouzar Abbaspour will discuss this based on his experience working with startups, amusement parks, e-commerce, and now Tesla.

At Efteling theme park, he built queue-time forecasting and recommendation systems for visitors. At bol.com, he helped deploy a recommendation engine to over 6 million users. And today at Tesla, he works on predictive maintenance and integrates LLM agents into production systems.
In this conversation, Abouzar shares stories from the front lines of data and ML engineering: what succeeds, what fails, and what matters when you move from experiments to production.
We plan to cover:
Forecasting queues and building recommendation systems at a theme park
Deploying large-scale recommender systems at bol.com
The leap from data engineering into ML engineering
Predictive maintenance and LLM agents at Tesla
Why productionizing ML is about much more than the model
Which trends in data and ML are hype, and which are here to stay
speech: Today on the podcast, we’re joined by Abouzar Abbaspour.

He’s worked on data and ML projects in very different places — from amusement parks to e-commerce and now Tesla. We’ll talk about how to turn data into real products, what it means to productionize ML, and what the future of data and ML engineering might look like.
Abouzar, welcome to the podcast!
Abouzar, your career spans startups, amusement parks, e-commerce, and now Tesla. Can you walk us through your journey?
Questions:
1. Personal reflections
You co-founded a startup, worked in academia, and are now in big tech. Which of these gave you the biggest learning curve?
You grew up in Iran and then built your career in Europe. How has your background shaped the way you solve problems?
2. Stories from the amusement park (Efteling)
At Efteling, you built models to predict queue times and recommend next best actions. How did you turn raw visitor data into something useful?
What did you learn about making ML models work well for real people in such a physical, customer-facing place?
3. From theme parks to 6M users at bol.com
At bol.com, you worked on a recommendation engine that later served 6 million users. How did you test and validate the model before going live at that scale?
Can you share a story where business or product limits shaped the final solution more than the technical side?
4. Bridging data engineering and ML engineering
You started as a senior data engineer and later moved into ML engineering. Which skills carried over, and what was the hardest part to learn in the new role?
In your view, what do data engineers often misunderstand about ML, and what do ML engineers often misunderstand about data engineering?
5. Tesla and production ML at scale
At Tesla, you split your time between data engineering and ML. Can you tell us about a project where predictive maintenance models or LLM agents directly improved workflows?
What does deployment and monitoring look like in such a high-stakes environment? How do you balance moving fast with keeping things safe and reliable?
6. Productionizing
You’ve said it’s important to think about the end user, not just the model. What are the common mistakes teams make when moving from a Jupyter notebook to a real product?
Can you share an example where improving the pipeline — things like Airflow, CI/CD, or Kubernetes — made a big difference for the ML product?
7. Problem-solving mindset
You describe yourself as a problem solver. Can you share a story where limits or constraints forced you into a creative but effective solution?
8. Industry evolution
You’ve seen the industry shift from Hadoop to GCP pipelines to today’s LLM agents. Which of these changes are true game-changers, and which are more hype?
How do you see the roles of data engineer and ML engineer changing in the next few years? Do you think they will merge, or become even more separate?
9. Closing
If you could give one piece of advice to someone who wants to build impactful ML or data products today, what would it be?
Where can listeners follow your work and see the visualizations you’ve prepared?
Are there any books or other resources you would recommend to the listeners?
Core Themes to Explore
1. Data + ML at Tesla (current role)
Predictive maintenance with ML: how do you design and deploy models that directly impact physical systems (cars, factories)?
Integrating LLM RAGs and agents into existing apps: what works, what’s hype, and how do you make them useful in production?
Building custom Airflow operators and CI/CD pipelines: lessons learned in making data/ML workflows actually reliable at scale.
Kubernetes + ML systems: how to balance scalability, cost, and team productivity.
2. Transition from Data Engineering to ML Engineering
At bol.com he moved from DE → MLE. What skills and mindset shifts were most important?
How do data engineering foundations prepare someone for ML system design?
Advice for people who want to make the same transition.
3. Real-world Recommendation Systems
At bol.com: recommendation engine for 6M+ users. What did it take to validate and deploy it?
At Efteling: queue forecasting + “next best action” recommendations in a theme park setting. What unique data challenges appear outside the “classic tech company” environment?
How do you balance business goals, user experience, and technical constraints in recommender systems?
4. From Startup Founder → Engineer in Big Tech
Netgat: building a data startup monitoring telecom networks, lessons in handling raw sensor data at scale.
Contrast between startup life (end-to-end ownership, resource constraints) and big-tech engineering (Tesla, bol.com).
5. Evolution of the Data & ML Landscape
He’s worked across: telecom, theme parks, e-commerce, automotive. What are the recurring data problems across industries, and what’s unique in each?
Trends he’s seen: Hadoop → GCP pipelines → LLM agents. What’s hype vs. real progress?
Where he sees the data/ML engineer role going in the next 5–10 years.
6. Personal Perspective
Coming from Iran → TU Eindhoven → Europe tech → Tesla: reflections on navigating global tech career paths.
How cultural roots and personal background shape his approach to problem-solving.

topic: How to Build Reliable AI Assistants:
bio: Ranjitha Gurunath Kulkarni is a Staff Machine Learning Engineer at NeuBird.ai. Previously, she built LLM- and agent-powered product capabilities at Dropbox Dash and worked on speech recognition, language modeling, online metrics, and assistant evaluation at Microsoft. Her publications span voice query reformulation and automatic online evaluation of intelligent assistants, and her patents include automated closed captioning using temporal data and hyperarticulation detection. Ranjitha holds a master’s from Carnegie Mellon University (Language Technologies Institute). Speech
questions: How did your time at CMU and Microsoft working on speech and language modeling shape how you approach today’s LLM and agent systems?
What’s an example where user feedback or behavior forced you to rethink the design of an ML-powered assistant?
How do you measure whether an assistant or agent is truly helping the user?
What evaluation methods or metrics turned out to be misleading in practice, and what did you replace them with?

event_description: Ranjitha has shipped AI features across multiple generations of NLP: from speech recognition and online evaluation at Microsoft to LLMs, RAG, and agents in production at Dropbox Dash, and now agentic systems at NeuBird.ai. In this conversation, she shares the concrete practices that make assistants useful, trustworthy, and maintainable in real products.
We plan to cover:
How early work in speech systems still shapes today’s LLMs and agents
What it takes to turn an LLM demo into a dependable product
Where RAG and agents shine and where they fall short
Skills engineers need today to succeed with applied NLP and agents
bio: Ranjitha Gurunath Kulkarni is a Staff Machine Learning Engineer at NeuBird.ai. Previously, she built LLM- and agent-powered product capabilities at Dropbox Dash and worked on speech recognition, language modeling, online metrics, and assistant evaluation at Microsoft. Her publications span voice query reformulation and automatic online evaluation of intelligent assistants, and her patents include automated closed captioning using temporal data and hyperarticulation detection. Ranjitha holds a master’s from Carnegie Mellon University (Language Technologies Institute).
speech: Today on the podcast, we’re joined by Ranjitha Gurunath Kulkarni — Staff ML Engineer at NeuBird.ai, previously on Dropbox Dash building LLM- and agent-powered products, with earlier work in speech recognition and NLP at Microsoft and research at Carnegie Mellon University.
Ranjitha, welcome to the podcast!
You’ve worked on NLP and AI systems at Microsoft, Dropbox, and now Neubird. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Looking back, what’s been the common thread in the problems you’ve chosen to work on?
Questions:
How did your time at CMU and Microsoft working on speech and language modeling shape how you approach today’s LLM and agent systems?
At Dropbox Dash, you worked on building products powered by LLMs and agents. What were the most practical challenges of moving from prototypes to something reliable for end users?
In your experience, what kinds of business problems are a good fit for RAG or AI agents — and where do they fall short?
What’s an example where user feedback or behavior forced you to rethink the design of an ML-powered assistant?
How do you measure whether an assistant or agent is truly helping the user?
What evaluation methods or metrics turned out to be misleading in practice, and what did you replace them with?

At Microsoft you worked on hyperarticulation detection and query reformulation. Can you explain us what these projects were about? What did those projects teach you about real-world user behavior that still applies in today’s LLM world?
Having seen the shift from statistical NLP to neural models and now LLMs/agents, what has really changed in building AI assistants — and what fundamentals stayed the same?
For engineers who want to work on applied NLP and agent systems, what skills or practices do you think matter most today?

team_tab: Title: From Biotechnology to Bioinformatics Software
Subtitle: Developing tools to make complex biological data more accessible – with Sebastian Ayala Ruano

Sebastian Ayala Ruano began his studies in Biotechnology Engineering, but soon realized the wet lab wasn’t the right environment for him. With a growing interest in programming, he pursued a minor in Software Engineering and later a Master’s degree that connected both fields.

Today, Sebastian works in bioinformatics software development, contributing to open-source projects like MicW2Graph, VueGen, and VueCore. These tools help researchers make sense of complex multi-omics data, supporting new ways of understanding biology.
We plan to cover:
Transitioning from biotechnology to computational work
Building and maintaining open-source bioinformatics tools
The challenges and opportunities of multi-omics data analysis
Advice for life science students interested in programming and bioinformatics
About the Guest

Sebastian Ayala Ruano is a bioinformatics software developer whose work bridges biotechnology and computational biology. He has contributed to open-source tools including MicW2Graph (https://github.com/Multiomics-Analytics-Group/MicW2Graph), VueGen (https://github.com/Multiomics-Analytics-Group/vuegen), and VueCore (https://github.com/Multiomics-Analytics-Group/vuecore), designed to simplify multi-omics data analysis for researchers. Previously, he worked on projects in cheminformatics, peptide discovery, and network-based analysis, and has developed educational bioinformatics tools for open science communities.

Sebastian is currently a research assistant in the Multiomics Network Analytics Group (https://multiomics-analytics-group.github.io/) at DTU Biosustain (https://www.biosustain.dtu.dk/) in Denmark, where he focuses on integrating machine learning and network science into biological research. Also, he is an active member of several research, open-science, and software development communities (The Turing Way (https://book.the-turing-way.org/), Open Life Science (https://openlifesci.org/), Streamlit Creators (https://streamlit.io/creators), ISCBSC (https://iscbsc.org/), and The Carpentries (https://carpentries.org/)) and led initiatives that advance Bioinformatics in Ecuador and Latin America. He shares his projects and insights through his personal website and GitHub.

Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))

India - London - Germany - New York - Boston; From Physics, Maths and Computer Science to Electrical Communication Engineering, and finally from Computer Science to Computational Biology to Cancer Genomics
Where does this story begin for you? Ph.D, alternative splicing, etc
Ph.D work on prediction of alternative splicing
Which events, successes, or stumbles taught you the most?
Features often matter more than model choice, e.g. BN & SVM were essentially tied (but a true expert in a given model can probably squeeze a lot more out of it )
With a sufficiently accurate model, you can even question the data (FPs turned out to be real AS events with updated evidence)

With a sufficiently strong model, FNs might point to something missed entirely by your model, and potentially a new mechanism - reanalyze just the FNs & see if something “pops up”. We found conserved secondary structure which occluded the splice site, but were pipped/scooped by someone else to the paper (they became a future collaborator and friend)
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?
Validate, validate, validate!
Know the application domain
Make sure you’re indeed comparing apples to apples
Was there a detour that almost changed your direction?
That might be happening right now - I’m in between jobs, and might end up pivoting into AI/ML without Biology if I have to, though ideally I would combine them, e.g. in Early Cancer Detection.
What led you to transition from bioinformatics research in cancer genomics to applying machine learning and AI?

That hasn’t really happened, in the sense that my Ph.D research already applied machine learning, and I moved into cancer genomics at the post-doc stage. Since then, I’ve applied ML depending on the project requirements, and have taught myself from various resources from time to time. In the last 2 years, I’ve done more courses (esp. from Coursera and DataTalks Club), and competed on Kaggle.
Projects & Industry Trends
Can you give an overview of your work on tumor modeling, integrative multi-omics analyses, and predictive modeling for cancer therapy?
Comparing cell lines and tumors, TCGA and other projects
This can get really long, so I’ll try to be very brief, also depending on where we are time-wise. Could always return to it if we seem to be finishing too soon
When working on these projects, how do you balance biological complexity with computational constraints?

This usually varies, and detailed discussions with collaborators play a crucial part in determining this. One often has to pick up new biological knowledge to do proper justice to a project. There are of course also project where you’re only expected to perform some standard analyses to help answer a given set of questions. It’s also fairly common to start out with standard methods and then having to get deep into the weeds depending on the early findings.
How has participating in Kaggle competitions and hands-on ML courses shaped your approach to problem-solving and collaboration in computational biology?

Participating in Kaggle competitions and Hands-on ML has sharpened my appreciation of the importance of reproducibility in particular. For a long time, many of us in academia used to be content with proof-of-application code and moving to the next project and publication. More recently, there has been a growing awareness that reproducibility is a core requirement of good science, and ML Zoomcamp, LLM Zoomcamp and ML Engineering Zoomcamp helped me build good habits related to various aspects of reproducibility including virtual environments, flask, docker, cloud implementations, etc.
Multi-omics and AI-driven research are growing rapidly; what opportunities or insights are now possible that weren’t before?

The fascinating thing is that there are things that are possible because of advances in technology that generates large-scale data, and there are things that are possible because of advances in ML/AI, and these can feed off each other.
From your perspective, what are the main challenges in managing and analyzing large-scale biological datasets today?

I guess there are challenges on multiple fronts - as data grows, there are challenges with access and storage, efficient retrieval, etc. Large-scale data can also require specialized tools in coding - for example, once file sizes run into gigabytes, conventional file opening tools might struggle. As an example, conventional R code might struggle with reading huge input files, but specialized tools like the data.table package or the tidyverse package do a much better job. Those responsible for managing large-scale data are expected to be familiar with cluster computing, cloud computing and so on.
How do you see the role of computational biologists and AI/ML practitioners evolving in cancer research over the next few years

This is very complex, because AI itself could be responsible for a loss of jobs in cancer research, just like in so many areas. It’s easy to say that “you won’t be replaced by AI, but you will be replaced by another Computational Biologist using AI”, but organizations are definitely looking to replace people with AI, so there’s a real risk of a certain percentage of people losing jobs across industries and research areas, including cancer research, and even AI/ML themselves. What do you think?
Personal Insights & Advice
Is there a beginner group that keeps asking you about this?

The most frequent questions I get from beginners nowadays are about how to do better on Kaggle, owing to my having done well in the Tabular Playground Series on Kaggle, which are monthly competitions that typically attract 2000-4000 participants. I’ve secured 11 top 10 finishes in the last 1.5 years, including two 1st place finishes. So I tend to get a lot of questions from beginners about doing well on Kaggle in general, and tabular competitions in particular.
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
Validate, validate, validate!
Always know your domain, and be ready to learn more, and consult experts/collaborators as needed.
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

There is, of course, Alexey’s book for a great hands-on introduction which naturally pairs very well with the ML-Zoomcamp course. Three great books combining some theory with hands-on work in both ML and DL are Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow by Aurélien Géron, Machine Learning with PyTorch and Scikit-Learn by Sebastian Raschka et al., and An Introduction to Statistical Learning by James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani; while those desiring more theory can look up books by Kevin Murphy, The Elements of Statistical Learning by Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani and Jerome Friedman, or Shai Shalev-Shwartz & Shai Ben-David. Raschka also has a great YouTube channel, and his courses are great, as are those by Data Talks Club, and by Prof. Andrew Ng of Stanford and DeepLearning.AI.
What skills or approaches have been most valuable in your transition from bioinformatics to AI-driven research?
Not really applicable, since I’ve used ML off and on since the beginning, and am not doing AI-driven research in cancer genomics right now.
For students or early-career researchers interested in computational biology and ML, what would be a practical first step to get started?

This would depend strongly on their background, e.g. for people with a computational background, it would be very important to learn some relevant biology, and for biologists, to learn some programming and data science - then both can learn Computational Biology. This was in fact the approach followed in a postgraduate course I did in Cologne, Germany. In some ways, ML Zoomcamp reminded me of that course, as we learned new things every week, and were tested on them before moving on to the next topic.
How do you decide which research ideas or tools to pursue as public projects versus specialized internal analyses?

This would really depend on the organization, and project leadership. As a very rough guide, one might expect more of a public approach in academia and internal analyses in industry, but there are always exceptions.
team_tab: Applying ML: An Ongoing Personal Journey
Bridging Biology, Data, and Machine Learning in Cancer Science – Rileen Sinha

Rileen’s path spans from bioinformatics research and cancer genomics to applying machine learning and AI in translational medicine, giving him a front-row view of how computational biology and AI intersect to advance cancer research.

In this conversation, we’ll explore what truly matters when integrating machine learning into biomedical research, the recurring themes in cancer genomics, and how hands-on learning, competitions, and practical application can shape impactful scientific work.
He’ll cover:
His path from genomics research to applying ML in biomedicine
Lessons from hands-on learning, Kaggle competitions, and practical ML projects
The role of interdisciplinary collaboration in modern cancer research
What truly matters when integrating AI and ML into scientific discovery
Advice for learners entering computational biology and data-driven medicine

About the speaker:Rileen Sinha is a senior computational biologist and cancer data scientist with extensive experience in genomics research, translational medicine, and AI/ML applications. He has published first-author papers in Nature Communications and Cell Reports Methods, contributed to large-scale consortia including TCGA and CPTAC, and led interdisciplinary projects integrating wet-lab and computational insights.
Rileen continues to expand his expertise in AI and machine learning through hands-on courses and competitions, bridging research and applied data science.
speech: This week, we’ll discuss how computational biology and AI intersect to advance cancer research.
We have a special guest today – Rileen Sinha.

Rileen is a senior computational biologist and cancer data scientist who has worked with leading institutions such as MSKCC, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, and Mount Sinai. His work spans cancer genomics, tumor modeling, integrative multi-omics, and machine learning applications in translational medicine. He has published first-author papers in Nature Communications and Cell Reports Methods, and continues to explore AI and ML through hands-on courses and Kaggle competitions.
Welcome, Rileen!

Was there a detour that almost changed your direction?
What’s your current role, and what single experience ties you to this theme?
What fact links present-you to this topic?
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
We’ll handle the editing and formatting from there. Thanks for contributing to the community!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
India - London - Germany - New York - Boston; From Physics, Maths and Computer Science to Electrical Communication Engineering, and finally from Computer Science to Computational Biology to Cancer Genomics
Where does this story begin for you? Ph.D, alternative splicing, etc
Ph.D work on prediction of alternative splicing
Which events, successes, or stumbles taught you the most?
Features often matter more than model choice, e.g. BN & SVM were essentially tied (but a true expert in a given model can probably squeeze a lot more out of it )
With a sufficiently accurate model, you can even question the data (FPs turned out to be real AS events with updated evidence)
With a sufficiently strong model, FNs might point to something missed entirely by your model, and potentially a new mechanism - reanalyze just the FNs & see if something “pops up”. We found conserved secondary structure which occluded the splice site, but were pipped/scooped by someone else to the paper (they became a future collaborator and friend)
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?
Validate, validate, validate!
Know the application domain
Make sure you’re indeed comparing apples to apples
Was there a detour that almost changed your direction?
That might be happening right now - I’m in between jobs, and might end up pivoting into AI/ML without Biology if I have to, though ideally I would combine them, e.g. in Early Cancer Detection.
What led you to transition from bioinformatics research in cancer genomics to applying machine learning and AI?
That hasn’t really happened, in the sense that my Ph.D research already applied machine learning, and I moved into cancer genomics at the post-doc stage. Since then, I’ve applied ML depending on the project requirements, and have taught myself from various resources from time to time. In the last 2 years, I’ve done more courses (esp. from Coursera and DataTalks Club), and competed on Kaggle.
Projects & Industry Trends
Can you give an overview of your work on tumor modeling, integrative multi-omics analyses, and predictive modeling for cancer therapy?
Comparing cell lines and tumors, TCGA and other projects
This can get really long, so I’ll try to be very brief, also depending on where we are time-wise. Could always return to it if we seem to be finishing too soon
When working on these projects, how do you balance biological complexity with computational constraints?
This usually varies, and detailed discussions with collaborators play a crucial part in determining this. One often has to pick up new biological knowledge to do proper justice to a project. There are of course also project where you’re only expected to perform some standard analyses to help answer a given set of questions. It’s also fairly common to start out with standard methods and then having to get deep into the weeds depending on the early findings.
How has participating in Kaggle competitions and hands-on ML courses shaped your approach to problem-solving and collaboration in computational biology?
Participating in Kaggle competitions and Hands-on ML has sharpened my appreciation of the importance of reproducibility in particular. For a long time, many of us in academia used to be content with proof-of-application code and moving to the next project and publication. More recently, there has been a growing awareness that reproducibility is a core requirement of good science, and ML Zoomcamp, LLM Zoomcamp and ML Engineering Zoomcamp helped me build good habits related to various aspects of reproducibility including virtual environments, flask, docker, cloud implementations, etc.
Multi-omics and AI-driven research are growing rapidly; what opportunities or insights are now possible that weren’t before?
The fascinating thing is that there are things that are possible because of advances in technology that generates large-scale data, and there are things that are possible because of advances in ML/AI, and these can feed off each other.
From your perspective, what are the main challenges in managing and analyzing large-scale biological datasets today?
I guess there are challenges on multiple fronts - as data grows, there are challenges with access and storage, efficient retrieval, etc. Large-scale data can also require specialized tools in coding - for example, once file sizes run into gigabytes, conventional file opening tools might struggle. As an example, conventional R code might struggle with reading huge input files, but specialized tools like the data.table package or the tidyverse package do a much better job. Those responsible for managing large-scale data are expected to be familiar with cluster computing, cloud computing and so on.
How do you see the role of computational biologists and AI/ML practitioners evolving in cancer research over the next few years
This is very complex, because AI itself could be responsible for a loss of jobs in cancer research, just like in so many areas. It’s easy to say that “you won’t be replaced by AI, but you will be replaced by another Computational Biologist using AI”, but organizations are definitely looking to replace people with AI, so there’s a real risk of a certain percentage of people losing jobs across industries and research areas, including cancer research, and even AI/ML themselves. What do you think?
Personal Insights & Advice
Is there a beginner group that keeps asking you about this?
The most frequent questions I get from beginners nowadays are about how to do better on Kaggle, owing to my having done well in the Tabular Playground Series on Kaggle, which are monthly competitions that typically attract 2000-4000 participants. I’ve secured 11 top 10 finishes in the last 1.5 years, including two 1st place finishes. So I tend to get a lot of questions from beginners about doing well on Kaggle in general, and tabular competitions in particular.
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
Validate, validate, validate!
Always know your domain, and be ready to learn more, and consult experts/collaborators as needed.
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
There is, of course, Alexey’s book for a great hands-on introduction which naturally pairs very well with the ML-Zoomcamp course. Three great books combining some theory with hands-on work in both ML and DL are Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow by Aurélien Géron, Machine Learning with PyTorch and Scikit-Learn by Sebastian Raschka et al., and An Introduction to Statistical Learning by James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani; while those desiring more theory can look up books by Kevin Murphy, The Elements of Statistical Learning by Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani and Jerome Friedman, or Shai Shalev-Shwartz & Shai Ben-David. Raschka also has a great YouTube channel, and his courses are great, as are those by Data Talks Club, and by Prof. Andrew Ng of Stanford and DeepLearning.AI.
What skills or approaches have been most valuable in your transition from bioinformatics to AI-driven research?
Not really applicable, since I’ve used ML off and on since the beginning, and am not doing AI-driven research in cancer genomics right now.
For students or early-career researchers interested in computational biology and ML, what would be a practical first step to get started?
This would depend strongly on their background, e.g. for people with a computational background, it would be very important to learn some relevant biology, and for biologists, to learn some programming and data science - then both can learn Computational Biology. This was in fact the approach followed in a postgraduate course I did in Cologne, Germany. In some ways, ML Zoomcamp reminded me of that course, as we learned new things every week, and were tested on them before moving on to the next topic.
How do you decide which research ideas or tools to pursue as public projects versus specialized internal analyses?
This would really depend on the organization, and project leadership. As a very rough guide, one might expect more of a public approach in academia and internal analyses in industry, but there are always exceptions.
Tab for Alexey and DTC Team
Applying ML: An Ongoing Personal Journey
Bridging Biology, Data, and Machine Learning in Cancer Science – Rileen Sinha
Rileen’s path spans from bioinformatics research and cancer genomics to applying machine learning and AI in translational medicine, giving him a front-row view of how computational biology and AI intersect to advance cancer research.
In this conversation, we’ll explore what truly matters when integrating machine learning into biomedical research, the recurring themes in cancer genomics, and how hands-on learning, competitions, and practical application can shape impactful scientific work.
He’ll cover:
His path from genomics research to applying ML in biomedicine
Lessons from hands-on learning, Kaggle competitions, and practical ML projects
The role of interdisciplinary collaboration in modern cancer research
What truly matters when integrating AI and ML into scientific discovery
Advice for learners entering computational biology and data-driven medicine
About the speaker:Rileen Sinha is a senior computational biologist and cancer data scientist with extensive experience in genomics research, translational medicine, and AI/ML applications. He has published first-author papers in Nature Communications and Cell Reports Methods, contributed to large-scale consortia including TCGA and CPTAC, and led interdisciplinary projects integrating wet-lab and computational insights.
Rileen continues to expand his expertise in AI and machine learning through hands-on courses and competitions, bridging research and applied data science.

This week, we’ll discuss how computational biology and AI intersect to advance cancer research.
We have a special guest today – Rileen Sinha.
Rileen is a senior computational biologist and cancer data scientist who has worked with leading institutions such as MSKCC, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, and Mount Sinai. His work spans cancer genomics, tumor modeling, integrative multi-omics, and machine learning applications in translational medicine. He has published first-author papers in Nature Communications and Cell Reports Methods, and continues to explore AI and ML through hands-on courses and Kaggle competitions.
Welcome, Rileen!
Before we go into our main topic of applying AI and machine learning in cancer genomics, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please :) )

topic: How to build and evaluate AI systems in the age of LLMs
subtitle: A practical guide for data scientists and engineers - Hugo Bowne-Anderson
bio: Hugo Bowne-Anderson is an independent data and AI consultant with extensive experience in the tech industry. He has advised and taught teams building AI-powered systems, including engineers from Netflix, Meta, and the U.S. Air Force. He is the host of Vanishing Gradients (https://vanishinggradients.fireside.fm/) and High Signal (https://high-signal.delphina.ai/), podcasts exploring developments in data science and AI. Previously, Hugo served as Head of Developer Relations at Outerbounds and held roles at Coiled and DataCamp, where his work in data science education reached over 6 million learners. He has taught at Yale University, Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, and conferences like SciPy and PyCon, and is a passionate advocate for democratizing data skills and open-source tools. He also regularly teaches courses on Building LLM Applications for Data Scientists and Software Engineers (https://maven.com/s/course/d56067f338).

team_tab: How to build and evaluate AI systems in the age of LLMs
Subtitle: A practical guide for data scientists and engineers - Hugo Bowne-Anderson

As AI moves from experimentation to real-world impact, the challenges are no longer just technical. They’re about design, evaluation, and collaboration. In this episode, Hugo will share his perspective on how teams and individuals can build AI responsibly, work effectively across disciplines, and keep learning as the field continues to change.
He’ll cover:
When (and when not) to build AI agents
Using AI for coding vs. building software with LLMs
The AI software development lifecycle and escaping “PoC purgatory”
What happens to data science in the age of AI
bio: Hugo Bowne-Anderson is an independent data and AI consultant with extensive experience in the tech industry. He has advised and taught teams building AI-powered systems, including engineers from Netflix, Meta, and the U.S. Air Force. He is the host of Vanishing Gradients (https://vanishinggradients.fireside.fm/) and High Signal (https://high-signal.delphina.ai/), podcasts exploring developments in data science and AI.

Previously, Hugo served as Head of Developer Relations at Outerbounds and held roles at Coiled and DataCamp, where his work in data science education reached over 6 million learners. He has taught at Yale University, Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, and conferences like SciPy and PyCon, and is a passionate advocate for democratizing data skills and open-source tools. He also regularly teaches courses on Building LLM Applications for Data Scientists and Software Engineers (https://maven.com/s/course/d56067f338).
speech: This week we’ll talk about: How the rise of large language models is reshaping how we build, test, and evaluate AI systems and what this means for data scientists, developers, and organizations aiming to create lasting value with AI.
We have a special guest today — Hugo Bowne-Anderson.

Hugo Bowne-Anderson is an independent data and AI consultant with extensive experience in the tech industry. He has advised and taught teams building AI-powered systems, including engineers from Netflix, Meta, and the U.S. Air Force. He is the host of Vanishing Gradients (https://vanishinggradients.fireside.fm/) and High Signal (https://high-signal.delphina.ai/), podcasts exploring developments in data science and AI.

Some of you may remember Hugo from his previous appearance on our podcast in June 2023, Data Developer Relations (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z7BvslwVRbQ), where we explored how developers and data scientists can collaborate to build better AI tools. We’re thrilled to have him back for a deeper dive into how AI systems are actually built and evaluated today.
Welcome, Hugo!

Before we go into our main topic of how to build and evaluate AI systems in the age of LLMs, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please :))
Bio/math/physics using iPython NBs and pydata stack
~2014/2015: data science /ML meetups
Joined DataCamp as 5th employee to build out Python curriculum
Wore many hats, products, data science, curriculum, marketing
Led devrel for several pydata adjacent OSS tools turn vendors
Went freelance last year: consulting, advising, teaching (https://maven.com/hugo-stefan/building-ai-apps-ds-and-swe-from-first-principles?promoCode=datatalksclub)
Questions:
When (and When Not) to Build AI Agents
When you look at real-world use cases, how do you decide if an AI agent is the right approach versus a simpler tool or workflow?
Answer the following questions:
Does it need to do something or is returning text enough?
Does the AI system need to trigger it’s own actions or can we define them in workflows?
Stepping back, let’s see “agents or llm” as a spectrum, rather than a binary:
First you have LLM; then memory, retrieval, tools; then workflows
What patterns or principles have you noticed that help teams avoid over-engineering with agents?
Write a PRD
Start small
Evaluate
iterate
How should teams evaluate whether an agent-based system is working as intended before scaling it?
Evaluate macro business goals as much as possible and tie them to more fine-grained metrics
AI for Coding vs. Building Software with LLMs
How do you distinguish between using AI as a coding assistant and actually building software that’s powered by LLMs?
You can use AI coding assistants for lots of SW that doesn’t involve LLMs at all, e.g. front end, data analysis, EDA, classic ML
I’d encourage everyone to. let’s briefly look at the wild rapid evolution of AI-assisted coding here:
Level 1: C&P code between ChatGPT & IDE
Level 2: Code completion in IDE
Level 3: agent in IDE/terminal (degrees of how much it breaks down multi-step tasks)
Level 4: AI embedded in other tools: slack, discord, email (whatsapp?)
Level 5: AI more proactive (e.g. code review in CI, gh actions)
Level 6: background agents
Level 7: (haven’t really seen yet) proactive AI – notices stuff in prod, comes to me on a monday and says hey check this out!
What kinds of design or engineering challenges appear once LLMs become core components of the software itself?
Nondeterminism of input and output
Complexity of the system
Think multi-agent crews
Need a good human-in-the-loop
Even looking at your data can be tough
How do you see the role of developers and data scientists evolving as these systems mature?
Increasingly important
Think about the skillsets
We need people who can think and talk data
We also need people who can build and maintain enterprise systems and software
The AI Software Development Lifecycle
You’ve written about “escaping PoC purgatory.” What typically keeps AI projects stuck in that phase, and how can teams break through it?
Let’s talk through this figure (https://www.oreilly.com/radar/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2025/04/LLM-SDLC_Fig1_edit3-1.png)

Great ideas that don’t solve problems: eg edTech company creating an all-purpose AI tutor instead of noticing they could solve 20% of customer support tickets with RAG-BOT that helped learners find the content they were looking

Re nondeterminism pain, the iteration / eval loop can really help (but in it, you can run the same prompts through the system many times! And see the variance of results; then as you iterate, work to reduce the variance)
Also use tests (eg pytest) for the output; regenerate in prod if it doesn’t satisfy certain criteria
Also note that nondeterminism can be a good thing: e.g. research analysts: if i send it out 10 times, i’d want 10 different results that I (or an LLM) can synthesize
What does evaluation-driven development look like in day-to-day practice?
Build MVP (including logging!)
Generate user personas, scenarios, and synthetic data
Run them through your MVP
Hand label 20, 50, 100 traces as pass/fail with reason
Cluster into failure modes
You can then see what needs fixing!

This may involve prompt eng, changing chunking strategies, embeddings; reranking, adding/removing tool calls; structured outputs; temp; top-p; how you handle memory for multi-turn convos ← but a lot of the time
How can teams balance rapid experimentation with the need for robust monitoring and testing in production AI systems?
Tooling and process
What Happens to Data Science in the Age of AI
With LLMs now handling parts of data exploration and modeling, how do you see the identity of data scientists changing?
Opens them up for more creative work
Opens them up to think about the business more
Allows them to design systems instead of debugging pandas code
Which skills or ways of thinking will remain critical for data scientists as AI systems evolve?
Failure analysis
How can organizations make sure human judgment still guides interpretation and decision-making in AI workflows?
Having people who are close to the business and the data
Broader Reflections
You’ve worked across education, infrastructure, and communication in the AI space — what connects those threads for you?
The wisdom layer
What’s one thing you’ve learned from hosting your own podcasts that changes how you think about AI discussions today?
We need more real talk
Are there any books, podcasts, or resources you’d recommend for people who want to better understand the evolving relationship between data science and AI systems?
I actually compiled a list and we can chat through some of it! (https://maven.com/p/b126cc/essential-llm-ai-engineering-resources-a-curated-guide-for-engineers-data-scientists)
