event_description: Title: How to Become an AI Engineer After a Career Break

Restarting a tech career after a long break can feel overwhelming, especially in a fast-moving field like AI. In this session, we’ll talk openly about what it really takes to return to work after years away and successfully transition into an AI Engineer role. This is not a motivational talk. It is a practical discussion grounded in real experience, real interviews, and real hiring processes.
In this conversation, we will cover:
How to restart your career after a long break
How to position past experience when switching domains
What the AI Engineer role looks like in practice
What technical questions are asked in AI Engineer interviews
What companies evaluate in take-home assignments
Practical steps to build confidence and become job-ready

If you are returning after maternity leave, changing career paths, or aiming for your first AI Engineering role, this session will give you a realistic view of the process and concrete actions you can take next.
About the guest:

Revathy Ramalingam is an AI Engineer at a healthcare startup with over nine years of prior experience in software development in the telecom domain. After a seven-year career break, she upskilled through ML Zoomcamp, AI Dev Tools Zoomcamp, and the AI Hero email course at DataTalks.Club, and successfully transitioned back into the industry. She will share the strategies, interview insights, and lessons that helped her rebuild her career step by step.
speech: This week, we’ll talk about restarting a tech career after a long break and the AI Engineer role.
Today’s guest is Revathy Ramalingam, an AI Engineer at a healthcare startup. She brings over nine years of software development experience, originally in the telecom domain.
After a seven-year career, she successfully transitioned back into tech by upskilling through ML Zoomcamp, AI Dev Tools Zoomcamp, and the AI Hero email course at DataTalks.Club.
In this podcast, she shares how she restarted her career, prepared for AI Engineer interviews, and navigated the journey back into the industry with a practical, project-driven approach.
Welcome, Revathy!
Questions
Opening and Background
Can you briefly share your background and what you were doing before becoming an AI Engineer?
What led you to transition into AI after your previous career in telecom and a long break?
After seven years away, what made it possible for you to land an AI Engineer role in today’s competitive market?
Career Restart Strategy
When you decided to return in 2025, what were the first concrete steps you took?
How did you position your telecom experience during interviews?
How did you explain the career break in a confident and structured way?
What is the biggest misconception people have about returning to tech after a long break?
What common mistake do you see people making when trying to re-enter the industry?
Role Reality
What does your work as an AI Engineer in a healthcare startup look like on a typical week?
How different is the role from what you expected while learning?
Which skills helped you most in getting the job: ML fundamentals, coding, system thinking, communication, or something else?
Which skills do candidates usually overestimate in importance?
Interview Process
Can you walk us through your interview process from start to offer?
What types of technical questions were you asked?
Were you evaluated more on theory, coding ability, practical ML experience, or system design?
If you can share, did you complete a take-home assignment? What was the task?
Beyond “does it work,” what do you think companies actually evaluate in take-home assignments?
Looking back, what do you think made your application stand out?
Courses and Resources
How did ML Zoomcamp, AI Dev Tools Zoomcamp, and AI Hero specifically help you prepare?
Which project gave you the strongest leverage in interviews?
If there are details you cannot share about the interview process, which resources, materials, or preparation strategies were especially useful?
Practical Close
What would you do differently if you had to restart your journey today?
If someone has been out of the workforce for years and feels behind, what would you tell them?
If they have just 15 minutes today and want to move toward an AI Engineer role, what should they do first?

team_tab: Inside the AI Engineer Role: Tools, Skills, and Career Path
Ruslan Shchuchkin is a Gen AI Engineer at Finanzguru, where he builds production systems around large language models (LLMs) and generative AI (GenAI) tools.

Before moving into AI engineering, Ruslan worked as a data scientist at Smart Steel Technologies and OLX Group. At OLX, he focused on experimentation, recommendation systems, NLP pipelines, and model evaluation frameworks. His work included building data-driven personalization features, designing ML training and evaluation workflows, and applying techniques such as clustering, topic modeling, and named entity recognition on marketplace data.

Over the past few years, his career has followed a path that many engineers are now exploring: moving from data science and ML experimentation toward building full AI-powered systems and applications.
In this conversation, we will discuss:
How the AI engineer role differs from the data scientist and ML engineer roles
What tools and technologies are used in production AI systems today
How experimentation, evaluation, and deployment change when working with LLMs
What skills aspiring AI engineers should focus on building today
​About the Speaker:

Ruslan Shchuchkin is a Gen AI Engineer at Finanzguru. He has a diverse background spanning Customer Success and Data Science, having previously built scalable machine learning frameworks at OLX Group. Having navigated the mental health challenges of the "Data Science roadmap," Ruslan is a strong advocate for biohacking and mindfulness within the tech community.
speech: This week, we’ll talk about the AI engineer role in practice.
We have a special guest today, Ruslan Shchuchkin.
Ruslan is currently a Gen AI Engineer at Finanzguru, where he builds systems and applications around large language models.

Over the past few years, Ruslan transitioned from classical data science work to building AI systems that integrate LLMs into real products. That makes him a great person to talk to about how the AI engineer role is evolving and what skills are needed today.
Today, we’ll discuss his career path, the tools he uses in practice, and his advice for engineers who want to move into AI engineering.
Welcome, Ruslan! Great to have you here.
Questions
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Career Transition
You started your career in customer success and marketing. What happened when you first saw that Tableau dashboard that made you think you wanted to work with data?
After that moment, you enrolled in a data-focused master’s program and started working on ML projects. What were the early projects that made you realize this field was for you?
You worked on NLP as a data scientist before moving into generative AI. How did the transition from classical NLP and ML to LLM-based systems happen for you?
At some point during your data science career, you were laid off. How did that experience change how you approached learning and preparing for interviews?
What AI Engineers Actually Do
Based on your experience, how would you describe the difference between a data scientist, ML engineer, and AI engineer?
What does a typical AI engineering project look like today compared to the work you did as a data scientist?
When building LLM-based applications, which parts of the system usually require the most engineering effort?
Tools and Working With LLMs
What tools and frameworks do you currently use most often when building AI systems?
The GenAI ecosystem moves very quickly. How do you stay up to date and filter signal from noise?
What are the biggest technical challenges when building applications around LLMs?
How do you approach evaluation and testing when working with generative models?
Breaking Into AI Engineering

You mentioned that personal projects are one of the best ways to get interviews and pass them. What kinds of projects would you recommend building for someone who wants to transition into AI engineering?
If someone today is a data engineer, data scientist, or software engineer and wants to move into AI engineering, what should they focus on learning first?
Looking Ahead
What do you think the AI engineering role will look like in the next 3-5 years?
Are there any resources, blogs, or engineers you recommend following to stay up to date?

guest_tab: These notes help us draft focused questions and an event description.
general_questions: How would you like us to introduce you?

I’m a Data Engineer who spent the bulk of my career at Meta, moving between Individual Contributor and Manager roles. During my time there, I focused on scaling the Data Engineering support for the Meta Ads ranking system. Beyond my direct team, I was also heavily involved in global hiring, conducting hundreds of interviews and helping shape the data engineering recruitment process for the company.
Today, I work as an independent consultant, helping businesses get more out of their Data Engineering teams. On the personal side, I’m a dad, a husband, and a failed bass player.
What are you currently focused on - projects or themes you’d enjoy discussing?

I recently published an ebook that digs into the Data Engineering role and the current hiring landscape. I wrote it because there’s so much confusion right now about what a Data Engineer actually does - different companies approach the role from completely different angles, and the responsibilities vary wildly.

Between the overlap with other roles and AI shaking things up, it's a bit of a mess. Some companies are pushing the envelope, while a long tail of others are stuck with legacy standards that can honestly become a career trap for engineers.
I’d love to talk about the reality of the DE role today - the different specializations, what companies are actually looking for, and where the field is headed.
Which topics are you most keen to cover?
Data Engineering:
Role and specialisations
Industry reality - what to expect and why responsibilities are so widely different
Future of DE role in the age of AI
Are there any topics you’d rather skip or avoid discussing publicly?
I’m under a standard NDA regarding my time at Meta, so I’ll need to skip anything involving proprietary internal workings, specific unreleased projects, or non-public metrics.

Github: TODO

detailed_questions: 1. What vivid fact, problem, or provocative question will hook listeners?

Despite the term existing for over a decade, "Data Engineer" is still a massive umbrella term that means everything and nothing. I’m constantly challenged on LinkedIn by people saying DEs are "just plumbers," or "the powerhouses of analytics," or that they "must or must not" do dashboards. There are a lot of misconceptions and talk about "how" we do things, rather than "why" or what actually happens as a result. It’s like saying a Software Engineer "just codes"—it misses the point entirely and leads to massive misalignment.
2. What quick stat or story first made you feel this topic matters?

Moving from the tech bubble to consulting with all kinds of corporations—non-tech-natives included—was a wake-up call. I’ve sat with senior data leaders who honestly didn't know the difference between a Data Engineer and an Analytics Engineer, or where Data Architects fit into the equation.

For experts in the field, especially those who are ambitious and up-to-date with trends, this may sound ridiculous. We take things for granted and often aren't aware that what we think is "natural" would be groundbreaking or controversial in other companies.
3. If someone had 10 seconds, which pain point would make them keep listening?

Hiring a Data Engineer can be a game-changer or a complete waste of money. Similarly, breaking into Data Engineering might be the best or worst career move you ever make. Context is king, yet we tend to simplify things and strip them of context until they become "must-have" tools or titles that don't actually serve the business.
4. What key insight or skill will listeners gain?

Real-world clarity on the DE role: the specializations, the career paths, the legacy traps, and what the future will bring. You’ll learn how to align your actual strengths with the right version of the role instead of getting lost in misleading titles.
5. Who needs this advice most right now?
Early-career engineers and those outside the "tech bubble" (classic corps).
Hiring managers and senior leadership without a hands-on data background.
Anyone currently unhappy in their DE journey and looking to pivot.
6. Who wastes time or money if they ignore this?
Companies hiring Data Engineers just because it’s a "must-have" title for data teams, without a plan for what they actually need them to build.
Individuals trying to "learn every tool" instead of picking a lane.
7. Is there a beginner group that keeps asking you about this?
Hiring Managers, Transformation Leads, and CDOs are often confused about the "why," while newcomers are overwhelmed by the "how" because they lack the big-picture context.
8. What 3–5 milestones or ideas should we cover, in order?
Data Engineer as an umbrella term hiding specific specializations.
The "Artisan" vs. "Factory" working models for analytics teams.
The future of the role with AI at the doorstep.
9. What’s your role, and what single metric or result links you to this topic?

As an independent consultant, I work with a wide variety of data teams. Exposure to this broad range of companies and backgrounds inspired me to consolidate that knowledge into an extensive description of the modern data engineer.

But this isn't just about helping companies; it's about individuals, too. The biggest mistake companies make is hiring without understanding the role. That leads to misleading job descriptions and ridiculous expectations—where an IC arrives ready to build but spends their day exporting dashboards to Excel. That is a direct path to burnout.
10. How does your current job shape your viewpoint here?

I’ve been the IC in the trenches and the manager at Meta. Having worked at both data-driven giants and "old-school" corporations, I’ve seen the role from its inception to the present day. That perspective allowed me to step out of the bubble and acquire a unique viewpoint on what works across the board.
11. If listeners have 15 minutes, what tiny first step should they take?
Hiring Managers: Audit your job description. Is it a copy-paste from ChatGPT, or do you actually know what this person will be doing on Tuesday morning? Do you know what talent you really need?

Engineers: Stop obsessing over titles. Map your actual strengths to the work you enjoy. Align your responsibilities with your strengths rather than trying to fit a generic, and often constraining, DE mold.
12. What small action, resource, or reflection would you recommend next?

Check out my ebook. Whether you're a manager trying to fix your hiring process or an engineer trying to find your direction, it’s designed to bring clarity to what this role actually is in 2026 and beyond.

event_description: Title: Data Engineer Career in 2026: Roles, Specializations, and What Companies Look For
Even though the Data Engineer title has been around for a long time, it can mean different things across companies.

In this episode, we’ll explore the reality of the Data Engineer role in 2026 with Slawomir Tulski, a Data Engineer who spent much of his career at Meta, where he moved between individual contributor and manager roles and conducted hundreds of technical interviews while helping shape the company’s Data Engineering hiring process.

Today, Slawomir works as an independent consultant, advising companies on structuring data teams and maximizing value from their Data Engineering efforts. His recent ebook examines the current Data Engineering landscape and why the role is often misunderstood across the industry.
In this conversation, we will discuss:
Why does the term Data Engineer still create confusion across the industry
The different specializations inside Data Engineering and how they differ in practice
How the same role changes depending on company maturity and team structure
What companies look for when hiring Data Engineers
Common career traps and misleading job descriptions that engineers should watch for
How the role may evolve as AI transforms the data ecosystem​
About the Speaker:

Slawomir Tulski is a Data Engineer who spent the bulk of his career at Meta, moving between Individual Contributor and Manager roles. During his time there, he focused on scaling the Data Engineering support for the Meta Ads ranking system. Beyond his direct team, he was also heavily involved in global hiring, conducting hundreds of interviews and helping shape the company's data engineering recruitment process.
Today, he works as an independent consultant, helping businesses get more out of their Data Engineering teams. On the personal side, he’s a dad, a husband, and a failed bass player.
speech: Hi everyone! This week, we're going to talk with Slawomir Tulski about the reality of the data engineer role in 2026.

Slawomir spent most of his career at Meta. He’s moved between individual contributor and management positions, focusing on scaling data engineering support for the Meta Ads ranking system. He’s also contributed to global hiring by conducting hundreds of interviews and helping shape the company's data engineer recruitment process.

Now, he works as an independent consultant, helping businesses make the most of their data engineering teams. On the personal side, Slawomir is a dad, a husband, and he jokes about being a failed bass player.
Let’s welcome Slawomir!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))

You conducted many interviews while working at Meta and now advise companies as a consultant. What capabilities actually matter when hiring Data Engineers?
What are the most common mistakes companies make when defining or hiring for Data Engineering roles?
For engineers considering Data Engineering, how should they think about choosing a direction instead of trying to learn every tool in the ecosystem?
What signals in a job description or interview process suggest that a Data Engineering role may be poorly defined?
As AI changes the data landscape, how do you expect the Data Engineer role to evolve over the next few years?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

Hiring a Data Engineer can be a game-changer or a complete waste of money. Similarly, breaking into Data Engineering might be the best or worst career move you ever make. Context is king, yet we tend to simplify things and strip them of context until they become "must-have" tools or titles that don't actually serve the business.
4. What key insight or skill will listeners gain?
Real-world clarity on the DE role: the specializations, the career paths, the legacy traps, and what the future will bring. You’ll learn how to align your actual strengths with the right version of the role instead of getting lost in misleading titles.
5. Who needs this advice most right now?
Early-career engineers and those outside the "tech bubble" (classic corps).
Hiring managers and senior leadership without a hands-on data background.
Anyone currently unhappy in their DE journey and looking to pivot.
6. Who wastes time or money if they ignore this?
Companies hiring Data Engineers just because it’s a "must-have" title for data teams, without a plan for what they actually need them to build.
Individuals trying to "learn every tool" instead of picking a lane.
7. Is there a beginner group that keeps asking you about this?
Hiring Managers, Transformation Leads, and CDOs are often confused about the "why," while newcomers are overwhelmed by the "how" because they lack the big-picture context.
8. What 3–5 milestones or ideas should we cover, in order?
Data Engineer as an umbrella term hiding specific specializations.
The "Artisan" vs. "Factory" working models for analytics teams.
The future of the role with AI at the doorstep.
9. What’s your role, and what single metric or result links you to this topic?
As an independent consultant, I work with a wide variety of data teams. Exposure to this broad range of companies and backgrounds inspired me to consolidate that knowledge into an extensive description of the modern data engineer.
But this isn't just about helping companies; it's about individuals, too. The biggest mistake companies make is hiring without understanding the role. That leads to misleading job descriptions and ridiculous expectations—where an IC arrives ready to build but spends their day exporting dashboards to Excel. That is a direct path to burnout.
10. How does your current job shape your viewpoint here?
I’ve been the IC in the trenches and the manager at Meta. Having worked at both data-driven giants and "old-school" corporations, I’ve seen the role from its inception to the present day. That perspective allowed me to step out of the bubble and acquire a unique viewpoint on what works across the board.
11. If listeners have 15 minutes, what tiny first step should they take?
Hiring Managers: Audit your job description. Is it a copy-paste from ChatGPT, or do you actually know what this person will be doing on Tuesday morning? Do you know what talent you really need?
Engineers: Stop obsessing over titles. Map your actual strengths to the work you enjoy. Align your responsibilities with your strengths rather than trying to fit a generic, and often constraining, DE mold.
12. What small action, resource, or reflection would you recommend next?
Check out my ebook. Whether you're a manager trying to fix your hiring process or an engineer trying to find your direction, it’s designed to bring clarity to what this role actually is in 2026 and beyond.

Title: Data Engineer Career in 2026: Roles, Specializations, and What Companies Look For
Even though the Data Engineer title has been around for a long time, it can mean different things across companies.
In this episode, we’ll explore the reality of the Data Engineer role in 2026 with Slawomir Tulski, a Data Engineer who spent much of his career at Meta, where he moved between individual contributor and manager roles and conducted hundreds of technical interviews while helping shape the company’s Data Engineering hiring process.
Today, Slawomir works as an independent consultant, advising companies on structuring data teams and maximizing value from their Data Engineering efforts. His recent ebook examines the current Data Engineering landscape and why the role is often misunderstood across the industry.
In this conversation, we will discuss:
Why does the term Data Engineer still create confusion across the industry
The different specializations inside Data Engineering and how they differ in practice
How the same role changes depending on company maturity and team structure
What companies look for when hiring Data Engineers
Common career traps and misleading job descriptions that engineers should watch for
How the role may evolve as AI transforms the data ecosystem​
About the Speaker:
Slawomir Tulski is a Data Engineer who spent the bulk of his career at Meta, moving between Individual Contributor and Manager roles. During his time there, he focused on scaling the Data Engineering support for the Meta Ads ranking system. Beyond his direct team, he was also heavily involved in global hiring, conducting hundreds of interviews and helping shape the company's data engineering recruitment process.
Today, he works as an independent consultant, helping businesses get more out of their Data Engineering teams. On the personal side, he’s a dad, a husband, and a failed bass player.
Speech
Hi everyone! This week, we're going to talk with Slawomir Tulski about the reality of the data engineer role in 2026.
Slawomir spent most of his career at Meta. He’s moved between individual contributor and management positions, focusing on scaling data engineering support for the Meta Ads ranking system. He’s also contributed to global hiring by conducting hundreds of interviews and helping shape the company's data engineer recruitment process.
Now, he works as an independent consultant, helping businesses make the most of their data engineering teams. On the personal side, Slawomir is a dad, a husband, and he jokes about being a failed bass player.
Let’s welcome Slawomir!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
The title Data Engineer has existed for more than a decade, yet it still means different things in different companies. Why has the industry not converged on a clear definition of the role?
When you look across different companies, what does the day-to-day work of a Data Engineer typically involve?
You argue that Data Engineering is not a single job but a family of roles. What are the main specializations you see in practice, and how do they differ?
How does the Data Engineer role change depending on company maturity, team structure, or industry?
You describe different operating models for data teams. Can you explain what the “artisan vs factory” model is and how it affects the work of Data Engineers?
You conducted many interviews while working at Meta and now advise companies as a consultant. What capabilities actually matter when hiring Data Engineers?
What are the most common mistakes companies make when defining or hiring for Data Engineering roles?
For engineers considering Data Engineering, how should they think about choosing a direction instead of trying to learn every tool in the ecosystem?
What signals in a job description or interview process suggest that a Data Engineering role may be poorly defined?
As AI changes the data landscape, how do you expect the Data Engineer role to evolve over the next few years?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

general_questions: How would you like us to introduce you?

Nasser Qadri is an Engineering Manager (and formerly an AI Engineer) at Google and a recognized leader in applied artificial intelligence, data science, and emerging technologies. With over a decade of experience spanning highly regulated industries, big tech, and national security, Nasser has built and led high-impact AI teams focused on generative AI, ML, and intelligent decision systems.As a former founder, Director of Data Science at Quest Diagnostics, and leader at Deloitte, Nasser has helped organizations use their data to build AI solutions and optimize billion-dollar operations. His recent work includes building AI-powered conversational experiences and next-gen enterprise workflows for highly regulated industries.Holding a PhD in Politics and International Relations, Nasser is known for integrating social science expertise with engineering rigor - translating cutting-edge AI into products that drive real world outcomes.

At work, my big focus right now is on introducing agentic workflows in the security space -
Outside of Google, I have a big interest in the intersection of AI and geopolitics, and how the geopolitics affects what we (engineers) build and focus on.

Path for people outside of “traditional engineering” to get into engineering roles
How do we build agentic systems that understand institutional context and not just tasks?

team_tab: Title: Understanding the AI Engineer Role
What does the AI engineer role look like in practice?
In this conversation, we explore the role through the career path of Nasser Qadri, AI Engineer at Google.

Nasser has an unusual path into the field. He started with a PhD in Politics and International Relations, then moved into data science roles at Gallup, Deloitte, and Quest Diagnostics, before transitioning into AI engineering.
Using his experience, we discuss how the AI engineer role appears in real projects and how people can move into it from different backgrounds.
In this conversation, we cover:
What the “AI engineer” role looks like in practice
Typical tasks and responsibilities in the role
How the role differs from ML engineer, data scientist, and software engineer
Skills that are useful for working with modern AI systems
Nasser’s transition from social science to data science and AI engineering
bio: Nasser Qadri is an AI Engineer at Google. Previously, he held data science leadership roles at Quest Diagnostics, Deloitte, and Gallup. Before moving into industry, Nasser completed a PhD in Politics and International Relations at the University of Glasgow. His background combines social science research, data science, and applied machine learning.
speech: This week we’ll talk about the AI engineer role: what it looks like in practice, what kinds of work it involves, and how people transition into it.
We have a special guest today, Nasser Qadri.

Nasser is an Engineering Manager, and formerly an AI Engineer at Google. Previously, he held data science leadership roles at Quest Diagnostics, Deloitte, and Gallup, working across domains such as healthcare, public sector, and national security.

Before moving into industry, Nasser completed a PhD in Politics and International Relations at the University of Glasgow. His background combines social science research, data science, and applied machine learning.
Welcome, Nasser!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
You started your career in political science and international relations. What originally led you from that path into data science and eventually AI engineering?
Your background combines social science and machine learning. How has your training as a political scientist influenced the way you approach technical problems?
Many people still struggle to understand what an “AI engineer” actually does. Based on your experience, how would you describe the role in practical terms?
What does a typical project for an AI engineer look like, from the initial problem to a working system?
You’ve worked across several industries, including healthcare, consulting, and public sector organizations. How does the role of AI or data work change across these domains?
Before becoming an AI engineer, you led data science teams. What differences did you notice between data science work and the kind of engineering work involved in modern AI systems?
In your experience, where do AI engineers typically spend most of their time: modeling, infrastructure, experimentation, or something else?
Today many AI systems rely heavily on LLMs, APIs, and tools rather than building models from scratch. How has this changed the skill set required for the role?
For someone coming from a non-technical or semi-technical background, what parts of the transition into AI engineering are usually the most difficult?
You’ve worked both in government-related environments and in private industry. Do the expectations for data and AI systems differ in these contexts?
Your career spans analytics leadership, research, and engineering. How did you decide when it was the right time to move from management and data science roles into hands-on engineering work?
Looking back at your own learning path, what skills or concepts turned out to be much more important than you initially expected?
Many people entering AI today focus heavily on frameworks and tools. In your opinion, what deeper foundations are still essential to understand?
What kinds of problems are particularly well-suited for AI systems today, and where do you still see limitations in practice?
For someone who is early in their career and interested in AI engineering, what kinds of projects or experiences would help them understand whether this path is right for them?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

event_description: Title: Starting a Data Conference: The Data Makers Fest Story
In this episode, we talk with Leonid Kholkine, co-founder of Data Makers Fest and Head of R&D at DareData.

Leonid’s career spans research, engineering, startups, and community building. He completed a PhD in machine learning applied to sports, worked on real-world AI systems across several industries, and helped launch multiple initiatives around data and innovation, including World Data League and Data Lead Club.
He is also one of the people behind Data Makers Fest, a conference created for practitioners who want to learn from real projects and real experience.
In this conversation, we discuss:
Leonid’s path from engineering to applied AI research
His work on machine learning in sports and applied data systems
How the idea for Data Makers Fest started

guest_tab: These notes help us draft focused questions and an event description.
general_questions: How would you like us to introduce you?

Tatiana Habruseva, Ph.D. is a Staff Software Engineer, Machine Learning at LinkedIn, where she leads applied R&D on recommendation systems, multimodal alignment, and AI systems benchmarking. Before joining LinkedIn, she developed AI-based decision support systems for labor monitoring at Cork University Maternity Hospital. Her work on Weighted Boxes Fusion, a method for combining predictions from object detection models, has been cited over 700 times and ranks in the top 0.1% cited publications in computer science. Tatiana is a Kaggle Competitions Master and the 1st prize winner of the international Sound Demixing Challenge 2023. She holds a Ph.D. in Applied Physics from Cork Institute of Technology, is a Senior IEEE Member, and has authored over 27 peer-reviewed publications in computer science and physics.
What are you currently focused on—projects or themes you’d enjoy discussing?
Kaggle and machine learning competitions; - how it works, proc and cons, when why to go on Kaggle, other competition platforms (AICrowd; topcoder)
Career advancements through competitions; - what to do and why; why winning competition is not enough, and what to do after you took part in competitions to promote your results and build portfolio
Challenges of AI systems benchmarking and agentic AI evaluation - why it’s getting harder to evaluate modern AI systems and AI agents; difficulty and importance of evolving benchmarks.
Responsible AI - what is this area about in reality; how is it governed and monitored; techniques and pitfalls
Which topics are you most keen to cover?
When and why to take part in ML competitions; proc and cons, Kaggle platforms and other platforms (i.e. topcoder), their procs and cons, see Li post:
https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tatigabru_kaggle-activity-7431239930309369856-Msnp?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAACZjXPsB1DR3DAarzweMrVrMw3VgvgNaNOs

How to use machine learning competitions for advancing your career: winning is not enough, you can use even non-winning results to build your portfolio of research papers /conferences/ blog-posts, build your github, and this will help your career a way more than the winning place itself.
How I personally used Kaggle competitions to publish my most-cited research papers - some personal examples - weighted based fusion method, pneumonia detection, 3D CNN
How those Kaggle competitions and SpaceNet challenges on topcoder converted into job offers, and my first consulting project at 500 euro/day.
How we used AICrowd challenge 2023 recently to get 2 published papers (2024) and one preprint under submission (now under review).
Changes since the last interview: a new baby-boy and relocation to Silicon Valley
Recent projects: benchmarks and datasets for music source separation (paper under review but there are links),
dataset for product descriptions in collaboration with Adapt (media coverage available).
Q: What are the current challenges of benchmarking? Why do we need evolving benchmarks?
Why it’s getting harder to evaluate modern AI systems and AI agents; difficulty and importance of evolving benchmarks: contamination, saturation.
Are there any topics you’d rather skip or avoid discussing publicly?
Cannot go into too much details on the work topics - how MSFT is ensuring responsible AI, which systems exactly Linkedin uses in productions, etc.

That doing good is not enough, marketing and pr is required everywhere. You need to promote your results further with blog post, github open source, marketing via social media and communities, meetup talks, and making arxiv/ or proper peer-reviewed research papers -> all this on top of a result will bring opportunities
Who needs this advice most right now?
Those who transition their career/ start career/ those feel they are stuck
Who wastes time or money if they ignore this?
Is there a beginner group that keeps asking you about this?
Those who want to transition career, struck in their career or take maternity leave
What 3-5 milestones or ideas should we cover, in order?

Career: You can leverage competitions like kaggle, topcoder, aicrowd platform, to promote your career and open source if you play it right. Kaggle (topcoder, aicrowd) can bring you research papers + open source -> this can get you in the interview or can get you a job
Career: Competition env are not real production env - treat it like a gym; yet please do not get hooked, there are proc and cons

Career: Doing great on competitions is not enough - marketing is the main differentiator - show your work, do blog posts, social media, meetunds… - same for your projects at work - show off! Do not be shy or “modest”, this is a graveyard.
Career part: The skills needed at the age of AI: about even more growing importance of soft skills, and debugging as a true skill;
Tech part: Debugging as a top skill and Data bebudding: data is everything (almost)
Tech part: Evaluations and benchmarks are needed to make a decision between System A and System B on which one is better. By "better," we mean more closely aligned with our intended human goal.

Tech part: Benchmarks challenges: Open benchmarks get leaked into train set and overfitted, this is why we cleared closer benchmark datasets for our music source separation project (you cannot download true labels, we run the eval on server, MVSep)

Tech part: As AI systems keeps improving - the benchmarks get “solved”- meaning they become too easy and do not help to distinguish between models/ systems anymore - so we need to keep going with more complex tasks and benchmarks as AI keeps improving
Evan and benchmarks are supposed to ensure Alignment to human: what are the problems with alignment; -> responsible AI field and what it entails
What’s your role, and what single metric or result links you to this topic?
I work as a staff engineer in machine learning at Core AI, Linkedin, driving research projects.I also served as a responsible AI champion at linkedin before maternity leave.
How does your current job shape your viewpoint here?
If listeners have 15 minutes, what tiny first step should they take?
Assess what they’ve done so far and think how they can promote it. Ask LLM to draft blog-posts based on their work/ github repo, etc.; Join meetup
What small action, resource, or reflection would you recommend next?
Books: Rise, https://www.amazon.com/Rise-Practical-Advancing-Career-Standing/dp/1607742608
Alignment: "Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control" by Stuart Russell (2019).

speech: This week, we’re talking about how ML competitions can help you grow your career, how to turn technical work into real opportunities, and why evaluation and benchmarks have become such a big challenge in AI.
Our guest today is Tatiana Habruseva.

Tatiana is a Staff Software Engineer in Machine Learning at LinkedIn, where she works on recommendation systems, multimodal alignment, and AI systems benchmarking. She is also a Kaggle Competitions Master, the winner of the Sound Demixing Challenge 2023, and the author of more than 27 peer-reviewed publications.
Tatiana, welcome back to the podcast!
Opening

Before we go into our main topic, let’s start with your background. This is your third time on the podcast. What has changed in your life and career since we last spoke, especially with becoming a mother and relocating to Silicon Valley?
Career growth through competitions
You've really used competitions wisely throughout your career. So, what do you think makes machine learning competitions not just useful for learning, but also for actually advancing in one’s career?
When you compare platforms like Kaggle, AIcrowd, and Topcoder, what do you see as the main differences? Who do you think each platform is best suited for?
You've mentioned before that competition settings don't really mirror real-world production environments. What do you think are some common misconceptions people have about that?
You've emphasized that simply winning isn't the end goal. For folks who compete, what steps should they take afterward if they want to turn their experience into genuine career opportunities?
How can someone who may not win still leverage their competition results to create a solid portfolio?
Speaking from your own experience, how did you manage to turn your competition involvement into published papers, open-source projects, consulting gigs, or even job offers?
Transition to evaluation
One of your recent competition projects led into benchmark creation and evaluation work. How did that transition happen for you?
Evaluation and benchmarks
Why is it becoming tougher to assess modern AI systems and agents?
What shifts when we go from looking at a single model to evaluating an entire AI system or agent?
What makes a benchmark valuable these days, and why do they need to change over time?
Responsible AI
Many people come across the term “responsible AI,” but its meaning often remains unclear. So, what does this field actually entail in practice?
How does the process of evaluation relate to responsible AI and ensuring alignment with human goals?
Additionally, where do you think the most significant disconnect lies between public discussions about responsible AI and its implementation within actual organizations?
Advice and closing
For someone trying to break into AI or move forward in their career, what is the highest-leverage way to make their work visible?
Looking ahead, which area do you think will become even more important over the next few years: competitions, evaluation, benchmarks, or responsible AI?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

To make your work visible:- Create GitHub repo !!! (any place on leaderboard is ok to do that)- Write up in the form of blog-post - promote it via your network (no need to win the competition)- Go further to the conference paper/research paper (if you have some innovative methods, approaches) - be at the top of the leaderboard, not necessarily a winner
- team up with the academic if you want to produce a nice research paper
- consider taking part in research competitions (see AICrowd, Grand challenges) -> can present your outputs and become a co-author without authoring experience
Resources:

Rise: 3 Practical Steps for Advancing Your Career, Standing Out as a Leader, and Liking Your Life (Ten Speed). By Patty Azzarello (https://www.porchlightbooks.com/pages/author/Patty_Azzarello-16156396) - awesome book about why doing good is not enough, and what else you need to do to promote your career (same applies to competitions as well)
Platforms we touched:
AICrowd https://www.aicrowd.com/challenges
Grand challenges: https://grand-challenge.org/challenges/
Kaggle competitions: https://www.kaggle.com/competitions
TopCoder challenge SpaceNet 9:
https://www.topcoder.com/challenges/9620f66a-767e-40ac-81d5-5cc61274b186
(no current active competitions, but they appear)
Writing about your competition participation (participation, not necessarily the win):
My medium blog post with instructions: Writing papers/tech reports after Kaggle competitions (https://medium.com/data-science/writing-papers-tech-reports-after-kaggle-competitions-ee504fc0c4c1)

Kaggle Solution Write-Up Documentation (https://www.kaggle.com/solution-write-up-documentation), Rubrics (criteria) and example provided - and can be leveraged for LLM instruction to help polish your write up
Papers about AI agents rocking on Kaggle:

October 2024. MLE-bench (https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.07095): Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering. The authors built a benchmark from 75 Kaggle competitions and found that their best setup (OpenAI's o1-preview with AIDE scaffolding) reached at least Kaggle bronze-medal level in 16.9% of competitions.

July 2025. MLE-STAR: Machine Learning Engineering Agent via Search and Targeted Refinement (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.15692v2): MLE-STAR achieves medals in 64% of the Kaggle competitions on the MLE-bench Lite\\

July 2025. AI Research Agents for Machine Learning: Search, Exploration, and Generalization in MLE-bench (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.02554). Our best pairing of search strategy and operator set achieves a state-of-the-art result on MLE-bench lite, increasing the success rate of achieving a Kaggle medal from 39.6% to 47.7%.

Career: You can leverage competitions like kaggle, topcoder, aicrowd platform, to promote your career and open source if you play it right. Kaggle (topcoder, aicrowd) can bring you research papers + open source -> this can get you in the interview or can get you a job
Career: Competition env are not real production env - treat it like a gym; yet please do not get hooked, there are proc and cons
Career: Doing great on competitions is not enough - marketing is the main differentiator - show your work, do blog posts, social media, meetunds… - same for your projects at work - show off! Do not be shy or “modest”, this is a graveyard.
Career part: The skills needed at the age of AI: about even more growing importance of soft skills, and debugging as a true skill;
Tech part: Debugging as a top skill and Data bebudding: data is everything (almost)
Tech part: Evaluations and benchmarks are needed to make a decision between System A and System B on which one is better. By "better," we mean more closely aligned with our intended human goal.
Tech part: Benchmarks challenges: Open benchmarks get leaked into train set and overfitted, this is why we cleared closer benchmark datasets for our music source separation project (you cannot download true labels, we run the eval on server, MVSep)
Tech part: As AI systems keeps improving - the benchmarks get “solved”- meaning they become too easy and do not help to distinguish between models/ systems anymore - so we need to keep going with more complex tasks and benchmarks as AI keeps improving
Evan and benchmarks are supposed to ensure Alignment to human: what are the problems with alignment; -> responsible AI field and what it entails
What’s your role, and what single metric or result links you to this topic?
I work as a staff engineer in machine learning at Core AI, Linkedin, driving research projects.I also served as a responsible AI champion at linkedin before maternity leave.
How does your current job shape your viewpoint here?
If listeners have 15 minutes, what tiny first step should they take?
Assess what they’ve done so far and think how they can promote it. Ask LLM to draft blog-posts based on their work/ github repo, etc.; Join meetup
What small action, resource, or reflection would you recommend next?
Books: Rise, https://www.amazon.com/Rise-Practical-Advancing-Career-Standing/dp/1607742608
Alignment: "Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control" by Stuart Russell (2019).
Eval: todo
https://scale.com/blog/rubrics-as-rewards

Episode in 3 lines

Part 2: why evaluating modern AI systems is getting harder

Speech
This week, we’re talking about how ML competitions can help you grow your career, how to turn technical work into real opportunities, and why evaluation and benchmarks have become such a big challenge in AI.
Our guest today is Tatiana Habruseva.
Tatiana is a Staff Software Engineer in Machine Learning at LinkedIn, where she works on recommendation systems, multimodal alignment, and AI systems benchmarking. She is also a Kaggle Competitions Master, the winner of the Sound Demixing Challenge 2023, and the author of more than 27 peer-reviewed publications.
Tatiana, welcome back to the podcast!
Opening
Before we go into our main topic, let’s start with your background. This is your third time on the podcast. What has changed in your life and career since we last spoke, especially with becoming a mother and relocating to Silicon Valley?
Career growth through competitions
You've really used competitions wisely throughout your career. So, what do you think makes machine learning competitions not just useful for learning, but also for actually advancing in one’s career?
When you compare platforms like Kaggle, AIcrowd, and Topcoder, what do you see as the main differences? Who do you think each platform is best suited for?
You've mentioned before that competition settings don't really mirror real-world production environments. What do you think are some common misconceptions people have about that?
You've emphasized that simply winning isn't the end goal. For folks who compete, what steps should they take afterward if they want to turn their experience into genuine career opportunities?
How can someone who may not win still leverage their competition results to create a solid portfolio?
Speaking from your own experience, how did you manage to turn your competition involvement into published papers, open-source projects, consulting gigs, or even job offers?
Transition to evaluation
One of your recent competition projects led into benchmark creation and evaluation work. How did that transition happen for you?
Evaluation and benchmarks
Why is it becoming tougher to assess modern AI systems and agents?
What shifts when we go from looking at a single model to evaluating an entire AI system or agent?
What makes a benchmark valuable these days, and why do they need to change over time?
Responsible AI
Many people come across the term “responsible AI,” but its meaning often remains unclear. So, what does this field actually entail in practice?
How does the process of evaluation relate to responsible AI and ensuring alignment with human goals?
Additionally, where do you think the most significant disconnect lies between public discussions about responsible AI and its implementation within actual organizations?
Advice and closing
For someone trying to break into AI or move forward in their career, what is the highest-leverage way to make their work visible?
Looking ahead, which area do you think will become even more important over the next few years: competitions, evaluation, benchmarks, or responsible AI?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
To make your work visible:- Create GitHub repo !!! (any place on leaderboard is ok to do that)- Write up in the form of blog-post - promote it via your network (no need to win the competition)- Go further to the conference paper/research paper (if you have some innovative methods, approaches) - be at the top of the leaderboard, not necessarily a winner
- team up with the academic if you want to produce a nice research paper
- consider taking part in research competitions (see AICrowd, Grand challenges) -> can present your outputs and become a co-author without authoring experience
Resources:
Rise: 3 Practical Steps for Advancing Your Career, Standing Out as a Leader, and Liking Your Life (Ten Speed). By Patty Azzarello (https://www.porchlightbooks.com/pages/author/Patty_Azzarello-16156396) - awesome book about why doing good is not enough, and what else you need to do to promote your career (same applies to competitions as well)
Platforms we touched:
AICrowd https://www.aicrowd.com/challenges
Grand challenges: https://grand-challenge.org/challenges/
Kaggle competitions: https://www.kaggle.com/competitions
TopCoder challenge SpaceNet 9:
https://www.topcoder.com/challenges/9620f66a-767e-40ac-81d5-5cc61274b186
(no current active competitions, but they appear)
Writing about your competition participation (participation, not necessarily the win):
My medium blog post with instructions: Writing papers/tech reports after Kaggle competitions (https://medium.com/data-science/writing-papers-tech-reports-after-kaggle-competitions-ee504fc0c4c1)
Kaggle Solution Write-Up Documentation (https://www.kaggle.com/solution-write-up-documentation), Rubrics (criteria) and example provided - and can be leveraged for LLM instruction to help polish your write up
Papers about AI agents rocking on Kaggle:
October 2024. MLE-bench (https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.07095): Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering. The authors built a benchmark from 75 Kaggle competitions and found that their best setup (OpenAI's o1-preview with AIDE scaffolding) reached at least Kaggle bronze-medal level in 16.9% of competitions.
July 2025. MLE-STAR: Machine Learning Engineering Agent via Search and Targeted Refinement (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.15692v2): MLE-STAR achieves medals in 64% of the Kaggle competitions on the MLE-bench Lite\\
July 2025. AI Research Agents for Machine Learning: Search, Exploration, and Generalization in MLE-bench (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.02554). Our best pairing of search strategy and operator set achieves a state-of-the-art result on MLE-bench lite, increasing the success rate of achieving a Kaggle medal from 39.6% to 47.7%.

Podcast Guest Information: Mariano Semelman
​General Questions

​How would you like us to introduce you? "A Data Scientist and Machine Learning Engineer in a Lead position with over 10 years of experience in e-commerce. A practitioner who focuses on building end-to-end solutions and explores how agentic tools can make the transition from research to production more seamless."

​What are you currently focused on? "I'm currently focused on media solutions for sellers in the marketplace at OLX. Specifically, using AI to simplify the listing process—like taking a user’s video and automatically extracting the images and details needed to create a high-quality ad."
​Which topics are you most keen to cover?
​An Integrated Workflow: A view on how tools like Claude Code and Cursor help bridge the gap between initial data discovery and production-ready scripts.
​Media Solutions for Sellers: The practical challenge of turning raw video into structured marketplace data (Video-to-Ad).
​Pragmatic Engineering: Why a data scientist should know how to operate their own models (Docker, logging, resource management) to build truly extensible systems.
ms.
​Social Media Links:
​LinkedIn: (https://www.linkedin.com/in/msemelman)https://www.linkedin.com/in/msemelman
​GitHub: [Your Link]
​Template 1: Practical Focus

​What vivid fact, problem, or provocative question will hook listeners? "The smartest AI is the one the user never notices. In a marketplace like OLX, our goal isn't just to have a high-performing model; it’s to build an invisible bridge from a user's video to a completed ad. If the engineering is clunky, the AI is useless."

​What quick stat or story first made you feel this topic matters? "Building Video-to-Ad solutions taught me that the real world is messy. A model might work in a notebook, but handling diverse video formats at scale requires a Lead who understands the full stack. Seeing a seller successfully list an item without typing a word—because our system 'read' their video—is what made me realize the power of this end-to-end approach."

​What key insight or skill will listeners gain? "A perspective on how to use agentic coding tools—like Claude Code—to handle repetitive engineering tasks (SQL optimization, boilerplate, data discovery). This isn't about replacing the human element; it's about enhancing our ability to focus on the harder, more interesting architectural problems."

​Who needs this advice most right now? "Mid-to-senior level Data Scientists who want to be more self-sufficient in production environments, and engineers moving into the ML space who want to see how modern tools can speed up research."
​What 3-5 milestones or ideas should we cover, in order?
​The 'End-to-End' Habit: Why operating your own models (Docker, Monitoring) makes you a better scientist.
​Enhancing the Workflow: A view on using Claude Code and Cursor to accelerate data discovery and move from notebooks to production scripts.
​Video-to-Ad Solutions: How we help OLX sellers by extracting ad details and images directly from their video uploads.
​Tech Debt vs. Impact: Starting with a reliable system that works for the customer and using that success to pay down debt later.

​What’s your role, and what single metric or result links you to this topic? "In my Lead role, I focus on the reliability and extensibility of our media solutions. Success for me is when a complex feature—like extracting ad data from a video—runs predictably for millions of users without requiring constant manual intervention."

​If listeners have 15 minutes, what tiny first step should they take? "Try taking a task you usually do in a notebook—like a complex SQL query or a data cleaning step—and solve it using a terminal-based tool like Claude Code. See how much faster you can move toward a reusable script."
​Current Projects at OLX

​Video-to-Ad Extraction: The primary focus is helping sellers save time. When a user uploads a video of a product, the system identifies the best frames for the gallery and extracts relevant details to populate the ad automatically.
​Ad-to-Video: We also help enhance listings by generating short, engaging videos based on the seller's pictures, adding voiceovers and subtitles to help buyers.

​Production Philosophy: These solutions are built to be robust and scalable. Because downtime equals lost revenue in e-commerce, these tools are built with an emphasis on being maintainable and extensible.
team_tab: From Notebook to Production: Building End-to-End AI Systems

In this episode, we talk with Mariano Semelman about what it takes to build end-to-end AI solutions in production, especially in messy, high-scale e-commerce environments where a good model alone is not enough.

Mariano is a Lead Data Scientist and Machine Learning Engineer with more than 10 years of experience in e-commerce. At OLX, he works on media solutions for sellers, including systems that turn a user’s product video into a structured marketplace ad by automatically selecting images and extracting relevant details.
We’ll discuss:
Why data scientists should learn to operate their own models
How tools like Claude Code and Cursor can help bridge the gap between research and production
What it takes to build video-to-ad systems that are reliable and scalable
How to think about extensibility, monitoring, and maintainability in AI products
When to prioritize customer impact first and pay down tech debt later
About the Speaker:

Mariano Semelman is a Lead Data Scientist and Machine Learning Engineer with over 10 years of experience in e-commerce. He focuses on building end-to-end solutions and explores how agentic tools can make the path from research to production more seamless. At OLX, he works on AI-powered media solutions that simplify the listing process for sellers.
speech: This week, we’re talking about what it takes to move from notebooks and experiments to AI systems that work in production.
Our guest today is Mariano Semelman.

Mariano is a Lead Data Scientist and Machine Learning Engineer with more than 10 years of experience in e-commerce. At OLX, he works on media solutions for sellers, including systems that can turn a user’s video into a structured marketplace ad by extracting images and relevant details automatically.

We’ll talk about building end-to-end AI solutions, why data scientists should understand how their models run in production, and how tools like Claude Code and Cursor can help bridge the gap between research and production work.
Mariano, welcome to the podcast.
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
You describe yourself as someone who focuses on end-to-end solutions. What does that mean in practice in your role today at OLX?
A lot of work looks good in a notebook but becomes much harder in production. Where do you usually see that gap most clearly?
Why do you think it is important for a data scientist to understand not just the model, but also how it runs in production?
You’ve mentioned Docker, logging, and resource management as important skills. Why are these becoming essential for data scientists?
You’re interested in tools like Claude Code and Cursor. Where have you found them genuinely useful in moving from exploration to production-ready scripts?
What kinds of tasks do these tools help with best today, and what still clearly needs human judgment?
One of the most interesting problems you’re working on is turning a user’s video into a marketplace ad. Can you walk us through that problem end-to-end?
What makes a video-to-ad system much harder to build in the real world than it sounds at first?
When you build something like this for a marketplace, how do you define success beyond model quality?
You mentioned reliability and extensibility as core principles. What do those mean in practice when you’re building AI systems for millions of users?
There is often a trade-off between shipping something useful now and building a cleaner long-term system. How do you decide when it is acceptable to take on tech debt?
For mid-to-senior data scientists who want to become more self-sufficient in production environments, what should they start doing differently?
Looking ahead, what do you think will matter more: better models, better tools, or better engineering around models?
What is one belief you’ve changed your mind about when it comes to applied AI in production?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

There is often a trade-off between shipping something useful now and building a cleaner long-term system. How do you decide when it is acceptable to take on tech debt?
For mid-to-senior data scientists who want to become more self-sufficient in production environments, what should they start doing differently?
Looking ahead, what do you think will matter more: better models, better tools, or better engineering around models?
What is one belief you’ve changed your mind about when it comes to applied AI in production?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

team_tab: From GenAI Pilots to Production

In this episode, we talk with Nikita Kozodoi, PhD, Senior AI Scientist at AWS Generative AI Innovation Center, about what it means to build generative AI and agentic solutions for real business problems.

Nikita works at the intersection of applied AI research, customer-facing consulting, and production engineering. His team helps companies move from early GenAI ideas to working systems across industries such as finance, life sciences, healthcare, automotive, logistics, and education.
We’ll discuss:
How GenAI consulting works in practice
What kinds of problems companies bring to AWS
How teams decide whether an AI agent, RAG system, coding assistant, or document processing pipeline is the right fit.

The conversation also covers the practical side of building with LLMs: working with enterprise constraints, evaluating outputs, managing cost and latency, using self-reflection, handling hallucinations, and moving GenAI systems from proof of concept to production.
About the Speaker:

Nikita Kozodoi, PhD is a Senior Applied Scientist at the AWS Generative AI Innovation Center working on the frontier of AI research and business. Nikita builds custom AI and agentic solutions to solve real-world business problems for AWS customers across industries.
speech: Today, we’re talking about what it takes to move GenAI from pilots to production.

In this episode, we speak with Nikita Kozodoi, PhD, Senior Applied Scientist at the AWS Generative AI Innovation Center. Nikita works with companies across industries to design and build custom GenAI and agentic solutions for real business problems.
Welcome, Nikita!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
You work with companies across different industries on GenAI solutions. What kinds of problems do customers usually bring to your team?
When a company comes with a GenAI idea, how do you decide whether it is a good use case for LLMs in the first place?
What usually changes when companies try to move from a prototype to a production system?
What are the most common reasons GenAI pilots fail to reach production?
How do you choose between different solution patterns, such as RAG, agents, document processing pipelines, coding assistants, or deep research workflows?
In your projects, what enterprise constraints matter most: data privacy, security, latency, cost, governance, integrations, or something else?
How do you evaluate LLM-based systems?
How do you handle hallucinations in enterprise GenAI systems?
Based on your experience, what advice would you give to teams that want to move from GenAI experimentation to production this year?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

Notes on discussion for the episode:
Ivan:
topic proposal: adoption of AI in enterprise and the related consequences
Sub-topics:
Enterprise decision:
Pay for a license of ready made tools - Claud, Cursor etc
vs. use open source models and spend more time building infra around them, i.e. on top of https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm , https://ollama.com, https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp
If you pay for i.e. Claud:
Spend time on context engineering, learning tools (marketplace plugins, agent teams etc.)
Moving to Context Engineering to ensure better output quality:
I.e. your repo docs need to live next to the code
Go extra mile and give access to team chat, docs, meeting notes etc.
If you go open source:
Choose either fine-tuning open source models
Models develop so fast now that your fine tuning needs to happen often
Or RAG
You need a RAG framework (Llamaindex, LangChain, Haystack, Dify, RAGFlow, AnythingLLM etc)
And a vector database (Qdrant, pgvector, Pinecone, Weavite, Milvus, Chroma etc.)
Orchestration issues are on you (KV cache, pipelines, hosting etc.)
Mixing of both, i.e. with AWS Bedrock
Impact of broader AI adoption on perception of software engineering:
Everyone now found out that coding is not just coding - you need to architect, define requirements, think about tech debt, observability and testing, rollout, maintenance
So AI solved the coding part to an extent but everything else still needs to be done, this is the reason why engineers are still needed

Many companies encourage all roles to code and contribute to production but don’t do the upfront investment into the related release and monitoring automation, and forget that reviewing the code will cost other engineers more time
Developers need time to experiment with the setups to be more efficient
Broader industry impact
Many stopped hiring junior engineers, this is a problem that will only hit us later on as lack of replacement will be a problem
No more training data produced, we stopped asking questions online, so AI can’t learn from new questions - model collapse as a concept and what we can do about it
AI is writing code only it can understand
Now when we write a project, we can tweak and fix it for the most part ourselves
When AI writes a project, we usually don’t understand much or need a lot of time to understand the code, so it’s then easier to ask AI again to rewrite everything again
Gets even more complex with projects like https://veralang.dev/
Shift Right: AI creates more work - more code to review, more output to review
If non-engineers commit code - even more time needed for review
For non-engineering work - work slop means the output is not as good as if an expert did it and requires more more time to review and i.e. verify sources
Orchestration tax
You have to put everything AI gives you together, proof it, fix it, redo etc. Often this takes as much time as if you’d have done the work yourself
Tokenmaxxing vs. valuemaxxing
Instead of using as many tokens as possible (https://www.businessinsider.com/jensen-huang-500k-engineers-250k-ai-tokens-nvidia-compute-2026-3) maximise value and outcomes created
AI security
Necessary guardrails:
PII filtering
Topic blocking
Tone control
Many examples of this backfiring in real world conversations
Output constraints
Ideas:
AI security tools https://www.anthropic.com/glasswing
Broader repercussions: https://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence/anthropic-claude-mythos-escaped-sandbox
https://cybernews.com/ai-news/ai-models-scheme-protect-other-models/
Impact of AI on recent leaks and incidents, i.e. claude code leak, axios etc.
Crazy hacks like https://neuraltrust.ai/blog/grok-morse-code
Stance from security experts: AI can do discovery of issues where there is either no incentive for real world experts or it’s actually not encouraged
Impact of AI on mental health of engineers
Concept of token anxiety https://writing.nikunjk.com/p/token-anxiety; AI psychosis as a new term
AI in some companies became an objective and not a tool
Moving to Context Engineering to ensure better output quality
Great success of AI integration: data analytics and data visualisation tools like Snowflake and Hex have integrated AI agents you can use to speed up data analysis and visualisation
Broader societal impact
Dead internet theory, tools like https://github.com/jamiepine/voicebox or imagegen are getting indistinguishable from real life footage
Internal setups and tweaks to make agents more useful
OpenClaw and other alternatives
Setups like https://github.com/russelleNVy/three-man-team
Optimising token use, i.e. with https://github.com/cancerit/CaVEMan
Effects of claude.md and agents.md https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
Other tweaks combining models https://claude.com/blog/the-advisor-strategy

team_tab: AI Adoption in Enterprise Beyond Writing Code
In this episode, we speak with Ivan Bilan, Senior Engineering Manager at Personio, about the transition of AI adoption from experimental phases to routine engineering tasks.

AI can assist with implementation, but software engineering teams still need to handle several critical responsibilities: defining requirements, designing architecture, managing technical debt, testing systems, monitoring production, reviewing changes, planning rollouts, and maintaining their outputs.
We’ll discuss:
How engineering managers can approach AI adoption in enterprise settings.
Where AI can expedite team processes and where it may introduce hidden challenges.
Why companies must be cautious about encouraging everyone to contribute code without investing in processes for releases, review capabilities, observability, and maintenance.

We also explore the wider implications of AI adoption within the software industry, including reduced junior hiring opportunities, evolving expectations for engineers, risks associated with AI-generated systems that may be difficult for humans to comprehend, security concerns, and the shift from “using more tokens” to creating greater value.
bio: Ivan Bilan is a Senior Engineering Manager at Personio, leading multiple teams in the Identity and Access Management (IAM) domain. Previously, he served as a Data Science & Engineering Manager at TrustYou, where he led NLP and infrastructure groups to optimize massive ETL and ML pipelines. With an MS in Computational Linguistics from LMU Munich and a background at CDTM, Ivan bridges the gap between deep technical NLP research and senior leadership in high-throughput enterprise environments.
speech: Today, we’re discussing AI adoption in enterprise engineering teams. We’ll look at how AI changes perceptions of software engineering, common pitfalls in its adoption, and how to move from just using AI to creating value.

Our guest is Ivan Bilan, Senior Engineering Manager at Personio. He leads teams in Identity and Access Management and previously optimized ETL and ML pipelines at TrustYou. With a master’s in Computational Linguistics from LMU Munich, Ivan connects deep technical research with leadership in fast-paced environments.
Welcome, Ivan!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))

You’ve worked in NLP and data science before, and now you manage engineering teams in IAM. From your perspective, how has the current AI wave changed the way companies think about software engineering?
If AI can help with implementation, which parts of engineering still require strong human judgment?
In enterprise environments, what usually happens after someone generates code with AI? What are the next steps before it can become part of a real product?
Do you think AI-generated code changes the cost of code review? Can it make reviewing harder rather than easier?
How should engineering managers decide where AI can speed up a team and where it may create hidden work?
There is a lot of pressure to use more AI, more agents, and more tokens. How can teams avoid optimizing for AI usage itself and focus instead on business or engineering value?
What would you measure to understand whether AI is actually improving engineering work: delivery speed, quality, incidents, developer experience, cost, customer outcomes, or something else?
There is a trend of companies hiring fewer junior engineers or expecting juniors to become productive faster with AI. What long-term risks could this create for engineering teams?
Does AI change what junior engineers should learn first? For example, should they focus more on debugging, architecture, testing, or understanding systems?
What internal setup makes AI coding tools or agents more useful in real teams? For example, documentation, coding standards, repo structure, architecture notes, or files like claude.md and agents.md.
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

Go extra mile and give access to team chat, docs, meeting notes etc.
If you go open source:
Choose either fine-tuning open source models
Models develop so fast now that your fine tuning needs to happen often
Or RAG
You need a RAG framework (Llamaindex, LangChain, Haystack, Dify, RAGFlow, AnythingLLM etc)
And a vector database (Qdrant, pgvector, Pinecone, Weavite, Milvus, Chroma etc.)
Orchestration issues are on you (KV cache, pipelines, hosting etc.)
Mixing of both, i.e. with AWS Bedrock
Impact of broader AI adoption on perception of software engineering:
Everyone now found out that coding is not just coding - you need to architect, define requirements, think about tech debt, observability and testing, rollout, maintenance
So AI solved the coding part to an extent but everything else still needs to be done, this is the reason why engineers are still needed
Many companies encourage all roles to code and contribute to production but don’t do the upfront investment into the related release and monitoring automation, and forget that reviewing the code will cost other engineers more time
Developers need time to experiment with the setups to be more efficient
Broader industry impact
Many stopped hiring junior engineers, this is a problem that will only hit us later on as lack of replacement will be a problem
No more training data produced, we stopped asking questions online, so AI can’t learn from new questions - model collapse as a concept and what we can do about it
AI is writing code only it can understand
Now when we write a project, we can tweak and fix it for the most part ourselves
When AI writes a project, we usually don’t understand much or need a lot of time to understand the code, so it’s then easier to ask AI again to rewrite everything again
Gets even more complex with projects like https://veralang.dev/
Shift Right: AI creates more work - more code to review, more output to review
If non-engineers commit code - even more time needed for review
For non-engineering work - work slop means the output is not as good as if an expert did it and requires more more time to review and i.e. verify sources
Orchestration tax
You have to put everything AI gives you together, proof it, fix it, redo etc. Often this takes as much time as if you’d have done the work yourself
Tokenmaxxing vs. valuemaxxing
Instead of using as many tokens as possible (https://www.businessinsider.com/jensen-huang-500k-engineers-250k-ai-tokens-nvidia-compute-2026-3) maximise value and outcomes created
AI security
Necessary guardrails:
PII filtering
Topic blocking
Tone control
Many examples of this backfiring in real world conversations
Output constraints
Ideas:
AI security tools https://www.anthropic.com/glasswing
Broader repercussions: https://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence/anthropic-claude-mythos-escaped-sandbox
https://cybernews.com/ai-news/ai-models-scheme-protect-other-models/
Impact of AI on recent leaks and incidents, i.e. claude code leak, axios etc.
Crazy hacks like https://neuraltrust.ai/blog/grok-morse-code
Stance from security experts: AI can do discovery of issues where there is either no incentive for real world experts or it’s actually not encouraged
Impact of AI on mental health of engineers
Concept of token anxiety https://writing.nikunjk.com/p/token-anxiety; AI psychosis as a new term
AI in some companies became an objective and not a tool
Moving to Context Engineering to ensure better output quality
Great success of AI integration: data analytics and data visualisation tools like Snowflake and Hex have integrated AI agents you can use to speed up data analysis and visualisation
Broader societal impact
Dead internet theory, tools like https://github.com/jamiepine/voicebox or imagegen are getting indistinguishable from real life footage
Internal setups and tweaks to make agents more useful
OpenClaw and other alternatives
Setups like https://github.com/russelleNVy/three-man-team
Optimising token use, i.e. with https://github.com/cancerit/CaVEMan
Effects of claude.md and agents.md https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
Other tweaks combining models https://claude.com/blog/the-advisor-strategy
Template 2 — Personal Story Focus (narrative-forward)
Give a one-sentence snapshot of the moment that set this journey in motion.
Where does this story begin for you?

Which events, successes, or stumbles taught you the most?
