If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
We’ll handle the editing and formatting from there. Thanks for contributing to the community!
Tab for Alexey and DTC Team
AI Adoption in Enterprise Beyond Writing Code
In this episode, we speak with Ivan Bilan, Senior Engineering Manager at Personio, about the transition of AI adoption from experimental phases to routine engineering tasks.
AI can assist with implementation, but software engineering teams still need to handle several critical responsibilities: defining requirements, designing architecture, managing technical debt, testing systems, monitoring production, reviewing changes, planning rollouts, and maintaining their outputs.
We’ll discuss:
How engineering managers can approach AI adoption in enterprise settings.
Where AI can expedite team processes and where it may introduce hidden challenges.
Why companies must be cautious about encouraging everyone to contribute code without investing in processes for releases, review capabilities, observability, and maintenance.
We also explore the wider implications of AI adoption within the software industry, including reduced junior hiring opportunities, evolving expectations for engineers, risks associated with AI-generated systems that may be difficult for humans to comprehend, security concerns, and the shift from “using more tokens” to creating greater value.
About the Speaker
Ivan Bilan is a Senior Engineering Manager at Personio, leading multiple teams in the Identity and Access Management (IAM) domain. Previously, he served as a Data Science & Engineering Manager at TrustYou, where he led NLP and infrastructure groups to optimize massive ETL and ML pipelines. With an MS in Computational Linguistics from LMU Munich and a background at CDTM, Ivan bridges the gap between deep technical NLP research and senior leadership in high-throughput enterprise environments.
Speech
Today, we’re discussing AI adoption in enterprise engineering teams. We’ll look at how AI changes perceptions of software engineering, common pitfalls in its adoption, and how to move from just using AI to creating value.
Our guest is Ivan Bilan, Senior Engineering Manager at Personio. He leads teams in Identity and Access Management and previously optimized ETL and ML pipelines at TrustYou. With a master’s in Computational Linguistics from LMU Munich, Ivan connects deep technical research with leadership in fast-paced environments.
Welcome, Ivan!
Before we go into our main topic of X, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
You’ve worked in NLP and data science before, and now you manage engineering teams in IAM. From your perspective, how has the current AI wave changed the way companies think about software engineering?
If AI can help with implementation, which parts of engineering still require strong human judgment?
In enterprise environments, what usually happens after someone generates code with AI? What are the next steps before it can become part of a real product?
Do you think AI-generated code changes the cost of code review? Can it make reviewing harder rather than easier?
How should engineering managers decide where AI can speed up a team and where it may create hidden work?
There is a lot of pressure to use more AI, more agents, and more tokens. How can teams avoid optimizing for AI usage itself and focus instead on business or engineering value?
What would you measure to understand whether AI is actually improving engineering work: delivery speed, quality, incidents, developer experience, cost, customer outcomes, or something else?
There is a trend of companies hiring fewer junior engineers or expecting juniors to become productive faster with AI. What long-term risks could this create for engineering teams?
Does AI change what junior engineers should learn first? For example, should they focus more on debugging, architecture, testing, or understanding systems?
What internal setup makes AI coding tools or agents more useful in real teams? For example, documentation, coding standards, repo structure, architecture notes, or files like claude.md and agents.md.
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

general_questions: How would you like us to introduce you?

Senior Data Scientist at Intuit, based in London — doing what most people outside the company would now call AI engineering: building AI-powered features in production at scale. About 9 years across banking (Raiffeisen), cybersecurity (Kaspersky), retail (X5 Retail Group), and fintech (Intuit), with experience on both sides of the IC–management line: I led two data science teams at X5 Retail Group before moving back to IC at Intuit, and I founded and run Intuit's 70+ member Data Science Guild. Inventor on 15+ ML/AI patents.
What are you currently focused on—projects or themes you’d enjoy discussing?

Agentic systems for executive decision-making. I architected an LLM-based agentic system at Intuit that aggregates data, automatically generates reports, and delivers them directly to decision-makers in Slack. It saves 30+ hours/week at the leadership level — but the more interesting story is how it started as an AI chatbot and pivoted to automation after customer interviews showed me the chatbot was nice-to-have, not the real pain.

Grounding and trust for AI outputs. I'm currently building a knowledge base with human-in-the-loop verification used to verify / ground AI outputs and increase trust in them. Every enterprise is wrestling with the same two problems right now: speeding up delivery for non-experts AND getting people to actually trust AI tools. They're the same problem.
Which topics are you most keen to cover?

Career arc: Data scientist → ML engineer → Data scientist → AI engineer. The labels change often, but the actual work has barely changed. My current title at Intuit is Senior Data Scientist; outside Intuit the same work is called AI Engineering. I'd love to tell that story honestly

"The model is rarely the win." Three stories from different parts of my career where business reframing mattered far more than the model: a fine-tuned BERT for customer customer-support project at Kaspersky (2019–2020) where actual win was reframing 200 categories down to the 20–30 that were actually actionable and linking ML metrics to business outcomes; the agentic insights system at Intuit pivoting from chatbot to automation; and a cost-efficient ML-based filter for LLM-as-judge evaluation that cut inference spend by about 33%.

Knowing when to abandon. Most people publish their wins. I'd love to talk about a transaction-extraction project I worked on at Intuit that I had to abandon — and then watched a more senior team try it again with better infrastructure and abandon it for the same reason. Some problems don't move. Recognizing that earlier is one of the most senior moves you can make.

The big-company career path. Most "how to become an AI engineer" stories are startup or side-project flavored. I'd love to share the other path: navigating changes, manager conversations, and lateral moves inside a big corp. I led two data science teams at X5 Retail Group before circumstances took me back to IC at Intuit, where I later founded the Data Science Guild — because day one I'd told my manager I wanted to return to management eventually.
Are there any topics you’d rather skip or avoid discussing publicly?

I'd rather keep references to the wars in Russia and Israel descriptive rather than political — I left Russia in 2022 like many tech workers did, and we left Israel in 2024 because of the regional situation. Beyond that I'd rather not go into either.
Recent layoffs at Intuit (May 20, 2026)

detailed_questions: Mixed Template Answers (Template 2 spine + selected Template 1 anchors)

Give a one-sentence snapshot of the moment that set this journey in motion.During my bachelor's at MIPT, I took a machine learning course that wasn't part of my major. I went out of curiosity. The idea that you could use data to find repeatable, predictable patterns — and in some sense predict the future — felt like magic. That was the moment.

What vivid fact, problem, or provocative question will hook listeners?AI didn't shrink the gap between PoC and product. It just made the PoCs cheaper. I've watched seasoned DS professionals — people who spent years on the same problem — get seduced by a slick AI demo that looked great from the dev side and was a disaster from the customer side. The unglamorous work between demo and deployment is still there.
Which events, successes, or stumbles taught you the most?Three projects, in order of how much they shaped me:

Kaspersky, 2019–2020. I fine-tuned BERT for customer-support automation. Initially I was given a 200-category text classification task, but only 20–30 of those categories were actionable for the business. The win wasn't the BERT model — fine-tuning a transformer was hard in 2019 and felt like a real achievement at the time, but that wasn't the value. The value was reframing the problem around what the business actually cared about, and translating ML metrics like precision and recall into business metrics like First Contact Resolution and automation rate. Once stakeholders saw the value framed that way, we iterated fast and cut support costs by about 20%. That lesson — the model is rarely the win — is one I've recycled in every project since.

Intuit, early 2023, transaction extraction from 20,000+ financial data sources. We initially tried using LLMs to write extraction scripts per provider. The infrastructure wasn't ready, the LLMs at that moment couldn't write executable code reliably on the first try, and we abandoned that approach. We pivoted to a divide-and-conquer model — a small NER-like system that navigated HTML pages and parsed transactional columns. It worked well. But the project stalled because the customer couldn't set up data access in time — PII obfuscation took longer than the model itself. Two years later, after I moved to London, I heard a more senior team picked the project up again with better infrastructure and current-generation LLMs. It got stuck at the same stage. First I thought I hadn't pushed hard enough the first time. When it failed under better-resourced people, I realised — some problems just don't move. The most productive thing you can do is recognize that earlier and move to the next doable thing.

The agentic insights system at Intuit. Started as a POC: an AI chatbot with access to internal databases. In parallel I led customer interviews with marketing, sales, and other internal teams. The interviews made it clear that the chatbot was nice-to-have. The real pain was manual report assembly — leadership was making decisions on Tuesday on last week's data, because the analysts were manually writing their summaries and commentary. We pivoted to automated reports plus AI-generated commentary (summaries) delivered straight into Slack. We didn't just save 30+ hours of executive time per week. We collapsed the decision-latency gap. If I could plant one lesson in every junior LLM-app builder's head, it would be: run customer interviews before you build the chatbot.
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?
The model is rarely the win. The leverage is upstream: in choosing the right problem, talking to customers, reframing metrics around what the business actually cares about.
Know when to abandon. Some projects don't move regardless of infrastructure or team. The most senior thing you can do is recognize that early and move to the next doable thing.
Who might recognize their own journey in yours?
People doing AI/ML work inside large companies, especially those whose classical ML projects have been disrupted by LLMs and who are trying to figure out where their value sits now.

People who've taken a sideways step in their career — back to IC after managing, back to senior/middle after tech lead / staff — to get into a new country, a new company, or a new domain, and are wondering whether they'll ever climb back.
And anyone currently grinding on a project that won't move, blaming themselves for it.
What 3–5 main stops, projects, or challenges mark the path?

Kaspersky, 2019–2021. Fine-tuned BERT for customer-support automation; 20% cost reduction. First time deploying a transformer into production felt like a real engineering achievement at the time. The bigger lesson was that the model wasn't what made the project ship.

X5 Retail Group, 2021–2022. My first management role. Led two data science teams on promo optimization and pricing, forecasting 100,000+ time series daily across more than 20,000 stores. Hired one team from scratch and also supported hiring for DS, DE, and DQ specialists for other teams. I moved back to IC at Intuit during a relocation, and the management muscle has stayed active through the Data Science Guild.

The 2022 move to Israel and Intuit. I was already looking to leave. When the X5 product I led was closed, external forces simplified a decision I'd been struggling with — I wanted to leave but was attached to the career I'd built. I applied to management roles from outside the country, got zero responses, and took an IC role at Intuit to get my foot in the door. I was transparent with my manager and told I wanted to return to management eventually. The Guild opportunity came about some time after I'd told my manager about my ambitions — he remembered, and when his manager floated reviving the data community, I was ready.

London, end of 2024 — becoming the AI person of contact. After moving to London, several of my classical ML projects were deprioritized, and the new projects I was assigned were much smaller and less impactful. Emotionally this was the toughest stretch of my career so far. But that same period turned into a transformation. I started giving seminars on LLM architectures and coding agents for analysts and developers across the org, and gradually became the AI person of contact for people learning to work with these tools. A Canadian data scientist later told me their AI setup was inspired by my seminars. That kind of feedback meant a lot — and it pointed at something I'm still thinking about. Impact in AI work is often real but hard to feel. A project that saves 30+ hours a week at the executives level matters in money terms (imagine salary of International business leaders and impact of their decisions on the business), but it doesn't land emotionally the way one colleague telling you their work shifted because of yours does. That stretch is also when my own role transitioned from classical ML and data science into what's now called AI engineering.
What key insight or skill will listeners gain?
For anyone working on AI features in a real company:
Run customer interviews before you build a complex AI solution. Often chatbots are not the answer. Automation usually is.
Translate ML metrics into business metrics on day one. ML metrics are an input. They are not deliverables.
Use cheap models for simple tasks. Most cases in any classifier are easy. Save the LLM judge for the hard cases.
For anyone navigating a career inside a big company:

Tell your manager what you want — clearly, explicitly, more than once. You can't control whether they remember, whether the opportunity comes up, or whether it comes up in time. You can only control whether your ambitions are legible. It doesn't always work. But it never works if you don't try.
Know when to abandon. Pushing harder is not always the answer.

If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?The model is rarely the win.Looking back at the projects that mattered most to me — fine-tuning BERT for customer support at Kaspersky in 2019, the agentic insights pivot at Intuit, the LLM-as-judge cost optimization — the model was always necessary and almost never sufficient. What shipped the result was the business reframing upstream of it. That's worth holding onto in the LLM era especially, when the demos look so impressive it's easy to forget where the real work lives.
Optional extras the team is welcome to use or ignore:

An early-career detail that didn't fit the main arc: At Raiffeisen Bank in 2018–2019, our team spent a couple of weeks at Yandex and Mail.ru — we brought our bank data onto their infrastructure to see if richer features would translate to enough business impact to pay for the partnership. That's where I first saw an interesting data engineering technique — embedding-centroid customer profiling: interests as vectors, distances from customer-vectors to the interests as features. I tried to replicate it later at a smaller scale and it didn't work, because at that dimensionality the data volume just wasn't there. A small early lesson in not copy-pasting techniques across scale.

A demystification of patents: a patent is usually a new combination of existing ideas applied to a domain where they weren't combined before. Example from my own list: combining embeddings with n-grams to disambiguate short bank-transaction descriptions, using the financial domain as the anchor. Public on USPTO.

A humanizing anecdote outside work: I picked up skimboarding in Israel after friends saw me watching videos and gifted me a board. When I moved to London I tried to organize a legal spot in a park — contacted the council, the municipal events team, and several insurance companies (most don't insure individual-led events). I pivoted to a smaller version: building a 25-meter tarp pool 5cm deep with 1–2 tons of water. I was tired before I even started riding. I abandoned the project and now skimboard a few times a year at the seaside. The same principle as my professional projects, at miniature scale.

event_description: Title: How to Build AI That Actually Ships in Production

Aleksandr Kim has spent nearly a decade doing what the industry now calls AI engineering, long before the title existed. As a Senior Data Scientist at Intuit in London, he builds AI-powered features in production at scale, and his career traces the full arc from data scientist to ML engineer and now AI engineer, where, as he puts it, the labels keep changing but the actual work barely has.

In this conversation, Aleksandr unpacks one hard-won lesson from the front lines of enterprise AI: the model is rarely the win. He walks through the agentic system he architected at Intuit, one that aggregates data, auto-generates reports, and delivers them straight to leadership in Slack, saving over 30 hours of executive time a week, and explains why it only worked after he killed the original chatbot idea.
What we get into:
Why AI didn't shrink the gap between proof-of-concept and product. It just made the PoCs cheaper, and the unglamorous work between demo and deployment is still very much there.
The agentic system that pivoted from chatbot to automation, and why customer interviews, not better modeling, were what made it ship.
Translating ML metrics into business outcomes, turning precision and recall into things like First Contact Resolution and automation rate, on day one.
Cost-efficient AI at scale, using cheap models for the easy cases and saving the LLM judge for the hard ones, cutting inference spend by about a third.
Knowing when to abandon, the underrated senior skill of recognizing a problem that simply won't move, no matter the infrastructure or team.
If you're an engineer or data scientist trying to figure out where your value sits in the LLM era, especially inside a large company, this one's for you.
bio: Senior Data Scientist at Intuit, based in London — doing what most people outside the company would now call AI engineering: building AI-powered features in production at scale. About 9 years across banking (Raiffeisen), cybersecurity (Kaspersky), retail (X5 Retail Group), and fintech (Intuit), with experience on both sides of the IC–management line: I led two data science teams at X5 Retail Group before moving back to IC at Intuit, and I founded and run Intuit's 70+ member Data Science Guild. Inventor on 15+ ML/AI patents.
speech: This week we're talking about what it actually takes to build AI in production, the unglamorous work that happens after the impressive demo, and how to tell when a project is worth pushing on versus walking away from.

We have a special guest today, Aleksandr Kim. Aleksandr is a Senior Data Scientist at Intuit, based in London, doing what most people outside the company would now call AI engineering: building AI-powered features in production at scale. He's spent about nine years across banking, cybersecurity, retail, and fintech, at Raiffeisen, Kaspersky, X5 Retail Group, and now Intuit, on both sides of the IC and management line. He led two data science teams at X5 before moving back to IC at Intuit, where he founded and runs the company's 70-plus-member Data Science Guild. He's also an inventor on more than 15 ML and AI patents.
Welcome, Aleksandr!
Top 10 questions

Your title is Senior Data Scientist, but the work is what most people now call AI engineering. Walk us through that arc from data scientist to ML engineer to AI engineer. What actually changed, and what didn't?
You say "the model is rarely the win." What do you mean by that, and when did that lesson first land for you?
Tell us about the agentic system you built at Intuit. What does it do, and how did it end up saving leadership over 30 hours a week?
That system started as a chatbot and you killed that idea. What did the customer interviews tell you that the demo couldn't?
You've said AI didn't shrink the gap between proof-of-concept and product, it just made the PoCs cheaper. Why is the work between demo and deployment still so hard?
You have a habit of translating ML metrics into business metrics on day one. Why does that matter so much, and what goes wrong when teams skip it?
Let's talk cost. You cut inference spend by about a third using cheap models for the easy cases and saving the expensive LLM judge for the hard ones. How does that work in practice?
One of your themes is knowing when to abandon a project. Tell us about one you walked away from, and how you knew it was the right call.
You watched a more senior team later pick up a project you'd abandoned and get stuck at the same point. What did that teach you about which problems simply don't move?
For an engineer or data scientist trying to figure out where their value sits in the LLM era, especially inside a large company, what's the one thing you'd tell them?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

Initially I was given a 200-category text classification task, but only 20–30 of those categories were actionable for the business. The win wasn't the BERT model — fine-tuning a transformer was hard in 2019 and felt like a real achievement at the time, but that wasn't the value. The value was reframing the problem around what the business actually cared about, and translating ML metrics like precision and recall into business metrics like First Contact Resolution and automation rate. Once stakeholders saw the value framed that way, we iterated fast and cut support costs by about 20%. That lesson — the model is rarely the win — is one I've recycled in every project since.
Intuit, early 2023, transaction extraction from 20,000+ financial data sources. We initially tried using LLMs to write extraction scripts per provider. The infrastructure wasn't ready, the LLMs at that moment couldn't write executable code reliably on the first try, and we abandoned that approach. We pivoted to a divide-and-conquer model — a small NER-like system that navigated HTML pages and parsed transactional columns. It worked well. But the project stalled because the customer couldn't set up data access in time — PII obfuscation took longer than the model itself. Two years later, after I moved to London, I heard a more senior team picked the project up again with better infrastructure and current-generation LLMs. It got stuck at the same stage. First I thought I hadn't pushed hard enough the first time. When it failed under better-resourced people, I realised — some problems just don't move. The most productive thing you can do is recognize that earlier and move to the next doable thing.
The agentic insights system at Intuit. Started as a POC: an AI chatbot with access to internal databases. In parallel I led customer interviews with marketing, sales, and other internal teams. The interviews made it clear that the chatbot was nice-to-have. The real pain was manual report assembly — leadership was making decisions on Tuesday on last week's data, because the analysts were manually writing their summaries and commentary. We pivoted to automated reports plus AI-generated commentary (summaries) delivered straight into Slack. We didn't just save 30+ hours of executive time per week. We collapsed the decision-latency gap. If I could plant one lesson in every junior LLM-app builder's head, it would be: run customer interviews before you build the chatbot.
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?
The model is rarely the win. The leverage is upstream: in choosing the right problem, talking to customers, reframing metrics around what the business actually cares about.
Know when to abandon. Some projects don't move regardless of infrastructure or team. The most senior thing you can do is recognize that early and move to the next doable thing.
Who might recognize their own journey in yours?
People doing AI/ML work inside large companies, especially those whose classical ML projects have been disrupted by LLMs and who are trying to figure out where their value sits now.
People who've taken a sideways step in their career — back to IC after managing, back to senior/middle after tech lead / staff — to get into a new country, a new company, or a new domain, and are wondering whether they'll ever climb back.
And anyone currently grinding on a project that won't move, blaming themselves for it.
What 3–5 main stops, projects, or challenges mark the path?
Kaspersky, 2019–2021. Fine-tuned BERT for customer-support automation; 20% cost reduction. First time deploying a transformer into production felt like a real engineering achievement at the time. The bigger lesson was that the model wasn't what made the project ship.
X5 Retail Group, 2021–2022. My first management role. Led two data science teams on promo optimization and pricing, forecasting 100,000+ time series daily across more than 20,000 stores. Hired one team from scratch and also supported hiring for DS, DE, and DQ specialists for other teams. I moved back to IC at Intuit during a relocation, and the management muscle has stayed active through the Data Science Guild.
The 2022 move to Israel and Intuit. I was already looking to leave. When the X5 product I led was closed, external forces simplified a decision I'd been struggling with — I wanted to leave but was attached to the career I'd built. I applied to management roles from outside the country, got zero responses, and took an IC role at Intuit to get my foot in the door. I was transparent with my manager and told I wanted to return to management eventually. The Guild opportunity came about some time after I'd told my manager about my ambitions — he remembered, and when his manager floated reviving the data community, I was ready.
London, end of 2024 — becoming the AI person of contact. After moving to London, several of my classical ML projects were deprioritized, and the new projects I was assigned were much smaller and less impactful. Emotionally this was the toughest stretch of my career so far. But that same period turned into a transformation. I started giving seminars on LLM architectures and coding agents for analysts and developers across the org, and gradually became the AI person of contact for people learning to work with these tools. A Canadian data scientist later told me their AI setup was inspired by my seminars. That kind of feedback meant a lot — and it pointed at something I'm still thinking about. Impact in AI work is often real but hard to feel. A project that saves 30+ hours a week at the executives level matters in money terms (imagine salary of International business leaders and impact of their decisions on the business), but it doesn't land emotionally the way one colleague telling you their work shifted because of yours does. That stretch is also when my own role transitioned from classical ML and data science into what's now called AI engineering.
What key insight or skill will listeners gain?
For anyone working on AI features in a real company:
Run customer interviews before you build a complex AI solution. Often chatbots are not the answer. Automation usually is.
Translate ML metrics into business metrics on day one. ML metrics are an input. They are not deliverables.
Use cheap models for simple tasks. Most cases in any classifier are easy. Save the LLM judge for the hard cases.
For anyone navigating a career inside a big company:
Tell your manager what you want — clearly, explicitly, more than once. You can't control whether they remember, whether the opportunity comes up, or whether it comes up in time. You can only control whether your ambitions are legible. It doesn't always work. But it never works if you don't try.
Know when to abandon. Pushing harder is not always the answer.
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?The model is rarely the win.Looking back at the projects that mattered most to me — fine-tuning BERT for customer support at Kaspersky in 2019, the agentic insights pivot at Intuit, the LLM-as-judge cost optimization — the model was always necessary and almost never sufficient. What shipped the result was the business reframing upstream of it. That's worth holding onto in the LLM era especially, when the demos look so impressive it's easy to forget where the real work lives.
Optional extras the team is welcome to use or ignore:
An early-career detail that didn't fit the main arc: At Raiffeisen Bank in 2018–2019, our team spent a couple of weeks at Yandex and Mail.ru — we brought our bank data onto their infrastructure to see if richer features would translate to enough business impact to pay for the partnership. That's where I first saw an interesting data engineering technique — embedding-centroid customer profiling: interests as vectors, distances from customer-vectors to the interests as features. I tried to replicate it later at a smaller scale and it didn't work, because at that dimensionality the data volume just wasn't there. A small early lesson in not copy-pasting techniques across scale.
A demystification of patents: a patent is usually a new combination of existing ideas applied to a domain where they weren't combined before. Example from my own list: combining embeddings with n-grams to disambiguate short bank-transaction descriptions, using the financial domain as the anchor. Public on USPTO.
A humanizing anecdote outside work: I picked up skimboarding in Israel after friends saw me watching videos and gifted me a board. When I moved to London I tried to organize a legal spot in a park — contacted the council, the municipal events team, and several insurance companies (most don't insure individual-led events). I pivoted to a smaller version: building a 25-meter tarp pool 5cm deep with 1–2 tons of water. I was tired before I even started riding. I abandoned the project and now skimboard a few times a year at the seaside. The same principle as my professional projects, at miniature scale.
Tab for Alexey and DTC Team
Rough Plan
Overall duration – up to 1 hour
Intro (5 minutes)
Prepared questions from me about the topic (30-40 minutes)
Questions from the audience (the remaining time)
Event Description
Title: How to Build AI That Actually Ships in Production
Aleksandr Kim has spent nearly a decade doing what the industry now calls AI engineering, long before the title existed. As a Senior Data Scientist at Intuit in London, he builds AI-powered features in production at scale, and his career traces the full arc from data scientist to ML engineer and now AI engineer, where, as he puts it, the labels keep changing but the actual work barely has.
In this conversation, Aleksandr unpacks one hard-won lesson from the front lines of enterprise AI: the model is rarely the win. He walks through the agentic system he architected at Intuit, one that aggregates data, auto-generates reports, and delivers them straight to leadership in Slack, saving over 30 hours of executive time a week, and explains why it only worked after he killed the original chatbot idea.
What we get into:
Why AI didn't shrink the gap between proof-of-concept and product. It just made the PoCs cheaper, and the unglamorous work between demo and deployment is still very much there.
The agentic system that pivoted from chatbot to automation, and why customer interviews, not better modeling, were what made it ship.
Translating ML metrics into business outcomes, turning precision and recall into things like First Contact Resolution and automation rate, on day one.
Cost-efficient AI at scale, using cheap models for the easy cases and saving the LLM judge for the hard ones, cutting inference spend by about a third.
Knowing when to abandon, the underrated senior skill of recognizing a problem that simply won't move, no matter the infrastructure or team.
If you're an engineer or data scientist trying to figure out where your value sits in the LLM era, especially inside a large company, this one's for you.
Bio:
Senior Data Scientist at Intuit, based in London — doing what most people outside the company would now call AI engineering: building AI-powered features in production at scale. About 9 years across banking (Raiffeisen), cybersecurity (Kaspersky), retail (X5 Retail Group), and fintech (Intuit), with experience on both sides of the IC–management line: I led two data science teams at X5 Retail Group before moving back to IC at Intuit, and I founded and run Intuit's 70+ member Data Science Guild. Inventor on 15+ ML/AI patents.

Speech
This week we're talking about what it actually takes to build AI in production, the unglamorous work that happens after the impressive demo, and how to tell when a project is worth pushing on versus walking away from.
We have a special guest today, Aleksandr Kim. Aleksandr is a Senior Data Scientist at Intuit, based in London, doing what most people outside the company would now call AI engineering: building AI-powered features in production at scale. He's spent about nine years across banking, cybersecurity, retail, and fintech, at Raiffeisen, Kaspersky, X5 Retail Group, and now Intuit, on both sides of the IC and management line. He led two data science teams at X5 before moving back to IC at Intuit, where he founded and runs the company's 70-plus-member Data Science Guild. He's also an inventor on more than 15 ML and AI patents.
Welcome, Aleksandr!
Top 10 questions
Your title is Senior Data Scientist, but the work is what most people now call AI engineering. Walk us through that arc from data scientist to ML engineer to AI engineer. What actually changed, and what didn't?
You say "the model is rarely the win." What do you mean by that, and when did that lesson first land for you?
Tell us about the agentic system you built at Intuit. What does it do, and how did it end up saving leadership over 30 hours a week?
That system started as a chatbot and you killed that idea. What did the customer interviews tell you that the demo couldn't?
You've said AI didn't shrink the gap between proof-of-concept and product, it just made the PoCs cheaper. Why is the work between demo and deployment still so hard?
You have a habit of translating ML metrics into business metrics on day one. Why does that matter so much, and what goes wrong when teams skip it?
Let's talk cost. You cut inference spend by about a third using cheap models for the easy cases and saving the expensive LLM judge for the hard ones. How does that work in practice?
One of your themes is knowing when to abandon a project. Tell us about one you walked away from, and how you knew it was the right call.
You watched a more senior team later pick up a project you'd abandoned and get stuck at the same point. What did that teach you about which problems simply don't move?
For an engineer or data scientist trying to figure out where their value sits in the LLM era, especially inside a large company, what's the one thing you'd tell them?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

team_tab: Title: Building a Career in AI: From Real Estate to AI Engineering
Subtitle: The self-taught engineer's playbook with Gustaf Gyllensporre

Breaking into the AI engineering space without a traditional computer science degree can feel impossible, but it is entirely achievable with the right strategy. In this upcoming live event, we are sitting down with Gustaf Gyllensporre, who successfully executed a massive career pivot from working as a Miami real estate agent to becoming a self-taught Senior AI Engineer and PropTech startup founder.

Gustaf will share his unconventional journey, detailing the sheer discipline and actionable tactics he used to bypass industry gatekeepers and land senior roles. We will dive into the strategic ways to contribute to open source, how he leverages his domain expertise to build practical AI solutions for real estate, and why "learning in public" on his YouTube channel ultimately solidified his technical mastery. Whether you are transitioning from a completely different industry or looking to accelerate your AI engineering career, this session will provide a realistic, battle-tested roadmap.
He’ll cover:
The pivot from real estate to senior AI engineer
Bypassing gatekeepers with open source
Learning in public and teaching
Leveraging domain expertise to build a PropTech startup
Bypassing Gatekeepers with Open Source
Learning in Public and Teaching
Leveraging Domain Expertise to Build a PropTech Startup
About the Speaker:

Gustaf Gyllensporre is a former real estate agent turned Senior AI Engineer. His frustrations with the lack of technology in the real estate industry prompted him to build his own. Since then, He led development of conversational search for eCommerce at Depict.AI, founded by Anton Osika (Lovable) and Oliver Edholm.
He is currently building the agentic OS for construction at Brickanta. Open-source contributor with 100+ stars on GitHub, contributed to projects such as: CPython, LangGraph
speech: This week we’ll talk about Building a Career in AI: From Real Estate to AI Engineering.
We have a special guest today - Gustaf Gyllensporre.

Gustaf Gyllensporre is a former real estate agent turned Senior AI Engineer. His frustrations with the lack of technology in the real estate industry prompted him to build his own. Since then, He led development of conversational search for eCommerce at Depict.AI, founded by Anton Osika (Lovable) and Oliver Edholm.
He is currently building the agentic OS for construction at Brickanta. Open-source contributor with 100+ stars on GitHub, contributed to projects such as: CPython, LangGraph
Welcome, Gustaf!
Before we go into our main topic of Building a Career in AI, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
The Career Transition and Learning Process
What was the specific inflection point or reality check that made you decide to leave real estate for software engineering?
When you began teaching yourself, how did you filter through the massive amount of available learning materials to find what actually mattered for production environments?
Many self-taught developers struggle to bridge the gap between building tutorial projects and understanding complex system architecture. How did you cross that technical threshold?
Navigating the Job Market and Open Source

You have mentioned using open-source contributions to bypass traditional credential requirements. What did that strategy look like in practice, and how did you select projects that would catch the eye of hiring managers?

When transitioning straight into a senior-level role without a corporate tech background, how did you handle the non-coding aspects of the job, such as navigating engineering processes, sprint planning, and team dynamics?
Learning in Public and Content Creation

Documenting a career pivot publicly on YouTube introduces a lot of vulnerability. How did sharing your mistakes and technical gaps openly affect your professional credibility and your own learning curve?

Your content connects AI engineering to real-world business cases rather than abstract theory. From an educational standpoint, what do you think traditional tech curricula miss when preparing people for actual industry roles?
PropTech and Domain-Specific AI Integration
How has your insider perspective on the real estate market shaped how you evaluate AI use cases, compared to an engineer who only looks at the industry from the outside?
In the PropTech space, where do you see AI introducing genuine, long-term operational efficiency versus where is it just being used as a temporary marketing buzzword?
Community and the Evolving Tech Landscape

Given your experience interacting with cross-over audiences on YouTube and within repositories like the AI Engineering Field Guide, what are the biggest misconceptions traditional developers have when trying to pivot into AI engineering?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
My AI Engineer interview prep playlist
Evals - Hamel Husain’s blog
Anthropic blog
Deeplearning.AI (http://deeplearning.ai) Machine Learning Specialization

general_questions: How would you like us to introduce you?
Maryam is Data and AI leader with 20+ years experince leading data teams across big corporates such as adidas, sky, delivery hero and Zalando.
What are you currently focused on—projects or themes you’d enjoy discussing?

During my last assignment in Zalando, I realized that companies all want AI but don’t really know where to start. Although the title has got fancy and there are a lot of new tools, the problem goes back to Data, and that is where I have spent almost all my career. Data strategy. Therefore I have decided to help companies to build their data and AI strategy .

On the other hand, I feel I owe something to the community, it is time for me to give back by coaching and helping Data experts to find ther path. I have started career coaching, and I have created design thinking workshop tailored to data professionals to help them decide on their career step and find their dream job.
Which topics are you most keen to cover?

I would like this podcast to focus on Data careers, help listeners to navigate their data career journey and focus Focus on soft skills needed for AI Era, and share my experines working as leader in data organzaitions, what I have learned and what I see important going forward

Main message for audience: resilience
The must have skill to deal with the change and chaos is resilience

With 25 professional year of experience I can say that we as person and also companies change and grow best in the face of adversity, we find identities and grow our boundaries of resilience when something bad happens
resilience is like a child bouncing back from failure and having courage to continue, having flexibility and optimism ( how many times the baby falls before they can walk?)
Start with my young person : the fight for perfection
294 out of 1M
First to xxxx
Story of failure: the first time, I couldnt face going back, feeled with shame, grief,
What made me who I am is failures

What makes you resilient is failures, accepting , normalize failure, storty of pysocolocal safety Margin of failure ( being able to accept that you make failures and you can bounce back) : my story of failures

Script:
Introduction : Maryam
- Tell us about what you are working on now and what brought you there:

During my last assignment in Zalando, I realized that companies all want AI but don’t really know where to start. Although the title has got fancy and there are a lot of new tools, the problem goes back to Data, and that is where I have spent almost all my career. Data strategy. Therefore I have decided to help companies to build their data and AI strategy .

On the other hand, I feel I own something to the community, it is time for me to give back by coaching and helping Data experts to find their path. I have decided to start career coaching, design thinking workshop tailored to data professionals to help them decide on their career step and find their dream job.
- as, Our listeners are interested to know what skills you see as essential for their job and how they can land their dream job.
Hard skills -> your courses are great, no bluff, technicaly very strong for data/ML engineers and even data scientists. In addition for data scince the core of data scince has not changed .

What I see more and more happening is the blending of skills. There migh not all the different roles as before : enginer, scientist, analyts, etc. companies are demanding more and more gemeralist who can use AI and bring a mixure of skills. I would still say to chose one area as your code competency where you are so strong that you can also
Soft skills: what I see as major soft skills are curiosity resilience and storytelling.
I would like to talk about each one and give you examples of my career. Not what I did right but where I actually make mistakes to nable other not to make same
Thse are my WI notes
as a young kid, I was extremely perfectionsit. I worked very hard to get everything fully completely correct. In iran we get grades from 20 …

when I joined the work force, my approach was the same, I thought work hard, deliver results and this is the way to success to the next career step. I was ambitious and I went the career steps fast. But what I had not learned , was how to fail, or better to say how to accept failure, learn from it and bounce back.

The first time I heard from a manager that I have not delivered to his expectations, I really didn’t know how to react. I couldn’t even accept that I have done something wrong. I got a difficult burn out and it took me really long time to get out of it.
I have several learning from that event . First and most important, we are human , we are allowed to sometimes fail. It is not important not to fail but how you find the failure, how you react.
Fail forward
if you have failed, congratulations, you already made a first step of becoming resilient.
It is in moments of deep hardship that you find your real strength.
People and also companies change and grow best in the face of adversity. we find our true identity and grow our boundaries when something hard happens.
When I got hard feedback, I was so shamed, I felt like grief , I felt depressed
The reality is that all of that is made by my brain not accepting what is
In life you need to accept what you cannot change ( mostly past) and focus on really deserves your attention. Where your focus goes your energy flows
And now about the why I got that feedback .
My mistake was not that I didn’t do hard work , or I didn’t deliver but in communication. In how I created visibility on what needs to be done

When your manage ask you a do a project, it is super important to align on outcomes, to create a constant line of communication and to make sure there is alignment not only with your manger but across the organization, This gets tricky the higher your level is.
My mistake for example in this case was that the alignment was with manager but not with stokeholders or the senior leaders who had a different expectations.

I was sometimes to fast during the digital transformation and expectation of where the company and how fast they need to move. For example my first role in adidas, I came from AdTech where we had build real time DMP. And landing in adidas to see there is even not a proper data base to have a 360 view of customer was a shock.
Story of creating CDNA , BIG Data, digital transformation:
Learning: you can not move faster than the organization. Focus on alignment and bringing other behind your ideas
2. somthes bringing other behind, might mean your project or idea can go to someone else * examples Turbin, Cortex API

I was emotional about it, fehl like my idea is stolen, now I would say to my younger self, you should be proud and happy that your idea is realized. Your idea was bigger than you, you don’t need to lead everything to end. Focus on being useful for your organization. It is not about you , it is about the organization and the goals.
So , in summary I would say when it comes to soft skills focus on :
Resilience : be ready to fail, seek feedback from failure, ask for help, bounce back

Communication and story telling: seek allignemnt with your stakholders in every step and know what they care about . The details of you technical project might be very inetering for some but when it comes to your stakhodler or potentially your manager, or senior leaders focus on what hatters for them.
- How can someone improve their communication skills?
I am a member of toastmaster and I recommend it to everyone
Beside that practice your listing and presentation skills on daily basis. You can even try that with AI 😊
About the career workshops:
2nd part : Data and Ai strategy
event_description: Title: Thriving in the AI Era with Human Skills
Subtitle: Building a Future-Ready Data Career with Maryam Ramezani-Bartsch

As companies eagerly adopt the latest AI tools, many overlook the foundational data strategy required to actually make them work. Maryam has seen firsthand how the rapid evolution of technology is blurring the lines between traditional data roles. In this new landscape, professionals are increasingly expected to be AI-empowered generalists, yet maintaining a strong core technical competency is more important than ever to stand out.

However, technical skills alone are no longer enough to guarantee long-term success. In this episode, Maryam shares her deeply personal career journey from battling perfectionism and navigating burnout to learning how to fail forward and manage complex stakeholder alignment. She reveals why the top three human skills are the ultimate career moats in an automated world. Listeners will walk away with actionable tools, including insights from her Design Thinking workshops, to help them intentionally craft and future-proof their dream data careers.
She’ll cover:
What AI Changes about Data Careers
The Human Skills That Become More Valuable as AI Advances
Designing Your Next Career Chapter Intentionally
About the Speaker:
Maryam Ramezani-Bartsch is Data and AI leader with 20+ years experience leading data teams across big corporates such as adidas, sky, delivery hero and Zalando. Find out more at maryamramezani.com.
speech: This week we’ll talk about Thriving in the AI Era with Human Skills.
We have a special guest today - Maryam Ramezani Bartsch
TODO: bio
Welcome, Maryam!
Before we go into our main topic of Thriving in the AI Era with Human Skills, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
The Evolution of Data Roles and Strategy
After 20 years at companies like adidas and Zalando, how have you seen corporate "data readiness" evolve in the rush to adopt AI?
The industry is shifting toward "AI-empowered generalists." How can a data professional broaden their skills without losing their deep technical edge?
When diagnosing an enterprise's data problems, how do you separate true infrastructure bottlenecks from simple strategic misalignment?
Resilience and Managing Failure
You have spoken about moving away from perfectionism. What does a healthy "margin of failure" actually look like in high-stakes corporate data projects?
Based on your experience with burnout, what are the early signs that an engineer is tying too much of their personal identity to their project outcomes?
When using Design Thinking to coach data professionals, what common blind spots do they uncover about their actual career goals?
Stakeholder Alignment
How do you map out and manage expectations with hidden or secondary stakeholders who aren't your direct manager?
When a technical project you championed gets handed over to another team, how do you process that shift objectively?
How do you determine the maximum speed at which a traditional organization can realistically absorb digital transformation?
Communication and Soft Skills
How do you structure a technical narrative for senior leaders so that it addresses business priorities without oversimplifying the engineering reality?
Aside from formal training like Toastmasters, what subtle, daily habits can engineers practice to improve their alignment and listening skills?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

event_description: Title: Engineering Your Own AI Assistant
Subtitle: Deep Research, Personal Automation, and the Agentic Workflow with Paul Iusztin

As AI tools become increasingly accessible, the frontier is shifting from massive enterprise applications to highly customized personal automation. In this live event, Paul Iusztin returns to dive deep into the mechanics of building personal AI assistants.

Moving past our previous discussion on enterprise LLMOps, Paul will break down the practical realities of agentic AI engineering for the individual developer. We will explore how to architect specialized agents for deep research, how to safely manage unstructured personal data, and why over-engineering multi-agent systems is the fastest way to end up in proof-of-concept purgatory.
He’ll cover:
The mechanics of personal automation: knowing when to build vs. prompt
Architecting autonomous agents for deep research and content creation
Escaping proof-of-concept purgatory: lightweight infrastructure for individual developers
Insights from the Agentic AI Engineering course and core skills for 2026
About the Speaker:

Paul Iustin is the author of the bestseller LLM Engineer’s Handbook, lead instructor of the Agentic AI Engineering course, founding AI Engineer of a San Francisco start-up, and obsessed with making knowledge accessible through AI.

With over 10 years of experience and 20 apps shipped, he teaches AI Engineering as he wanted to at the beginning of his career. End-to-end. From idea to production. From data collection to deploying, monitoring, and evaluation. With a focus on AI principles, software patterns, and infrastructure systems that will thrive in a future dominated by AI coding tools.
His ultimate goal is to help other engineers escape PoC purgatory and 10x their AI Engineering skills.
speech: This week we’ll talk about Engineering Your Own AI Assistant.
We have a special guest today - Paul Iustin.
TODO: bio
Welcome, Paul!
Before we go into our main topic of Engineering Your Own AI Assistant, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
The Mechanics of Personal Automation

When evaluating a repetitive daily task, what specific threshold or heuristic do you use to decide if it is worth the engineering effort to build an agent, rather than just doing it manually with ChatGPT?
What is the most common way developers over-engineer or overcomplicate their personal automation workflows when they first start building them?

Personal workflows involve messy, highly unstructured private data. How does data preparation and context ingestion differ when building a system meant only for yourself compared to a multi-user enterprise application?
Agents for Research and Content Creation
When building an agent for open-ended, deep research, how do you programmatically define when the agent should stop searching and conclude it has gathered enough context?

Agents designed for content creation often fall into the trap of producing generic or highly sanitized outputs. What is the technical mechanism for ensuring an agent consistently replicates a specific, nuanced human voice?

When chaining multiple personal agents together for example, one that curates emails and another that drafts responses how do you manage the context handoff so information isn't lost or hallucinated between steps?
Maintenance and Infrastructure for the Individual

In your previous appearance, we discussed enterprise LLMOps tools like Opik. For an individual developer running a personal assistant locally, what does a realistic, lightweight approach to monitoring failures and token costs look like?

Personal automation scripts are notorious for breaking when underlying APIs or models update. How do you architect personal agents to be resilient to these external shifts without spending hours on weekly maintenance?

When allowing a personal agent to execute code or send communications autonomously, how do you implement fail-safes that protect against costly mistakes without stripping away the agent's actual usefulness?
Insights from the Agent Engineering Course

Based on your experience teaching the Agent Engineering course, what specific architectural concept do students struggle with the most when trying to move from basic API calls to true autonomous behavior?

If a developer wants to fundamentally transform their productivity but only has a few hours a week to dedicate to learning, what is the single highest-leverage first project they should attempt to build?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

general_questions: How would you like us to introduce you?

Radovan Bacovic: Radovan Bacovic is a Staff Data Engineer who lives, enjoys, and codes in Novi Sad, Serbia. Trapped more than 20 years in the Data world. Like open source, public speaking and Brasilian Jiu Jistcu
What are you currently focused on—projects or themes you’d enjoy discussing?

Radovan Bacovic: I am mainly focused on exploring and implementing AI in a modern Data stack ecosystem. For instance, how to build an AI-based data products. It is an interesting angle of blend software and data engineering to mix it with AI and get the product people need.
Which topics are you most keen to cover?
Radovan Bacovic: As I mentioned, mainly exploring and implementing AI in the Data world and how to use the best engineering practices and industry standards for fast-pace development

Radovan Bacovic: Also want to focused (except that topic) on the Streamlit and how to connect data with visual applications. This can be beneficial as traditional roles (AE, DE, ML, DS) in the Data world are mixed and worth to put some attention on this part as well.

Radovan Bacovic: If it is ok, want to talk more about the open source tech stack in the Data and AI world and how folks can use it. Good example is my “talk to data” project where used OSS tooling to make nice AI based data product.
Are there any topics you’d rather skip or avoid discussing publicly?
Radovan Bacovic: No, always transparent and happy to tell my opinion

template_personal_story: Give a one-sentence snapshot of the moment that set this journey in motion.
Radovan Bacovic:
Where does this story begin for you?
Radovan Bacovic:
Who or what first pulled you toward the topic?

Radovan Bacovic: It was a problem nobody wanted to take and own. Was attracted by a messy, sprawling, high-stakes pipeline with no clear owner. That first time I took one apart, understood it, and rebuilt it so it actually ran reliably, that was the pull. Happy to share my experience on the early days of Data Engineering and my project in a large Dutch telekom company
Which events, successes, or stumbles taught you the most?

Radovan Bacovic: The tool choices, without a doubt. I've watched teams, including my own, fall in love with a new framework before asking what problem they were actually solving. Shiny tools have a gravitational pull in this field, and I've got several stories of pipelines that became monuments to technical ambition rather than business value. Those failures taught me more than any success did. Let's not forget to mention I am failing daily.
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?

Radovan Bacovic: Most data problems are people and process problems and not a technical issue. The broken pipeline usually has a broken handoff behind it. The missing data usually has a missing owner behind it. Happy to mention a boring solution example from GitLab
Who might recognize their own journey in yours?

Radovan Bacovic: Wide audience, as data is everywhere and there is something for everyone in it. Remember 2015. One expert said: data volume will grow 150x year on year, and the number of real data and AI experts will grow 5% per year. This is our chance, isn’t it?
Whose path resembles your early struggles?
Radovan Bacovic:
What 3-5 main stops, projects, or challenges mark the path—listed in order?
Radovan Bacovic:
AccountIQ - talk to your own data in AI fashion - put AI project at scale
An AI-based data product with processing Zendesk data - put AI in production on a smaller scale. Small effort with a huge impact
Streamlit Application framework - pioneering the Streamlit deployment
GitLab itself - 50m users, one of the biggest Postgres instances in the world, open source…
Was there a detour that almost changed your direction?

Radovan Bacovic: I was offered a move into management several times. What pulled me back was a pretty simple realisation: the problems I find genuinely interesting are technical, and the leverage I have comes from being deep in the work, not above it. And I see can contribute more as an IC - why lose a good engineer to get a mediocre manager?
What’s your current role, and what single experience ties you to this theme?
Radovan Bacovic: I'm a Staff/Principal Data Engineer. In brief, a high overview and strategic and architectural decisions one day and diving deeply into the code another day
What facts link present-you to this topic? Also, I am a regular international public speaker and find it super interesting to live several parallel lives

Radovan Bacovic: At the Staff level, my job is increasingly about helping other engineers avoid the mistakes I've already made, especially the tool-first, problem-second trap. The fact that I've lived through enough of these patterns across enough teams to recognise them early makes the role meaningful now.
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
Radovan Bacovic: Don't be scared to try and to fail
We’ll handle the editing and formatting from there. Thanks for contributing to the community!
Radovan Bacovic bio:Radovan Bacovic is a Staff Data Engineer who lives, enjoys, and codes in Novi Sad, Serbia.
Part of the Data giants ambassador program: Snowflake Squad member and dbt spotlight member.

An experienced data engineer and “wannabe” the best bad conference speaker. He is always eager to discover new data technologies in a rapidly changing environment. He has a profound background in application development at large international companies worldwide. He strongly advocates for the open-source community and the open-core approach.
Undoubtedly, he is a fervent data geek who is delighted to share his extensive experience with a broader audience.
He was trapped in the Data world for 20 years.
Passionate Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioner.
team_tab: Title: Engineering AI-Powered Data Products
Subtitle: Blending Software, Data, and AI with Radovan Bacovic

As artificial intelligence reshapes the modern data stack, the strict boundaries between Data Engineers, Analytics Engineers, and Data Scientists are rapidly blurring. To build truly impactful AI-driven data products, today’s professionals must blend traditional software engineering best practices with cutting-edge data and AI ecosystems.

Radovan Bacovic will share his insights from over 20 years in the data world regarding how to navigate this fast-paced landscape. We will break down how to implement AI responsibly using industry-standard engineering practices, the power of open-source tooling, and why connecting backend data to frontend visual applications using Streamlit is becoming a critical skill.
He’ll cover:
Two Decades in Data: Industry Evolution and Lessons from the BJJ Mat
The Blurring Boundaries of Traditional Data Roles
Implementing AI in the Modern Data Stack Using Engineering Best Practices
Building with Open Source and Streamlit
About the Speaker:
Radovan Bacovic is a Staff Data Engineer who lives, enjoys, and codes in Novi Sad, Serbia.
Part of the Data giants ambassador program: Snowflake Squad member and dbt spotlight member.

An experienced data engineer and “wannabe” the best bad conference speaker. He is always eager to discover new data technologies in a rapidly changing environment. He has a profound background in application development at large international companies worldwide. He strongly advocates for the open-source community and the open-core approach.
Undoubtedly, he is a fervent data geek who is delighted to share his extensive experience with a broader audience.
He was trapped in the Data world for 20 years.
Passionate Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioner.
speech: This week we’ll talk about Engineering AI-Powered Data Products.
We have a special guest today - Radovan Bacovic.
Radovan Bacovic is a Staff Data Engineer who lives, enjoys, and codes in Novi Sad, Serbia.
Part of the Data giants ambassador program: Snowflake Squad member and dbt spotlight member.

An experienced data engineer and “wannabe” the best bad conference speaker. He is always eager to discover new data technologies in a rapidly changing environment. He has a profound background in application development at large international companies worldwide. He strongly advocates for the open-source community and the open-core approach.
Undoubtedly, he is a fervent data geek who is delighted to share his extensive experience with a broader audience.
He was trapped in the Data world for 20 years.
Passionate Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioner.
Welcome, Radovan!
Before we go into our main topic of Engineering AI-Powered Data Products, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Background & Career Journey
You do Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu and you’ve been in data for 20 years. How has surviving messy data pipelines prepared you for the mats?
You once noted that data volume grows 150x year-over-year, but real experts only grow by 5%. Are we still drowning in that gap today?
You’ve actively avoided the management track to stay an Individual Contributor. Why do you think tech forces great engineers into management, and why did you fight back?
Team Dynamics & The Changing Landscape
You’ve said most data problems are actually people/process problems, not technical ones. Where are the biggest handoff friction points right now?
Data Engineers, Analytics Engineers, and Data Scientists are seeing their boundaries blur. Is this evolution a good thing, or total chaos?
The AI & Tooling Reality Check
With everyone rushing into GenAI, how do you spot a team building a "monument to technical ambition" rather than actual business value?
You built a smaller Zendesk AI tool and a massive "talk to your data" product (AccountIQ). What changes most when you take AI from a small experiment to production scale?
GitLab has 50 million users and a massive Postgres instance. How does working at that scale change how you approach AI architecture?
Why are you such a fierce advocate for open-source and open-core when it comes to the AI data stack?
Connecting Data to Frontends (Streamlit)
Why is a frontend framework like Streamlit becoming a critical tool for traditionally backend data engineers?
What’s the biggest mistake data folks make when trying to build their first data-driven visual application?
Lessons & Takeaways
Borrowing a page from GitLab's philosophy, what is one "boring" engineering practice that would make everyone's AI data products instantly better?
You proudly say you fail daily. What’s an educational failure you've had recently?
What’s a tiny first step a data professional can take right after this episode to start building AI products responsibly?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?

Shiny tools have a gravitational pull in this field, and I've got several stories of pipelines that became monuments to technical ambition rather than business value. Those failures taught me more than any success did. Let's not forget to mention I am failing daily.
Is there a theme that keeps recurring in your work or study?
Radovan Bacovic: Most data problems are people and process problems and not a technical issue. The broken pipeline usually has a broken handoff behind it. The missing data usually has a missing owner behind it. Happy to mention a boring solution example from GitLab
Who might recognize their own journey in yours?
Radovan Bacovic: Wide audience, as data is everywhere and there is something for everyone in it. Remember 2015. One expert said: data volume will grow 150x year on year, and the number of real data and AI experts will grow 5% per year. This is our chance, isn’t it?
Whose path resembles your early struggles?
Radovan Bacovic:
What 3-5 main stops, projects, or challenges mark the path—listed in order?
Radovan Bacovic:
AccountIQ - talk to your own data in AI fashion - put AI project at scale
An AI-based data product with processing Zendesk data - put AI in production on a smaller scale. Small effort with a huge impact
Streamlit Application framework - pioneering the Streamlit deployment
GitLab itself - 50m users, one of the biggest Postgres instances in the world, open source…
Was there a detour that almost changed your direction?
Radovan Bacovic: I was offered a move into management several times. What pulled me back was a pretty simple realisation: the problems I find genuinely interesting are technical, and the leverage I have comes from being deep in the work, not above it. And I see can contribute more as an IC - why lose a good engineer to get a mediocre manager?
What’s your current role, and what single experience ties you to this theme?
Radovan Bacovic: I'm a Staff/Principal Data Engineer. In brief, a high overview and strategic and architectural decisions one day and diving deeply into the code another day
What facts link present-you to this topic? Also, I am a regular international public speaker and find it super interesting to live several parallel lives
Radovan Bacovic: At the Staff level, my job is increasingly about helping other engineers avoid the mistakes I've already made, especially the tool-first, problem-second trap. The fact that I've lived through enough of these patterns across enough teams to recognise them early makes the role meaningful now.
If listeners remember one idea next week, what should it be?
Radovan Bacovic: Don't be scared to try and to fail
We’ll handle the editing and formatting from there. Thanks for contributing to the community!

Tab for Alexey and DTC Team
Title: Engineering AI-Powered Data Products
Subtitle: Blending Software, Data, and AI with Radovan Bacovic
As artificial intelligence reshapes the modern data stack, the strict boundaries between Data Engineers, Analytics Engineers, and Data Scientists are rapidly blurring. To build truly impactful AI-driven data products, today’s professionals must blend traditional software engineering best practices with cutting-edge data and AI ecosystems.
Radovan Bacovic will share his insights from over 20 years in the data world regarding how to navigate this fast-paced landscape. We will break down how to implement AI responsibly using industry-standard engineering practices, the power of open-source tooling, and why connecting backend data to frontend visual applications using Streamlit is becoming a critical skill.
He’ll cover:
Two Decades in Data: Industry Evolution and Lessons from the BJJ Mat
The Blurring Boundaries of Traditional Data Roles
Implementing AI in the Modern Data Stack Using Engineering Best Practices
Building with Open Source and Streamlit
About the Speaker:
Radovan Bacovic is a Staff Data Engineer who lives, enjoys, and codes in Novi Sad, Serbia.
Part of the Data giants ambassador program: Snowflake Squad member and dbt spotlight member.
An experienced data engineer and “wannabe” the best bad conference speaker. He is always eager to discover new data technologies in a rapidly changing environment. He has a profound background in application development at large international companies worldwide. He strongly advocates for the open-source community and the open-core approach.
Undoubtedly, he is a fervent data geek who is delighted to share his extensive experience with a broader audience.
He was trapped in the Data world for 20 years.
Passionate Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioner.
Links:
LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/radovanbacovic/
Twitter: https://x.com/SvenMurtinson
Github: https://gitlab.com/radovan.bacovic/
Website: www.bacovic.com
Speech
This week we’ll talk about Engineering AI-Powered Data Products.
We have a special guest today - Radovan Bacovic.
Radovan Bacovic is a Staff Data Engineer who lives, enjoys, and codes in Novi Sad, Serbia.
Part of the Data giants ambassador program: Snowflake Squad member and dbt spotlight member.
An experienced data engineer and “wannabe” the best bad conference speaker. He is always eager to discover new data technologies in a rapidly changing environment. He has a profound background in application development at large international companies worldwide. He strongly advocates for the open-source community and the open-core approach.
Undoubtedly, he is a fervent data geek who is delighted to share his extensive experience with a broader audience.
He was trapped in the Data world for 20 years.
Passionate Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioner.
Welcome, Radovan!
Before we go into our main topic of Engineering AI-Powered Data Products, let’s start with your background. Can you tell us about your career journey so far? (not too long please =))
Background & Career Journey
You do Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu and you’ve been in data for 20 years. How has surviving messy data pipelines prepared you for the mats?
You once noted that data volume grows 150x year-over-year, but real experts only grow by 5%. Are we still drowning in that gap today?
You’ve actively avoided the management track to stay an Individual Contributor. Why do you think tech forces great engineers into management, and why did you fight back?
Team Dynamics & The Changing Landscape
You’ve said most data problems are actually people/process problems, not technical ones. Where are the biggest handoff friction points right now?
Data Engineers, Analytics Engineers, and Data Scientists are seeing their boundaries blur. Is this evolution a good thing, or total chaos?
The AI & Tooling Reality Check
With everyone rushing into GenAI, how do you spot a team building a "monument to technical ambition" rather than actual business value?
You built a smaller Zendesk AI tool and a massive "talk to your data" product (AccountIQ). What changes most when you take AI from a small experiment to production scale?
GitLab has 50 million users and a massive Postgres instance. How does working at that scale change how you approach AI architecture?
Why are you such a fierce advocate for open-source and open-core when it comes to the AI data stack?
Connecting Data to Frontends (Streamlit)
Why is a frontend framework like Streamlit becoming a critical tool for traditionally backend data engineers?
What’s the biggest mistake data folks make when trying to build their first data-driven visual application?
Lessons & Takeaways
Borrowing a page from GitLab's philosophy, what is one "boring" engineering practice that would make everyone's AI data products instantly better?
You proudly say you fail daily. What’s an educational failure you've had recently?
What’s a tiny first step a data professional can take right after this episode to start building AI products responsibly?
Are there any books or other resources that you can recommend to the listeners?
